> 3. Draw a **chart** showing your vitals trending over time (this is **data visualization**).
> 4. *Only then* do they diagnose, predict, and prescribe.

**Descriptive analytics is the "check-up" of business data** — before you can forecast, optimize, or prescribe, you must first *describe* what already exists in the data. Similarly, **data visualization is the "X-ray"** — a visual diagnostic that reveals patterns hidden in raw numbers.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic is "High-Yield" in KTU Exams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test:
> - The **distinction** between the four types of analytics.
> - **Computation** of mean, median, mode, variance, standard deviation, skewness, kurtosis.
> - **Selection** of the correct chart type for a given data scenario.
> - **Interpretation** of a given chart and deriving business meaning from it.
> Marks are often awarded for **terminology precision** and **formula correctness** — not just final answers.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Descriptive Analytics

Descriptive analytics rests on two conceptual pillars:

### Pillar 1: Numerical (Quantitative) Summaries
These reduce the entire dataset into a few representative numbers:

**(a) Measures of Central Tendency** — identify the "center" of the data.
- **Mean (Arithmetic Average)** — sensitive to outliers; best for symmetric, continuous data.
- **Median** — the middle value; robust to outliers; best for skewed data or ordinal scales.
- **Mode** — the most frequent value; best for categorical/nominal data.

**(b) Measures of Dispersion (Variability)** — quantify how "spread out" the data is.
- **Range** — simplest, but uses only two values.
- **Variance** — average of squared deviations from the mean.
- **Standard Deviation** — square root of variance; expressed in original units.
- **Interquartile Range (IQR)** — robust spread measure (Q3 − Q1).
- **Coefficient of Variation (CV)** — relative dispersion (useful for comparing two datasets with different units/means).

**(c) Measures of Distribution Shape**
- **Skewness** — measures asymmetry of the distribution.
- **Kurtosis** — measures the "tailedness" (peakedness) of the distribution.

### Pillar 2: Visual (Graphical) Summaries
These preserve the *structure* of the data (which numerical summaries lose):
- **Bar / Column Chart** — compare categories.
- **Histogram** — show distribution of a continuous variable.
- **Line Chart** — show trend over time.
- **Pie / Donut Chart** — show part-to-whole (max 5–7 slices).
- **Scatter Plot** — show relationship between two numeric variables.
- **Box-and-Whisker Plot** — show five-number summary + outliers.
- **Heatmap** — show magnitude across two categorical dimensions.
- **Treemap** — hierarchical part-to-whole.
- **Pareto Chart** — bars sorted descending + cumulative % line (80/20 rule).

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Analytical Step | Why It Matters | Real-world Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Compute Mean | Provides a single "representative" value for the dataset. | Average monthly revenue, average customer age. |
| Compute Median | Eliminates the distortion of extreme values. | Median household income (used by governments). |
| Compute Mode | Identifies the most common category. | Most-sold product size, most-frequent complaint type. |
| Compute Std. Dev. | Quantifies risk/volatility around the mean. | Portfolio risk analysis, quality control limits. |
| Compute Skewness | Reveals whether the data leans left/right. | Income distributions are right-skewed → mean > median. |
| Choose Chart Type | Different charts answer different questions. | Trends → line; comparison → bar; composition → pie. |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for Module 1. Memorize the notation and units carefully.

| # | Concept | Formula | Notation Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Arithmetic Mean (Population) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}{n}$ | $x_i$ = each observation; $n$ = sample size |
| 2 | Arithmetic Mean (Grouped) | $\bar{x} = \dfrac{\sum f_i \cdot m_i}{\sum f_i}$ | $f_i$ = frequency; $m_i$ = class midpoint |
| 3 | Median (Odd $n$) | $\text{Median} = x_{\left(\frac{n+1}{2}\right)}$ | Position formula |
| 4 | Median (Even $n$) | $\text{Median} = \dfrac{x_{(n/2)} + x_{(n/2+1)}}{2}$ | Average of two central values |
| 5 | Mode | Highest frequency value | For grouped: $L + h \cdot \dfrac{d_1}{d_1 + d_2}$ |
| 6 | Range | $R = x_{\max} - x_{\min}$ | Simplest dispersion |
| 7 | Variance (Population) | $\sigma^{2} = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}{n}$ | Uses divisor $n$ |
| 8 | Variance (Sample) | $s^{2} = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}{n - 1}$ | Uses divisor $n-1$ (Bessel's correction) |
| 9 | Standard Deviation | $\sigma = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}}$ or $s = \sqrt{s^{2}}$ | Same units as data |
| 10 | Interquartile Range | $IQR = Q_3 - Q_1$ | 25th and 75th percentiles |
| 11 | Coefficient of Variation | $CV = \dfrac{\sigma}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$ | Unitless percentage |
| 12 | Skewness (Pearson's) | $Sk = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{3} / n}{\sigma^{3}}$ | $Sk = 0$ symmetric; $>0$ right; $<0$ left |
| 13 | Kurtosis | $Ku = \dfrac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{4} / n}{\sigma^{4}}$ | $Ku = 3$ mesokurtic (normal) |
| 14 | Outlier Bound (1.5·IQR rule) | $[Q_1 - 1.5 \cdot IQR,\; Q_3 + 1.5 \cdot IQR]$ | Beyond = outlier |
| 15 | Z-Score | $z_i = \dfrac{x_i - \bar{x}}{\sigma}$ | Standardized score |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol Safety:** All absolute-value expressions and conditional formulas above have been written using LaTeX `\vert`-equivalent notations to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. In your answer scripts, always use $\vert x \vert$ instead of the bare pipe.

---

## 2.4 Real-world Engineering / Business Utility

- **Manufacturing (IAT 6-sigma context):** Standard deviation defines the upper/lower control limits (UCL/LCL = $\bar{x} \pm 3\sigma$).
- **Finance:** CV is used to compare the *relative* risk of two assets with different price scales.
- **Retail / Supply Chain:** Median lead time and IQR help set safety-stock buffers.
- **Healthcare Analytics:** Skewness of patient waiting times signals operational bottlenecks.
- **HR Analytics:** Mode of employee exit reasons guides retention strategy.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following worked example is structured exactly as a KTU 14-mark answer expects: *Given → Formula → Substitution → Computation → Interpretation*. Practice reproducing this format under timed conditions.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example — Monthly Sales of a Retail Outlet

**Given Data:** The monthly sales (in ₹ '000) of a product across 12 months are:

$$12,\ 15,\ 14,\ 10,\ 18,\ 20,\ 16,\ 14,\ 13,\ 17,\ 19,\ 15$$

Compute: **(a)** Mean, Median, Mode; **(b)** Variance, Standard Deviation, Range, IQR, CV; **(c)** Comment on skewness.

---

### 3.1(a) Measures of Central Tendency

**Step 1 — Compute the Mean:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{12} x_i = 12+15+14+10+18+20+16+14+13+17+19+15$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{12} x_i = 183$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{183}{12} = 15.25 \text{ (₹ '000)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Median (sort the data first):**

$$10,\ 12,\ 13,\ 14,\ 14,\ 15,\ \mathbf{15},\ 16,\ 17,\ 18,\ 19,\ 20$$

Since $n = 12$ (even), the median is the mean of the 6th and 7th sorted values:

$$\text{Median} = \frac{15 + 15}{2} = 15.0 \text{ (₹ '000)}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Mode:**

The value **14** appears twice and **15** appears twice — the dataset is **bimodal**:

$$\text{Mode} = \{14,\ 15\} \text{ (₹ '000)}$$

---

### 3.1(b) Measures of Dispersion

**Step 4 — Build the deviation table:**

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 10 | −5.25 | 27.5625 |
| 12 | −3.25 | 10.5625 |
| 13 | −2.25 | 5.0625 |
| 14 | −1.25 | 1.5625 |
| 14 | −1.25 | 1.5625 |
| 15 | −0.25 | 0.0625 |
| 15 | −0.25 | 0.0625 |
| 16 | 0.75 | 0.5625 |
| 17 | 1.75 | 3.0625 |
| 18 | 2.75 | 7.5625 |
| 19 | 3.75 | 14.0625 |
| 20 | 4.75 | 22.5625 |
| **Sum** | **0.00** | **94.2500** |

**Step 5 — Compute Sample Variance (KTU default for analytics):**

$$s^{2} = \frac{\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2}}{n - 1} = \frac{94.25}{12 - 1} = \frac{94.25}{11} \approx 8.5682$$

**Step 6 — Compute Sample Standard Deviation:**

$$s = \sqrt{8.5682} \approx 2.9273 \text{ (₹ '000)}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Range:**

$$R = x_{\max} - x_{\min} = 20 - 10 = 10 \text{ (₹ '000)}$$

**Step 8 — Compute Quartiles and IQR:**

- Lower half (first 6): $10, 12, 13, 14, 14, 15$ → $Q_1 = (13+14)/2 = 13.5$
- Upper half (last 6): $15, 16, 17, 18, 19, 20$ → $Q_3 = (17+18)/2 = 17.5$

$$IQR = Q_3 - Q_1 = 17.5 - 13.5 = 4.0 \text{ (₹ '000)}$$

**Step 9 — Compute Coefficient of Variation:**

$$CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.9273}{15.25} \times 100\% \approx 19.20\%$$

---

### 3.1(c) Interpretation (Skewness & Business Meaning)

**Step 10 — Compare Mean vs Median:**

$$\bar{x} = 15.25 \quad ; \quad \text{Median} = 15.00$$

Since $\bar{x} > \text{Median}$, the distribution is **slightly right-skewed (positively skewed)**. The handful of high sales months (18, 19, 20) pulls the mean above the median.

**Step 11 — Business Interpretation:**

> The retail outlet's typical monthly sales are ₹ 15,000, with a moderate spread (CV ≈ 19%). Sales fluctuate by roughly ±₹ 2,927 around the mean. The 4-unit IQR indicates the "middle 50%" of months lie between ₹ 13,500 and ₹ 17,500. The right-skewness suggests the business occasionally benefits from "boom months" — the manager should investigate the drivers of these high-sales months and try to replicate them.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Visualization

> [!TIP]
> KTU loves questions framed as: *"Given the following scenario, which chart would you use and why?"* The matrix below is your decision-making tool.

| Business Question | Recommended Chart | Why This Chart? |
|---|---|---|
| Compare sales across 5 product categories | **Bar (Column) Chart** | Categorical x-axis, easy magnitude comparison |
| Show monthly revenue trend over 2 years | **Line Chart** | Time-series on x-axis, continuous trend visibility |
| Show part-to-whole of a budget | **Pie / Donut Chart** | Composition of a single whole (≤7 categories) |
| Show distribution of customer ages | **Histogram** | Continuous variable, equal-width bins |
| Show relationship between ad spend & sales | **Scatter Plot** | Two continuous variables, correlation detection |
| Detect outliers in delivery times | **Box Plot** | Five-number summary + outlier flagging |
| Show sales density across regions × quarters | **Heatmap** | Two categorical axes + color-coded magnitude |
| Identify the "vital few" causes of a problem | **Pareto Chart** | Sorted bars + cumulative % line (80/20 rule) |
| Compare actual vs target across KPIs | **Bullet Chart** | Compact performance vs benchmark |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU mistake:** Students frequently use pie charts for *trends* or *comparisons across many categories*. Both are wrong. Pie charts are **composition-only** and **max 5–7 slices**.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

    C1[Mean Median Mode] --> C
    C2[Variance Std Dev IQR] --> C

    Q3 -. Upper Whisker .-> MAX

    style MIN fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Similar Pattern)] (3 Marks)**
**"Differentiate between Descriptive Analytics and Predictive Analytics."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Descriptive Analytics | Predictive Analytics |
|---|---|---|
| Core Question | *What happened?* | *What will happen?* |
| Data Used | Historical (past) data | Historical + real-time data |
| Techniques | Mean, median, std. dev., charts | Regression, classification, time-series forecasting |
| Output Type | Summary statistics, dashboards | Probability scores, forecasted values |
| Uncertainty | Not quantified | Explicitly quantified (confidence intervals) |
| Example | "Average sales last quarter = ₹ 5L" | "Forecasted sales next quarter = ₹ 5.4L ± ₹ 0.3L" |

Descriptive analytics *summarizes* the past; predictive analytics *extrapolates* from the past into the future using statistical/ML models.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Similar Pattern)] (3 Marks)**
**"What is a Histogram? How is it different from a Bar Chart?"**

**Model Answer:**

A **histogram** is a graphical representation of the distribution of a *continuous* numerical variable, where data is grouped into equal-width intervals (bins) and the height of each bar represents the frequency (or relative frequency) of observations falling in that bin.

**Key Differences:**

| Aspect | Bar Chart | Histogram |
|---|---|---|
| Variable type | Categorical | Continuous (numerical) |
| Bar spacing | Gaps between bars (discrete) | Bars touch (continuous) |
| X-axis | Distinct categories | Numerical intervals (bins) |
| Bar order | Can be reordered meaningfully | Order is fixed (ascending bins) |
| Example | Sales by product type | Sales distribution by price range |

Histograms are used to inspect the **shape, center, and spread** of a continuous distribution (e.g., normality, skewness, modality).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): Full Descriptive Analytics Computation + Visualization Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Module 1, Choice A) Pattern]**

The daily customer footfall at a supermarket for 15 randomly selected days is given below:

$$45,\ 52,\ 48,\ 61,\ 55,\ 58,\ 42,\ 49,\ 56,\ 60,\ 47,\ 53,\ 51,\ 57,\ 62$$

**(a)** Compute the **mean, median, mode, range, variance, and standard deviation** of the footfall. Identify and justify the use of **sample** vs **population** formulas. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct a **5-number summary** and draw a **box plot**. Recommend **two suitable visualizations** for presenting this data to the store manager and justify your choice. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a) [7 Marks]**

**Step 1 — Sort the data:**

$$42,\ 45,\ 47,\ 48,\ 49,\ 51,\ 52,\ 53,\ 55,\ 56,\ 57,\ 58,\ 60,\ 61,\ 62$$

**Step 2 — Mean (Sample arithmetic average):**

$$\sum x_i = 42+45+47+48+49+51+52+53+55+56+57+58+60+61+62 = 796$$

$$\bar{x} = \frac{796}{15} \approx 53.07$$

**Step 3 — Median (n = 15, odd):**

$$\text{Median} = x_{\left(\frac{15+1}{2}\right)} = x_{(8)} = 53$$

**Step 4 — Mode:**

All values appear exactly once — **no mode** (uniform-like central region). We say the dataset is **amodal**.

**Step 5 — Range:**

$$R = 62 - 42 = 20$$

**Step 6 — Sample Variance:**

$$\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} = ?$$

Using $\bar{x} = 53.07$ (rounded), the squared deviations yield $\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^{2} \approx 531.87$ (you may show the deviation table similar to Section 3.1).

$$s^{2} = \frac{531.87}{15 - 1} = \frac{531.87}{14} \approx 37.99$$

**Step 7 — Sample Standard Deviation:**

$$s = \sqrt{37.99} \approx 6.16$$

**Justification (Sample vs Population):** Since the 15 days are a *sample* drawn from the population of "all days the supermarket is open", we use the **sample formulas with divisor $n-1$** (Bessel's correction) to obtain an unbiased estimator of the population variance.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b) [7 Marks]**

**Step 8 — Five-Number Summary:**

- $\text{Minimum} = 42$
- $Q_1$ = median of lower 7 values $\{42, 45, 47, 48, 49, 51, 52\}$ → $Q_1 = 48$
- $\text{Median} = 53$
- $Q_3$ = median of upper 7 values $\{55, 56, 57, 58, 60, 61, 62\}$ → $Q_3 = 58$
- $\text{Maximum} = 62$

**Step 9 — Box Plot description:**

A box plot would have a box from 48 to 58, with a median line at 53, whiskers extending to 42 (lower) and 62 (upper), and $IQR = 58 - 48 = 10$. The mild rightward extension of the upper whisker suggests slight right skew.

**Step 10 — Recommended Visualizations:**

1. **Histogram** — to show the *distribution* of footfall (symmetric, roughly normal) for staffing decisions.
2. **Box Plot** — to summarize the central tendency, spread, and identify potential outlier days requiring investigation.

**Justification:** A histogram reveals the *shape* of the distribution (helpful for shift-planning assuming bell-curve demand), while a box plot compresses the data into the five-number summary, making the spread and central tendency instantly visible to the store manager.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): Visualization Selection & Interpretation (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Module 1, Choice B) Pattern]**

**(a)** For each of the following business scenarios, identify the **most appropriate chart** and justify your choice in 2–3 lines. **(4 × 1.5 = 6 Marks)**
  1. Comparing market share of 4 telecom companies in Kerala.
  2. Showing the trend of a company's stock price over 5 years.
  3. Displaying the distribution of 1000 students' exam scores.
  4. Investigating whether advertising spend is correlated with sales revenue.

**(b)** A company's monthly expenses (in ₹ '000) for the last 6 months are: 50, 60, 55, 70, 65, 80. Compute the **mean, median, variance, and standard deviation**. Also compute the **coefficient of variation (CV)** and interpret whether the expenses are *relatively stable* or *highly variable*. **(8 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a) [6 Marks]**

1. **Pie Chart (or Donut Chart)** — Best for showing part-to-whole composition across 4 categories (≤7 slices). It instantly communicates each company's share of the total market.

2. **Line Chart** — Time-series data on the x-axis with a continuous variable on the y-axis. The line emphasizes trend direction, peaks, troughs, and turning points over the 5-year horizon.

3. **Histogram** — With 1000 continuous exam scores, a histogram reveals the *shape* of the distribution (normal, skewed, bimodal), which is essential for grading analytics and identifying the modal performance band.

4. **Scatter Plot** — Both advertising spend and sales revenue are continuous variables. A scatter plot displays individual data points and the *direction/strength* of correlation (positive, negative, none, linear, non-linear).

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b) [8 Marks]**

**Given:** $x = \{50, 60, 55, 70, 65, 80\}$, $n = 6$.

**Step 1 — Mean:**

$$\bar{x} = \frac{50+60+55+70+65+80}{6} = \frac{380}{6} \approx 63.33$$

**Step 2 — Median (n = 6, even):**

Sorted: $50, 55, 60, 65, 70, 80$

$$\text{Median} = \frac{60 + 65}{2} = 62.5$$

**Step 3 — Sample Variance:**

| $x_i$ | $x_i - \bar{x}$ | $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ |
|---:|---:|---:|
| 50 | −13.33 | 177.69 |
| 60 | −3.33 | 11.09 |
| 55 | −8.33 | 69.39 |
| 70 | 6.67 | 44.49 |
| 65 | 1.67 | 2.79 |
| 80 | 16.67 | 277.89 |
| **Sum** | **0.02** | **583.34** |

$$s^{2} = \frac{583.34}{6 - 1} = \frac{583.34}{5} = 116.67$$

**Step 4 — Sample Standard Deviation:**

$$s = \sqrt{116.67} \approx 10.80$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of Variation:**

$$CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\% = \frac{10.80}{63.33} \times 100\% \approx 17.06\%$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

A CV of **~17%** indicates **moderate relative variability**. In business terms, monthly expenses deviate by about 17% of the mean — neither highly stable (CV < 10%) nor highly volatile (CV > 30%). The mean (63.33) is slightly higher than the median (62.5), suggesting mild right-skewness driven by the ₹ 80k high-expense month. Management should investigate the drivers of that outlier month to ensure it is not a recurring cost spike.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Most Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing sample vs population formulas:** Using divisor $n$ instead of $n-1$ (or vice versa) without justifying the choice. Always *state which dataset you have* (sample/population) before computing variance.
> 2. **Skipping intermediate steps in deviation tables:** Examiners award step-marks for the *deviation table* itself. Always show $(x_i - \bar{x})$ and $(x_i - \bar{x})^{2}$ columns explicitly.
> 3. **Misapplying pie charts to trend or comparison questions:** Pie charts are *composition-only* with max 5–7 slices. Using them for time-series is a 2-mark deduction in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Forgetting to *interpret* the numbers:** A correct numerical answer with zero business interpretation loses 1–2 marks. Always close with: *"This means…"*
> 5. **Confusing histogram and bar chart:** KTU examiners specifically check whether you understand *bar spacing* (gaps vs touching) and *variable type* (categorical vs continuous).
> 6. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (e.g., writing "15" instead of "₹ 15,000" or "15 units").
> 7. **Skewness sign error:** Right-skewed means mean > median (tail on right). Many students write it backwards.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision checklist for Module 1 — Descriptive Analytics & Data Visualization:**

- [ ] **Four levels of analytics:** Descriptive → Diagnostic → Predictive → Prescriptive (memorize the *What/Why/Will/Should* framework).
- [ ] **Mean formula:** $\bar{x} = \sum x_i / n$ — sensitive to outliers; best for symmetric data.
- [ ] **Median:** Middle value; robust to outliers; best for skewed or ordinal data.
- [ ] **Mode:** Most frequent value; can be 0, 1, or many (bimodal, multimodal).
- [ ] **Variance — Sample** uses divisor $(n-1)$; **Population** uses divisor $n$. Always *state which one*.
- [ ] **Standard Deviation** = $\sqrt{\text{Variance}}$ — same units as original data.
- [ ] **Range** = Max − Min (simplest, but uses only 2 values).
- [ ] **IQR** = $Q_3 - Q_1$ — robust measure of spread; used in outlier detection (1.5·IQR rule).
- [ ] **CV** = $(\sigma / \bar{x}) \times 100\%$ — unitless; used to compare variability across datasets with different units/means.
- [ ] **Skewness:** $Sk = 0$ symmetric; $Sk > 0$ right-skewed (mean > median); $Sk < 0$ left-skewed (mean < median).
- [ ] **Kurtosis:** $Ku = 3$ is normal (mesokurtic); $>3$ heavy-tailed; $<3$ light-tailed.
- [ ] **Bar Chart** → categorical comparison; bars have *gaps*; order can be changed.
- [ ] **Histogram** → continuous distribution; bars *touch*; order is fixed by bin intervals.
- [ ] **Line Chart** → time-series / trend; x-axis must be time-ordered.
- [ ] **Pie Chart** → part-to-whole *composition only*; max 5–7 slices.
- [ ] **Scatter Plot** → relationship between *two* continuous variables; detects correlation.
- [ ] **Box Plot** → five-number summary; detects outliers; best for comparing distributions.
- [ ] **Pareto Chart** → sorted bar + cumulative % line; 80/20 principle.
- [ ] **Heatmap** → two categorical dimensions + color-encoded magnitude.
- [ ] **Z-score** standardizes a value: $z = (x - \bar{x}) / \sigma$ — useful for outlier detection ($|z| > 3$ is a strong outlier).
- [ ] Always **interpret** the final numerical answer in business terms — never end with just a number.
- [ ] Always **show the deviation table** when computing variance — it earns step-marks.
- [ ] Always **state units** in the final answer.
- [ ] Always **justify** the choice of chart with reference to the *type of question* being answered.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Statistical Methods for Summarizing Data: Frequency Distribution for Categorical and Numerical Data
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT632 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Statistical Methods for Summarizing Data: Frequency Distribution for Categorical and Numerical Data

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Frequency Distribution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Frequency Distribution (KTU 2024 Definition):** A *frequency distribution* is a systematic tabular arrangement of statistical data, organized into mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive classes (or categories) along with their corresponding frequencies (counts) or relative frequencies (proportions). It transforms raw, unorganized observations into a structured summary that reveals the underlying pattern, central tendency, spread, and shape of the data.

In the KTU 2024 Business Analytics framework, a frequency distribution is the **first and most fundamental step of descriptive statistics**. Before any inferential analysis, regression, or visualization can be performed, raw data must be organized into a frequency distribution to enable decision-makers to "see" the story hidden inside the numbers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Library Classification System"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Organizing a Library:**
> Imagine you walk into a massive library containing **10,000 books dumped into one giant pile on the floor** (this is your **raw data**). You cannot find a single book. Now, suppose a librarian sorts the books into labeled shelves: *Fiction, Science, History, Biography, Technology* (these are your **classes/categories**) and pastes a sticker on each shelf saying *"1,240 books"* (this is your **frequency**). Suddenly, the library is usable! That sorted list of shelves with counts is exactly what a **frequency distribution** does to raw business data — it converts chaos into clarity.

In a business context:
- **Raw Data:** 5,000 daily transaction records of a supermarket.
- **Frequency Distribution:** A table showing how many transactions fall into categories like *Grocery (1,800), Electronics (450), Apparel (900), Food Court (1,100), Others (750)*.
- **Benefit:** Management can immediately identify which department drives revenue.

## 1.3 Two Grand Categories of Data in Frequency Distributions

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly distinguishes frequency distribution construction for **two fundamentally different data types**. Misclassifying the data type is the **#1 cause of incorrect frequency tables** in board examinations.

| **Data Type** | **Also Called** | **Nature** | **Examples in Business Analytics** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Categorical Data** | Qualitative / Attribute Data | Labels, names, groups | Gender, Product Category, City, Payment Mode |
| **Numerical Data** | Quantitative / Variable Data | Measurable counts/amounts | Sales Revenue, Age, Marks, Stock Price |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Data has two sub-types that KTU students MUST know:**
> 1. **Discrete Numerical Data** — countable, finite values (e.g., number of customers per day: 10, 11, 12...).
> 2. **Continuous Numerical Data** — measurable, takes any value in a range (e.g., temperature, height, weight, transaction time).

## 1.4 Core Components of a Frequency Distribution Table (Syllabus Highlight)

For numerical data, every frequency distribution must contain the following elements:

1. **Class Limits** — the two endpoints defining a class (e.g., 10–20).
2. **Class Boundaries** — the *true* numeric boundaries that eliminate gaps between classes.
3. **Class Width (Size)** — the numerical span of a single class.
4. **Class Mark (Midpoint)** — the central value of a class.
5. **Frequency (f)** — the number of observations falling in a class.
6. **Relative Frequency (f/n)** — the proportion (decimal or percentage) of observations in a class.
7. **Cumulative Frequency (cf)** — running total of frequencies up to a class.

These components will be derived rigorously in **SECTION 3** with numerical examples.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Frequency Distribution for CATEGORICAL Data

Categorical data consists of *labels or names* that cannot be ordered numerically (or where order is not meaningful). The construction is the **simplest** form in statistics.

### Step-by-Step Logic for Categorical Frequency Tables

1. **Identify the categorical variable** (e.g., Mode of Payment).
2. **List all distinct categories** that appear in the raw data (e.g., *Cash, Credit Card, Debit Card, UPI, Net Banking*).
3. **Tally (count) the number of observations** falling under each category using a tally mark system: `||||` represents 4, and the 5th strike becomes `||||/` (a gate).
4. **Record the frequency (f)** for each category.
5. **Compute relative frequency** by dividing each frequency by the total number of observations.
6. **Optionally compute cumulative frequency** if the categories are *ordinal* (have a natural order, e.g., *Poor, Average, Good, Excellent*).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** For nominal categorical data (no natural order), do NOT compute cumulative frequency — it is meaningless and will be marked wrong. Cumulative frequency is **only valid for ordinal categorical data or numerical data**.

### Why Categorical Frequency Distribution Matters in Business Analytics

- **Market Segmentation:** Understanding customer distribution across age groups, regions, or income brackets.
- **Quality Control:** Tracking defect types in manufacturing.
- **Customer Analytics:** Analyzing payment preferences to optimize checkout infrastructure.

## 2.2 Frequency Distribution for NUMERICAL Data

Numerical data requires **class intervals (bins)** rather than simple category labels. The construction process is more involved.

### Step-by-Step Logic for Numerical Frequency Tables

1. **Sort the raw data** in ascending order to identify the minimum and maximum.
2. **Compute the Range:**

$$R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$$

3. **Decide the number of classes (k):** Use one of the standard rules below.
4. **Compute the class width (w):**
$$w = \frac{R}{k}$$
5. **Set the class limits** starting from a value $\leq X_{\min}$ using the computed class width.
6. **Tally the observations** into the appropriate class intervals.
7. **Compute class boundaries, midpoints, relative frequencies, and cumulative frequencies.**

### Standard Rules for Determining Number of Classes (k)

| **Rule Name** | **Formula** | **Best Used When** |
|---|---|---|
| Sturges' Rule | $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$ | Data is approximately normal; small-to-medium samples |
| Rice Rule | $k = 2 \cdot n^{1/3}$ | Quick estimation; large samples |
| Square Root Rule | $k = \sqrt{n}$ | Simple rule of thumb |
| Range Rule | $k = \dfrac{R}{\text{iqr}}$ | When IQR is known |

where $n$ is the total number of observations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** Sturges' Rule is the most frequently tested method. Students must remember $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$. The constant **3.322** equals $\log_2(10) = 1/\log_{10}(2)$.

## 2.3 Critical Distinctions: Class Limits vs. Class Boundaries

This is the **most heavily tested concept** in KTU board exams for this topic.

- **Class Limits** are the values *as reported in the data* (e.g., 10, 20, 30...).
  - *Lower Class Limit (LCL)* = smallest value in the class.
  - *Upper Class Limit (UCL)* = largest value in the class.
- **Class Boundaries** are the *true* numeric boundaries that ensure **no gap and no overlap** between consecutive classes.
  - *Lower Class Boundary (LCB)* = LCL $-$ 0.5 (for discrete data) or LCL $-$ half the precision unit.
  - *Upper Class Boundary (UCB)* = UCL $+$ 0.5 (for discrete data) or UCL $+$ half the precision unit.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse *class limits* with *class boundaries*. A question asking "find the class boundaries of the class 10–20" expects the answer **9.5–20.5**, NOT 10–20.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

| **Concept** | **Formula / Rule** | **Notes / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Range | $R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | Always in the same unit as the data |
| Class Width | $w = \dfrac{R}{k}$ | Usually rounded *up* to a convenient value |
| Sturges' Number of Classes | $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$ | Most commonly tested |
| Rice Number of Classes | $k = 2 \cdot n^{1/3}$ | For large datasets |
| Class Mark / Midpoint | $m = \dfrac{\text{LCL} + \text{UCL}}{2}$ | Also equals $\dfrac{\text{LCB} + \text{UCB}}{2}$ |
| Lower Class Boundary | $\text{LCB} = \text{LCL} - 0.5$ | For data with precision of 1 (integers) |
| Upper Class Boundary | $\text{UCB} = \text{UCL} + 0.5$ | For data with precision of 1 (integers) |
| Relative Frequency | $f_{\text{rel}} = \dfrac{f}{n}$ | Sum of all $f_{\text{rel}}$ equals **1.000** |
| Percentage Frequency | $f_{\%} = \dfrac{f}{n} \times 100$ | Sum of all $f_{\%}$ equals **100%** |
| Cumulative Frequency | $\text{cf}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j$ | Non-decreasing sequence |
| Cumulative Relative Frequency | $\text{crf}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_{\text{rel},j}$ | Last value equals **1.000** |
| Frequency Density | $f_d = \dfrac{f}{w}$ | Used for unequal class widths; for histograms with varying widths |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** In **Operations Analytics**, frequency distributions of product defect sizes (continuous numerical) are used to compute **process capability indices (Cp, Cpk)**. In **Financial Analytics**, frequency distributions of daily stock returns are used to estimate **Value-at-Risk (VaR)**. The class width formula is the foundation of **histogram-based estimation** in these applications.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked-Out Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example Setup:** Consider the marks (out of 50) obtained by **15 students** in a Business Analytics internal test:
>
> $12,\ 25,\ 30,\ 8,\ 45,\ 18,\ 35,\ 22,\ 40,\ 28,\ 15,\ 32,\ 38,\ 20,\ 10$

We will construct the complete frequency distribution with all derived columns.

## 3.1 Step 1: Sort the Data and Compute the Range

First, arrange the data in ascending order:

$$8,\ 10,\ 12,\ 15,\ 18,\ 20,\ 22,\ 25,\ 28,\ 30,\ 32,\ 35,\ 38,\ 40,\ 45$$

From this sorted data, we extract:
- Minimum value: $X_{\min} = 8$
- Maximum value: $X_{\max} = 45$
- Total number of observations: $n = 15$

Now compute the range:

$$R = X_{\max} - X_{\min} = 45 - 8 = 37$$

**Interpretation:** The spread of the data is 37 marks, meaning the class intervals must collectively cover the span from 8 up to 45.

## 3.2 Step 2: Determine the Number of Classes (k) Using Sturges' Rule

Apply Sturges' formula:

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \cdot \log_{10}(n) = 1 + 3.322 \cdot \log_{10}(15)$$

Compute $\log_{10}(15)$:

$$\log_{10}(15) = \log_{10}(1.5 \times 10) = \log_{10}(1.5) + \log_{10}(10) \approx 0.1761 + 1.0000 = 1.1761$$

Substitute back:

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \cdot 1.1761 = 1 + 3.9072 = 4.9072$$

Since the number of classes must be a whole number, we round *up*:

$$k = 5 \text{ classes}$$

## 3.3 Step 3: Compute the Class Width (w)

Using the unrounded theoretical width:

$$w_{\text{theoretical}} = \frac{R}{k} = \frac{37}{5} = 7.4$$

For practical class construction, we **round up** to a convenient integer. Since the data is in whole-number marks, a width of **8** is the cleanest choice:

$$w = 8$$

**Reason for rounding up:** A width of 7 would yield a range of $7 \times 5 = 35$, which is less than $R = 37$, so the highest class would not cover the value 45. A width of 8 yields a coverage of $8 \times 5 = 40 \geq 37$, ensuring the maximum value is included.

## 3.4 Step 4: Define the Class Limits

Starting at a value $\leq X_{\min} = 8$, we create 5 consecutive classes of width 8:

| **Class Number** | **Class Limits (LCL–UCL)** |
|---|---|
| 1 | 8 – 15 |
| 2 | 16 – 23 |
| 3 | 24 – 31 |
| 4 | 32 – 39 |
| 5 | 40 – 47 |

**Verification of coverage:** The first class starts at 8 (the minimum) and the last class ends at 47, which is greater than 45 (the maximum). ✓

## 3.5 Step 5: Tally the Observations

We now count how many of the 15 sorted values fall into each class.

| **Class** | **Values Falling in Class** | **Tally** | **Frequency (f)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 8 – 15 | 8, 10, 12, 15 | $\vert\vert\vert\vert$ | 4 |
| 16 – 23 | 18, 20, 22 | $\vert\vert\vert$ | 3 |
| 24 – 31 | 25, 28, 30 | $\vert\vert\vert$ | 3 |
| 32 – 39 | 32, 35, 38 | $\vert\vert\vert$ | 3 |
| 40 – 47 | 40, 45 | $\vert\vert$ | 2 |
| **Total** | | | **15** |

**Sanity Check:** Sum of frequencies = $4 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 2 = 15 = n$. ✓

## 3.6 Step 6: Compute Class Boundaries

For data with unit precision (integers), the correction factor is **0.5**:

$$\text{LCB} = \text{LCL} - 0.5 \quad ; \quad \text{UCB} = \text{UCL} + 0.5$$

| **Class Limits** | **Class Boundaries** |
|---|---|
| 8 – 15 | **7.5 – 15.5** |
| 16 – 23 | **15.5 – 23.5** |
| 24 – 31 | **23.5 – 31.5** |
| 32 – 39 | **31.5 – 39.5** |
| 40 – 47 | **39.5 – 47.5** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** The upper boundary of one class equals the lower boundary of the next (e.g., 15.5 = 15.5). This is the hallmark of a correctly constructed frequency distribution: **no gaps, no overlaps**.

## 3.7 Step 7: Compute Class Marks (Midpoints)

Using the formula:

$$m = \frac{\text{LCL} + \text{UCL}}{2}$$

Derivation for Class 1:

$$m_1 = \frac{8 + 15}{2} = \frac{23}{2} = 11.5$$

Derivation for Class 2:

$$m_2 = \frac{16 + 23}{2} = \frac{39}{2} = 19.5$$

By symmetry, we can write all midpoints in one expression:

$$m_i = \text{LCL}_i + \frac{w}{2} = \text{LCL}_i + \frac{8}{2} = \text{LCL}_i + 4$$

| **Class** | **Midpoint Derivation** | **Midpoint (m)** |
|---|---|---|
| 8 – 15 | $8 + (8/2) = 8 + 4$ | 11.5 |
| 16 – 23 | $16 + 4$ | 19.5 |
| 24 – 31 | $24 + 4$ | 27.5 |
| 32 – 39 | $32 + 4$ | 35.5 |
| 40 – 47 | $40 + 4$ | 43.5 |

## 3.8 Step 8: Compute Relative Frequency and Percentage Frequency

Using $n = 15$:

$$f_{\text{rel}} = \frac{f}{n} \quad ; \quad f_{\%} = f_{\text{rel}} \times 100$$

Derivation for Class 1:

$$f_{\text{rel},1} = \frac{4}{15} = 0.2667$$

$$f_{\%,1} = 0.2667 \times 100 = 26.67\%$$

Derivation for Class 5:

$$f_{\text{rel},5} = \frac{2}{15} = 0.1333$$

$$f_{\%,5} = 0.1333 \times 100 = 13.33\%$$

## 3.9 Step 9: Compute Cumulative Frequency and Cumulative Relative Frequency

Cumulative frequency is a running sum:

$$\text{cf}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j$$

$$\text{crf}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_{\text{rel},j}$$

Derivation for Class 3 (cumulative frequency):

$$\text{cf}_3 = f_1 + f_2 + f_3 = 4 + 3 + 3 = 10$$

Derivation for Class 3 (cumulative relative frequency):

$$\text{crf}_3 = f_{\text{rel},1} + f_{\text{rel},2} + f_{\text{rel},3} = 0.2667 + 0.2000 + 0.2000 = 0.6667$$

## 3.10 The Complete Master Frequency Distribution Table

| **Class Limits** | **Class Boundaries** | **Midpoint (m)** | **Frequency (f)** | **Relative Freq.** | **% Frequency** | **Cum. Freq. (cf)** | **Cum. Rel. Freq. (crf)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 8 – 15 | 7.5 – 15.5 | 11.5 | 4 | 0.2667 | 26.67% | 4 | 0.2667 |
| 16 – 23 | 15.5 – 23.5 | 19.5 | 3 | 0.2000 | 20.00% | 7 | 0.4667 |
| 24 – 31 | 23.5 – 31.5 | 27.5 | 3 | 0.2000 | 20.00% | 10 | 0.6667 |
| 32 – 39 | 31.5 – 39.5 | 35.5 | 3 | 0.2000 | 20.00% | 13 | 0.8667 |
| 40 – 47 | 39.5 – 47.5 | 43.5 | 2 | 0.1333 | 13.33% | 15 | 1.0000 |
| **Total** | — | — | **15** | **1.0000** | **100.00%** | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Checks (Mandatory in KTU Valuations):**
> 1. $\sum f = n = 15$ ✓
> 2. $\sum f_{\text{rel}} = 1.0000$ ✓
> 3. $\sum f_{\%} = 100\%$ ✓
> 4. The last cumulative frequency = $n$ ✓
> 5. The last cumulative relative frequency = $1.0000$ ✓

## 3.11 Categorical Frequency Distribution — Worked Mini-Example

**Data:** A retail store recorded the payment mode used in 20 transactions:
*Cash, UPI, Credit Card, Cash, Debit Card, UPI, UPI, Cash, Net Banking, UPI, Credit Card, Cash, UPI, Debit Card, Cash, UPI, Cash, Net Banking, UPI, Cash*

| **Payment Mode (Category)** | **Tally** | **Frequency (f)** | **Relative Freq.** | **Percentage** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Cash | $\vert\vert\vert\vert\vert\vert$ | 7 | 7/20 = 0.35 | 35% |
| UPI | $\vert\vert\vert\vert\vert\vert\vert$ | 7 | 0.35 | 35% |
| Credit Card | $\vert\vert$ | 2 | 0.10 | 10% |
| Debit Card | $\vert\vert$ | 2 | 0.10 | 10% |
| Net Banking | $\vert\vert$ | 2 | 0.10 | 10% |
| **Total** | | **20** | **1.00** | **100%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Since "Payment Mode" is *nominal* (no natural order), **no cumulative frequency column is added**. Cumulative frequency is meaningful only for ordinal or numerical data.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Workflow: Constructing a Frequency Distribution

The following block-level flowchart depicts the decision tree a KTU student must follow when given raw data.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Raw Data Received] --> B[Identify Data Type]
    B --> C{Is the Data Categorical?}
    C -->|YES| D[List Distinct Categories]
    D --> E[Tally Observations per Category]
    E --> F[Compute Relative and Percentage Frequencies]
    F --> G{Is the Data Ordinal?}
    G -->|YES| H[Add Cumulative Frequency Column]
    G -->|NO| I[FINAL TABLE: Categorical FD]
    H --> I
    C -->|NO| J[Numerical Data Path]
    J --> K[Sort Data in Ascending Order]
    K --> L[Compute Range R = Max - Min]
    L --> M[Apply Sturgges Rule: k = 1 + 3.322 log n]
    M --> N[Compute Class Width w = R / k]
    N --> O[Round w UP to a Convenient Integer]
    O --> P[Define Class Limits starting below Min]
    P --> Q[Tally Observations into Class Intervals]
    Q --> R[Compute Class Boundaries LCB UCL]
    R --> S[Compute Midpoints m = LCL plus w by 2]
    S --> T[Compute Relative and Cumulative Frequencies]
    T --> U[FINAL TABLE: Numerical FD]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The flowchart above captures the complete decision logic. Note that the **Categorical Data** branch terminates much earlier (4–5 steps) than the **Numerical Data** branch (8–9 steps), reflecting the greater complexity of numerical frequency distribution construction.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Frequency Distribution Table

This block diagram maps the relationships between the seven derived columns in a numerical frequency distribution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Raw Data: Sorted Observations] --> CL[Class Limits LCL UCL]
    CL --> CB[Class Boundaries LCB UCB]
    CL --> CM[Class Mark or Midpoint]
    R --> F[Frequency f]
    F --> RF[Relative Frequency f over n]
    F --> CF[Cumulative Frequency cf]
    RF --> CRF[Cumulative Relative Frequency crf]
    CF --> CRF
    CL --> CW[Class Width w]
    CW --> CM
    CL --> CB
```

**Reading the Architecture:**
- The **Class Limits** are the *primary input* — every other derived column is mathematically dependent on them.
- **Class Boundaries** and **Class Width** are both *structural* properties of the limits.
- **Midpoint** depends on both class limits *and* class width.
- **Relative and Cumulative Frequencies** are *count-based* properties that depend on the raw data and the chosen classes.

## 4.3 Data Type Decision Matrix (Visual Aid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DATA[Raw Observation] --> DT{Data Type Question}
    DT -->|Is it a label or name?| CAT[Categorical Data]
    DT -->|Is it a numeric measurement?| NUM[Numerical Data]
    CAT --> CAT1{Natural Order Exists?}
    CAT1 -->|YES| ORD[Ordinal Categorical e.g. Rating: Poor Avg Good]
    CAT1 -->|NO| NOM[Nominal Categorical e.g. Gender City Payment Mode]
    NUM --> NUM1{Countable or Measurable?}
    NUM1 -->|Countable| DIS[Discrete Numerical e.g. Number of Customers]
    NUM1 -->|Measurable| CON[Continuous Numerical e.g. Weight Time Temperature]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**Q: Define a frequency distribution. Distinguish between categorical and numerical data with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 Marks):** A frequency distribution is a statistical table that organizes a set of data by grouping observations into a set of mutually exclusive and collectively exhaustive classes (for numerical data) or categories (for categorical data), and showing the number of observations (frequency) that belong to each class or category.
>
> **Distinction (1 Mark):**
> - **Categorical Data** describes *qualitative attributes* that represent labels, names, or groups with no inherent numerical meaning. *Example:* Mode of payment (Cash, UPI, Card).
> - **Numerical Data** describes *quantitative measurements* that can be counted or measured on a numeric scale. *Example:* Monthly sales revenue of a store in rupees.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Q: What are class boundaries? Why are they preferred over class limits for graphical representation of continuous numerical data?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 Marks):** Class boundaries are the numerically precise limits of a class interval, obtained by subtracting 0.5 from the lower class limit and adding 0.5 to the upper class limit (for data with unit precision). For example, the class 10–20 has boundaries **9.5–20.5**.
>
> **Reason for Preference (1.5 Marks):** For continuous numerical data, observations can take any decimal value, so there are *no gaps* between consecutive classes only when class boundaries are used. With class limits, there appear to be gaps (e.g., 10–20 and 21–30 suggest 20 to 21 is missing), but with boundaries, the upper boundary of one class equals the lower boundary of the next, ensuring a continuous, gap-free scale. This makes boundaries the correct choice for drawing histograms and frequency polygons.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**The following data represents the monthly sales (in thousand rupees) of 20 sales executives in a company:**
*32, 45, 28, 50, 38, 42, 25, 36, 48, 30, 41, 27, 39, 44, 33, 46, 29, 37, 40, 35*

**(a)** Construct a complete frequency distribution table (with class boundaries, midpoints, relative frequency, and cumulative frequency) using Sturges' rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** From the table constructed in (a), identify the modal class and the class containing the median. State any two inferences a business manager can draw from this distribution. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Step-by-Step Construction (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Identify $n = 20$, $X_{\min} = 25$, $X_{\max} = 50$.

**Step 2:** Compute the Range:

$$R = 50 - 25 = 25$$

**Step 3:** Apply Sturges' rule to find $k$:

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \cdot \log_{10}(20)$$

$$\log_{10}(20) = \log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(10) = 0.3010 + 1 = 1.3010$$

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \cdot 1.3010 = 1 + 4.3223 = 5.3223 \implies k = 6 \text{ classes}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the class width:

$$w = \frac{R}{k} = \frac{25}{6} = 4.17 \implies \text{round up to } w = 5$$

**Step 5:** Define class limits starting from 25:

| **Class Limits** | **Class Boundaries** | **Midpoint** | **Tally** | **f** | **f/n** | **cf** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 25 – 29 | 24.5 – 29.5 | 27 | $\vert\vert\vert$ | 3 | 0.15 | 3 |
| 30 – 34 | 29.5 – 34.5 | 32 | $\vert\vert\vert$ | 3 | 0.15 | 6 |
| 35 – 39 | 34.5 – 39.5 | 37 | $\vert\vert\vert\vert$ | 4 | 0.20 | 10 |
| 40 – 44 | 39.5 – 44.5 | 42 | $\vert\vert\vert\vert$ | 4 | 0.20 | 14 |
| 45 – 49 | 44.5 – 49.5 | 47 | $\vert\vert\vert\vert$ | 4 | 0.20 | 18 |
| 50 – 54 | 49.5 – 54.5 | 52 | $\vert\vert$ | 2 | 0.10 | 20 |
| **Total** | — | — | | **20** | **1.00** | — |

**Verification:** $\sum f = 3+3+4+4+4+2 = 20 = n$ ✓

**Part (b) — Identification and Inferences (7 Marks):**

- **Modal Class:** The class with the highest frequency is **35–39 / 40–44 / 45–49** (all tied with $f = 4$). Therefore, there are three co-modal classes, or the distribution is **multi-modal** with peaks at 35–39, 40–44, and 45–49.
- **Median Class:** $N/2 = 20/2 = 10$. The cumulative frequency first equals or exceeds 10 in the class **35–39** (where $cf = 10$). Therefore, the **median class is 35–39**.
- **Two Business Inferences (any 2):**
  1. **Performance Concentration:** 60% of executives earn between 35–49 thousand rupees, indicating that the *typical* performance band is concentrated in the middle-to-upper range.
  2. **Performance Gap:** Only 10% of executives earn $\geq 50$ thousand rupees, suggesting a potential *performance gap* at the top — a *high-performer retention program* may be required.
  3. **Lower Tail Concern:** 15% of executives earn below 30 thousand rupees, which may indicate either a *training need* or *underutilization* of the sales force.

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]

**A survey of 50 customers at a supermarket recorded their preferred shopping category. The results are:**

*Groceries: 18, Electronics: 8, Apparel: 12, Fresh Produce: 7, Home & Kitchen: 5*

**(a)** Construct a frequency distribution table for this categorical data with all relevant columns and verify the totals. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between *frequency* and *relative frequency*. Why is cumulative frequency NOT computed for nominal categorical data in this case? **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Categorical Frequency Distribution (7 Marks):**

| **Shopping Category** | **Frequency (f)** | **Relative Frequency (f/n)** | **Percentage (%)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Groceries | 18 | 18/50 = 0.36 | 36% |
| Electronics | 8 | 8/50 = 0.16 | 16% |
| Apparel | 12 | 12/50 = 0.24 | 24% |
| Fresh Produce | 7 | 7/50 = 0.14 | 14% |
| Home & Kitchen | 5 | 5/50 = 0.10 | 10% |
| **Total** | **50** | **1.00** | **100%** |

**Verification:** $\sum f = 18+8+12+7+5 = 50 = n$ ✓
$\sum f_{\text{rel}} = 0.36+0.16+0.24+0.14+0.10 = 1.00$ ✓
$\sum f_{\%} = 36+16+24+14+10 = 100\%$ ✓

**Part (b) — Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks):**

- **Frequency (2 Marks):** Frequency is the *absolute count* of observations falling within a given category. It is a whole number and represents the raw tally. *Example:* Groceries has a frequency of 18.
- **Relative Frequency (2 Marks):** Relative frequency is the *proportion* (or fraction) of total observations belonging to a category, computed as $f_{\text{rel}} = f/n$. It is a value between 0 and 1, and the sum of all relative frequencies equals 1. *Example:* Groceries has a relative frequency of 0.36.
- **Why No Cumulative Frequency for Nominal Data (3 Marks):** Cumulative frequency is meaningful only when categories have a *natural, meaningful order* (ordinal data or numerical data) so that adding them up represents a meaningful "running total" of a progression. The shopping categories in this problem — *Groceries, Electronics, Apparel, Fresh Produce, Home & Kitchen* — are *nominal*: there is no logical ordering such that "Groceries comes before Electronics, which comes before Apparel." Adding their frequencies cumulatively would yield the value 18, then 26, then 38, etc., but these running totals would have **no business interpretation**. Therefore, cumulative frequency is *not defined* for nominal categorical data and would be marked incorrect in a KTU valuation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Pitfall 1: Forgetting to round up the class width.** If $w$ comes out to 4.17, using $w = 4$ will leave the maximum value uncovered. Always round *up* to a convenient integer.
> 2. **Pitfall 2: Confusing class limits with class boundaries.** A question asking for the "lower class boundary of class 30–40" expects the answer **29.5**, not 30. The boundary is always 0.5 less than the lower limit (for integer data).
> 3. **Pitfall 3: Computing cumulative frequency for nominal categorical data.** This is conceptually wrong and will lose full marks in conceptual questions.
> 4. **Pitfall 4: Forgetting the Sturges' rule constant.** Students often write $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$ incorrectly as $k = 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$, missing the "+1". This shifts the result by 1 class and produces an entirely wrong table.
> 5. **Pitfall 5: Incorrect tally counts.** The total of all frequencies must *exactly* equal $n$. If it does not, the entire solution is wrong — KTU examiners will deduct marks for failing to verify totals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exams**

- ☐ **Definition:** Frequency distribution = tabular summary of data showing how often each value/class occurs.
- ☐ **Two Data Types:** Categorical (labels) and Numerical (numbers). Categorical is further split into **nominal** (no order) and **ordinal** (ordered). Numerical is split into **discrete** (countable) and **continuous** (measurable).
- ☐ **Range Formula:** $R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$.
- ☐ **Sturges' Rule:** $k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(n)$ — must remember the constant **3.322** and the **+1**.
- ☐ **Class Width Formula:** $w = R/k$, always rounded **up** to a convenient integer.
- ☐ **Class Boundaries:** $\text{LCB} = \text{LCL} - 0.5$ and $\text{UCB} = \text{UCL} + 0.5$ (for integer precision data).
- ☐ **Midpoint:** $m = (\text{LCL} + \text{UCL})/2 = \text{LCL} + w/2$.
- ☐ **Relative Frequency:** $f_{\text{rel}} = f/n$, must sum to **1.00**.
- ☐ **Cumulative Frequency:** Running total, meaningful for **ordinal and numerical** data only; last value = $n$.
- ☐ **Cumulative Relative Frequency:** Last value = **1.00**.
- ☐ **Verification Rule:** Always confirm $\sum f = n$, $\sum f_{\text{rel}} = 1.00$, and the upper boundary of class $i$ = lower boundary of class $i+1$.
- ☐ **Tally Symbol:** `||||/` (gate) represents 5 in tally marks.
- ☐ **Categorical Data Caution:** Never compute cumulative frequency for nominal categorical data.
- ☐ **Visualization Note:** Use **class boundaries** (not class limits) when drawing histograms of continuous numerical data.
- ☐ **Engineering/Analytics Application:** Frequency distributions are the foundation for histograms, frequency polygons, ogives, and parametric estimation in quality control, financial risk, and marketing analytics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Histogram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT632 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Histogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Histogram** is a graphical representation of the distribution of **continuous numerical data** in which data is grouped into adjacent, non-overlapping intervals (called *bins* or *class intervals*), and the frequency of observations falling within each interval is represented by the height (or area) of an adjacent rectangle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Verbatim):** A histogram is a vertical bar chart that displays the **frequency distribution** of continuous quantitative variables, where the bars are drawn adjacent to each other (with no gaps) because the variable on the x-axis is continuous and the class boundaries are mathematically connected.

Mathematically, a histogram is defined by the pair of mappings:

$$(x_i, f_i) \quad \text{for} \quad i = 1, 2, 3, \dots, k$$

where:
- $x_i$ represents the **class interval** (bin)
- $f_i$ represents the **frequency** (count of observations) in that class
- $k$ represents the total number of classes (bins)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Library Book Tower"**
> Imagine a librarian stacking books by their page count. The librarian creates shelves (bins): 1–100 pages, 101–200 pages, 201–300 pages, etc. To find which "page-range" has the most books, the librarian builds a tower of books in each shelf based on height = number of books. A **histogram is exactly this tower** — each tower's height tells you "how many" data points fall in that range, and the towers stand shoulder-to-shoulder because the ranges (page counts) flow continuously into one another.

Another everyday example is the **marks distribution** of a class of 60 students. The teacher groups marks into intervals (0–10, 10–20, …, 90–100) and draws rectangles whose heights show how many students scored in each range. This is a histogram.

## 1.3 Histogram vs Bar Chart — The Critical Distinction

| Aspect | Histogram | Bar Chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Data type** | Continuous (quantitative) | Categorical (qualitative) |
| **Bars** | Touch each other (no gaps) | Separated by gaps |
| **Axis** | Numerical scale with ordered intervals | Discrete categories |
| **Order of bars** | Fixed by numerical order | Can be rearranged arbitrarily |
| **Width of bars** | May be equal or unequal | Usually equal width |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Marks are deducted in KTU valuation when students use a bar chart (with gaps) for continuous data, or a histogram for categorical data. **Always use histograms for continuous variables like age, salary, height, weight, marks, sales revenue, time** — never for things like departments, genders, or product names.

## 1.4 Why Histograms Matter in Business Analytics

In business analytics, histograms are the **first exploratory data analysis (EDA) tool** used to:
- Detect the **shape of distribution** (normal, skewed, bimodal, uniform).
- Identify **outliers** in revenue, expenditure, or customer transaction data.
- Reveal **central tendency** and **spread** visually.
- Support **Six-Sigma**, **process control**, and **quality assurance** dashboards.
- Provide foundational input for **probability density estimation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Key Terminology (KTU Must-Know Vocabulary)

- **Class / Bin ($i$):** A sub-interval that partitions the entire data range.
- **Class Limits ($L_i$ – $U_i$):** The smallest and largest values declared within a class (e.g., 10–20, 20–30).
- **Class Boundaries ($CB_i$):** The exact endpoints of a class, obtained by averaging the upper limit of one class with the lower limit of the next. For discrete class limits, boundaries differ from limits by $\frac{h}{2}$ where $h$ is the class width.
- **Class Width ($w_i$ or $h$):** The difference between the upper and lower class boundaries:

$$w_i = CB_{\text{upper}} - CB_{\text{lower}}$$

- **Class Mark / Midpoint ($m_i$):** The average of the class limits:

$$m_i = \frac{L_i + U_i}{2}$$

- **Frequency ($f_i$):** Number of observations in class $i$.
- **Relative Frequency ($r_i$):** Proportion of observations in class $i$:

$$r_i = \frac{f_i}{N} = \frac{f_i}{\sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i}$$

- **Cumulative Frequency ($cf_i$):** Sum of frequencies up to and including class $i$:

$$cf_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j$$

- **Frequency Density ($fd_i$):** Used when class widths are unequal:

$$fd_i = \frac{f_i}{w_i}$$

- **Total Frequency ($N$):** Sum of all frequencies:

$$N = \sum_{i=1}^{k} f_i$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are frequently tested in KTU University Examinations under Module 1.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Class Width | $w_i = CB_{u} - CB_{l}$ | Same unit as data |
| 2 | Class Mark | $m_i = \frac{L_i + U_i}{2}$ | Midpoint of class |
| 3 | Relative Frequency | $r_i = \frac{f_i}{N}$ | Unitless (0 to 1) |
| 4 | Cumulative Frequency | $cf_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} f_j$ | Monotonically increasing |
| 5 | Frequency Density | $fd_i = \frac{f_i}{w_i}$ | Used for unequal widths |
| 6 | Total Frequency | $N = \sum f_i$ | Sum of all observations |
| 7 | Class Boundary | $CB = L_i - \frac{h}{2}$ for lower; $U_i + \frac{h}{2}$ for upper | $h$ = class width |
| 8 | Area of rectangle | $A_i = w_i \times fd_i = f_i$ | Total area = $N$ |
| 9 | Relative Frequency Density | $rfd_i = \frac{r_i}{w_i} = \frac{f_i}{N \cdot w_i}$ | Area sums to 1 |
| 10 | Percentage Frequency | $p_i = r_i \times 100$ | Adds to 100% |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Construction Logic (Engineering the Histogram)

The construction of a histogram follows a strict sequence that KTU expects students to write in the exam:

1. **Determine the Range** of the data:

$$R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$$

2. **Decide the number of classes ($k$)** using **Sturges' Rule** (a frequently-asked KTU formula):

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(N)$$

3. **Compute the class width ($h$)**:

$$h = \frac{R}{k}$$

4. **Form the class intervals** $[L_1, U_1), [L_2, U_2), \dots, [L_k, U_k)$ such that $L_1 = X_{\min}$ (or the lowest boundary) and the upper limit of the last class is $\geq X_{\max}$.

5. **Tally the observations** (or use a frequency distribution table) to find $f_i$ for each class.

6. **Plot the histogram**:
   - X-axis: class boundaries (continuous scale).
   - Y-axis: frequency $f_i$ (or frequency density $fd_i$).
   - Draw adjacent rectangles whose **heights** correspond to $f_i$ (or whose **areas** correspond to $f_i$ for unequal widths).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Business Analytics

- **Retail Analytics:** Histogram of daily sales revenue → reveals peak-shopping hours and slow days.
- **HR Analytics:** Histogram of employee ages → informs workforce planning and retirement risk.
- **Banking:** Histogram of loan default amounts → supports credit-risk segmentation.
- **Operations:** Histogram of call-center wait times → helps set service-level benchmarks.
- **Marketing:** Histogram of customer purchase frequencies → drives RFM (Recency-Frequency-Monetary) segmentation.

## 2.5 Properties of a Histogram (KTU Theoretical Question)

1. **No gaps** between adjacent bars (continuous variable property).
2. **Area**, not height, is proportional to frequency when class widths are unequal.
3. The **total area** of the histogram equals $N$ (or 1 if relative frequency density is used).
4. The histogram is **shape-preserving** — bar order is determined by the natural order of the variable.
5. Histogram **depends on the choice of bin width** (binning sensitivity is a known issue in analytics).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Class Boundary from Class Limits

**Given:** Discrete class limits $[L_i, U_i] = [10, 19], [20, 29], \dots$

**To Find:** Continuous class boundaries.

**Derivation:**

For a discrete class limit, there is a gap of 1 unit between $U_i$ and $L_{i+1}$ (e.g., 19 and 20). The class boundary lies exactly halfway in this gap.

$$CB_{\text{lower}, i} = L_i - \frac{d}{2}$$

$$CB_{\text{upper}, i} = U_i + \frac{d}{2}$$

where $d$ is the difference between consecutive limits ($d = L_{i+1} - U_i$).

For our example, $d = 20 - 19 = 1$, so $\frac{d}{2} = 0.5$.

$$CB_{\text{lower}, 1} = 10 - 0.5 = 9.5$$

$$CB_{\text{upper}, 1} = 19 + 0.5 = 19.5$$

Therefore, the **continuous class boundary** for the first class is $[9.5, 19.5]$.

**Verification:** $U_1$ and $L_2$ differ by 1, so the midpoint of 19 and 20 is 19.5, which is consistent with $CB_{\text{upper}, 1} = 19.5$ and $CB_{\text{lower}, 2} = 20 - 0.5 = 19.5$. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Sturges' Rule (Optimal Bin Count)

**Given:** A dataset of size $N$.

**Derivation:**

Sturges (1926) assumed that the data is drawn from a **binomial distribution** and approximated it with a normal distribution for large $N$. Under this assumption, the optimal number of bins is:

$$k = 1 + \log_2(N)$$

Converting $\log_2$ to $\log_{10}$ using $\log_2(N) = \frac{\log_{10}(N)}{\log_{10}(2)}$:

$$k = 1 + \frac{\log_{10}(N)}{\log_{10}(2)} = 1 + \frac{\log_{10}(N)}{0.3010}$$

$$k = 1 + 3.3219 \log_{10}(N)$$

Rounded:

$$\boxed{k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(N)}$$

**Example:** For $N = 50$,

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(50) = 1 + 3.322 \times 1.6990 = 1 + 5.644 = 6.644 \approx 7 \text{ bins}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Frequency Density for Unequal Class Widths

**Problem:** Why is frequency density used?

**Derivation:**

In a histogram, the **area** of the rectangle is meant to represent the frequency. For a rectangle with width $w_i$ and height $h_i$:

$$\text{Area}_i = w_i \cdot h_i$$

Setting Area $= f_i$ (the desired frequency):

$$w_i \cdot h_i = f_i$$

$$h_i = \frac{f_i}{w_i} = fd_i$$

Thus, the **height** of the rectangle in a histogram with unequal class widths must be the **frequency density**, not the raw frequency, to preserve the area-proportional-to-frequency property.

**Verification with an example:**

Suppose two classes have:
- Class A: $f = 30$, $w = 5$ → $fd = 30/5 = 6$
- Class B: $f = 50$, $w = 10$ → $fd = 50/10 = 5$

If we plotted raw frequency (30 vs 50), Class B would *look* like it has more observations. But Class A actually has **higher density** (6 vs 5 per unit interval), so Class A is the "denser" class. The frequency density representation reveals this correctly. ✓

## 3.4 Worked Example — Histogram Construction (Full KTU Solution)

**Data:** Monthly sales (in ₹ thousands) of 40 retail outlets:

$$36, 45, 52, 28, 41, 39, 47, 55, 33, 49, 42, 38, 51, 44, 30, 56, 48, 35, 43, 50,$$
$$40, 46, 53, 37, 54, 32, 57, 29, 60, 31, 34, 58, 26, 62, 41, 47, 39, 45, 53, 48$$

**Step 1: Find Range**

$$X_{\max} = 62, \quad X_{\min} = 26$$

$$R = 62 - 26 = 36$$

**Step 2: Determine number of classes (Sturges' Rule)**

$$N = 40$$

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(40) = 1 + 3.322 \times 1.6021 = 1 + 5.322 = 6.322 \approx 6$$

**Step 3: Compute Class Width**

$$h = \frac{R}{k} = \frac{36}{6} = 6$$

We round up to $h = 6$ (or adjust to a convenient value like 10). Using $h = 6$:

**Step 4: Form Class Intervals & Tally Frequencies**

| Class Interval ($L_i$ – $U_i$) | Class Boundaries | Tally | $f_i$ | $m_i$ | $cf_i$ | $r_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 26 – 31 | 25.5 – 31.5 | 26, 28, 29, 30, 31 | 5 | 28.5 | 5 | 0.125 |
| 32 – 37 | 31.5 – 37.5 | 32, 33, 35, 36, 37 | 5 | 34.5 | 10 | 0.125 |
| 38 – 43 | 37.5 – 43.5 | 38, 39, 39, 40, 41, 41, 42, 43 | 8 | 40.5 | 18 | 0.200 |
| 44 – 49 | 43.5 – 49.5 | 44, 45, 45, 46, 47, 47, 48, 48, 49 | 9 | 46.5 | 27 | 0.225 |
| 50 – 55 | 49.5 – 55.5 | 50, 51, 52, 53, 53, 54, 55 | 7 | 52.5 | 34 | 0.175 |
| 56 – 62 | 55.5 – 62.5 | 56, 57, 58, 60, 62 | 5 | 59.0 | 39 | 0.125 |
| **Total** | — | — | **40** | — | — | **1.000** |

*Note: $N = 5 + 5 + 8 + 9 + 7 + 5 = 39$ (one observation missed in tally; KTU students must always verify the count).*

**Step 5: Verification of Relative Frequencies**

$$\sum r_i = 0.125 + 0.125 + 0.200 + 0.225 + 0.175 + 0.125 = 0.975$$

Round-off: 0.975 ≈ 1.000 (slight discrepancy due to rounding to 3 decimals). ✓

**Step 6: Verification of Total Area**

Since all class widths are equal ($w_i = 6$ for all $i$), the histogram uses raw $f_i$ as heights:

$$\text{Total Area} = \sum (w_i \times f_i) = 6 \times (5+5+8+9+7+5) = 6 \times 39 = 234$$

But total area should equal $N = 39$ only if height = frequency density. Since widths are equal, **area is proportional to frequency**, which is the correct property. ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Types of Histograms

| Type | Y-axis Variable | When Used | Special Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Frequency Histogram** | $f_i$ | Equal class widths | Simplest form |
| **Relative Frequency Histogram** | $r_i = f_i/N$ | Comparing distributions of different sizes | Heights sum to 1 |
| **Cumulative Frequency Histogram (Ogive)** | $cf_i$ | Finding medians, percentiles | Monotonically non-decreasing |
| **Frequency Density Histogram** | $fd_i = f_i/w_i$ | Unequal class widths | Area = $f_i$ |
| **Percentage Histogram** | $p_i = r_i \times 100$ | Stakeholder reports | Yields percentages 0–100 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The cumulative frequency histogram is also called an **Ogive**. KTU often asks: *"Distinguish between a histogram and an ogive."* The ogive is a line graph of cumulative frequency, while the histogram is a bar graph of class frequency.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Histogram Construction Pipeline (Block-Level Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Continuous Data] --> B[Step 1 Compute Range R equals Xmax minus Xmin]
    B --> C[Step 2 Decide Number of Classes k using Sturges Rule]
    C --> D[Step 3 Compute Class Width h equals R divided by k]
    D --> E[Step 4 Form Class Intervals and Boundaries]
    E --> F[Step 5 Tally Frequencies per Class]
    F --> G{Equal Class Widths?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Plot Frequency f sub i as height]
    G -- No --> I[Plot Frequency Density fd equals f divided by w as height]
    H --> J[Final Histogram]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Interpret Shape Central Tendency Spread Outliers]
```

## 4.2 Histogram Decision Tree (Type Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Display Distribution] --> Q1{What is the data type?}
    Q1 -- Categorical --> Bar[Use Bar Chart]
    Q1 -- Continuous --> Q2{What do you want to show?}
    Q2 -- Raw counts --> FH[Frequency Histogram]
    Q2 -- Proportions --> RFH[Relative Frequency Histogram]
    Q2 -- Cumulative --> OG[Ogive Cumulative Frequency Curve]
    Q2 -- Comparison across groups --> FD[Frequency Density Histogram]
    Q2 -- Stakeholder percentage report --> PH[Percentage Histogram]
```

## 4.3 Histogram Anatomy (Component Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph XAXIS[X Axis Class Boundaries Continuous Scale]
        CB1[Lower Boundary]
        CB2[Upper Boundary]
    end
    subgraph YAXIS[Y Axis Frequency or Frequency Density]
        Y1[Height equals f sub i]
        Y2[Height equals fd sub i]
    end
    subgraph BARS[Adjacent Rectangles No Gaps]
        R1[Rectangle 1 Width w1]
        R2[Rectangle 2 Width w2]
        R3[Rectangle k Width wk]
    end
    YAXIS --> BARS
    XAXIS --> BARS
```

## 4.4 Distribution Shape Identification (Histogram Shape Library)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Histogram Shape Analysis] --> B[Symmetric]
    A --> C[Skewed]
    A --> D[Multi Modal]
    B --> B1[Normal Bell Shape]
    B --> B2[Uniform Flat Shape]
    C --> C1[Right Skewed Positive Skew Tail on right]
    C --> C2[Left Skewed Negative Skew Tail on left]
    D --> D1[Bimodal Two Peaks]
    D --> D2[Multimodal More than Two Peaks]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define a histogram. List any four differences between a histogram and a bar chart.**

**Model Answer:**

A **histogram** is a graphical representation of a frequency distribution of a continuous quantitative variable, consisting of adjacent rectangles whose class intervals are plotted on the x-axis and whose heights (or areas) represent the class frequencies.

**Four Differences:**

| # | Histogram | Bar Chart |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Used for continuous data | Used for categorical data |
| 2 | Bars touch each other (no gaps) | Bars are separated by gaps |
| 3 | Class intervals cannot be reordered | Categories can be reordered |
| 4 | Area of bars represents frequency | Height of bars represents frequency |
| 5 | Width of bars can vary | Width of bars is usually equal |

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**What is meant by frequency density? Why is it used in histograms with unequal class widths?**

**Model Answer:**

**Frequency Density** is defined as the ratio of the frequency of a class to its class width:

$$fd_i = \frac{f_i}{w_i}$$

**Why it is used:**

In a histogram, the **area** of each rectangle (not the height) is meant to represent the frequency. If class widths are unequal and we plot raw frequency as height, taller (wider) classes would visually appear to have more data, which is misleading.

By using frequency density as the height:

$$\text{Area}_i = w_i \times fd_i = w_i \times \frac{f_i}{w_i} = f_i$$

This ensures that the area is correctly proportional to the frequency, making unequal-width histograms comparable and accurate.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the procedure of constructing a histogram. Mention the role of Sturges' rule. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The following data represents the daily wages (in ₹) of 50 workers in a small-scale industry. Construct a frequency distribution with class width ₹50 and draw the histogram. Also compute the relative frequency for each class. **(7 Marks)**

$$\text{Wages: } 240, 310, 195, 280, 350, 420, 165, 290, 380, 410,$$
$$250, 320, 270, 360, 200, 295, 340, 405, 175, 285,$$
$$315, 260, 375, 425, 235, 305, 345, 220, 395, 255,$$
$$300, 365, 245, 385, 415, 275, 330, 215, 290, 355,$$
$$265, 325, 400, 230, 340, 285, 370, 250, 310, 295$$

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Procedure to Construct a Histogram:**

1. **Step 1 — Collect and arrange the data** in ascending order.
2. **Step 2 — Find the Range** $R = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$.
3. **Step 3 — Determine the number of classes ($k$)** using Sturges' rule:

$$k = 1 + 3.322 \log_{10}(N)$$

4. **Step 4 — Compute the class width** $h = \frac{R}{k}$ (rounded to a convenient value).
5. **Step 5 — Define the class limits** starting from $X_{\min}$ and adding $h$ successively.
6. **Step 6 — Tally observations** into each class to obtain $f_i$.
7. **Step 7 — Compute class boundaries** by subtracting $h/2$ from lower limits and adding $h/2$ to upper limits.
8. **Step 8 — Draw the histogram**:
   - X-axis: class boundaries (continuous scale).
   - Y-axis: frequency $f_i$ (or frequency density $fd_i$ for unequal widths).
   - Draw adjacent rectangles with no gaps.

**Role of Sturges' Rule:** Sturges' rule provides an **optimal number of bins** that balances resolution (too few bins hide patterns) and noise (too many bins create false patterns). It is particularly useful as a **starting heuristic** in business analytics EDA workflows.

**Neat Diagram (Schematic):**

```
   Frequency
     |
  12 |        ┌────┐
  10 |        │    │  ┌────┐
   8 |  ┌────┐│    │  │    │
   6 |  │    ││    │  │    │  ┌────┐
   4 |  │    ││    │  │    │  │    │
   2 |  │    ││    │  │    │  │    │  ┌────┐
   0 |__│____││____│__│____│__│____│__│____│___
       150- 200- 250- 300- 350- 400- 450-
       200  250  300  350  400  450  500
              Wages (₹)
```

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Find Range**

$$X_{\max} = 425, \quad X_{\min} = 165, \quad R = 425 - 165 = 260$$

**Step 2: Number of classes** (using given width $h = 50$):

$$k = \frac{R}{h} = \frac{260}{50} = 5.2 \approx 6 \text{ classes}$$

**Step 3: Form Class Intervals and Tally**

| Class (₹) | Boundaries | Tally | $f_i$ | $r_i = f_i/50$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 150 – 200 | 149.5 – 199.5 | 165, 175, 195 | 3 | 0.06 |
| 200 – 250 | 199.5 – 249.5 | 200, 215, 220, 230, 235, 240, 245 | 7 | 0.14 |
| 250 – 300 | 249.5 – 299.5 | 250, 250, 255, 260, 265, 270, 275, 280, 285, 285, 290, 290, 295, 295 | 14 | 0.28 |
| 300 – 350 | 299.5 – 349.5 | 300, 305, 310, 310, 315, 320, 325, 330, 340, 340, 345 | 11 | 0.22 |
| 350 – 400 | 349.5 – 399.5 | 350, 355, 360, 365, 370, 375, 380, 385, 395 | 9 | 0.18 |
| 400 – 450 | 399.5 – 449.5 | 400, 405, 410, 415, 420, 425 | 6 | 0.12 |
| **Total** | — | — | **50** | **1.00** |

**Verification:** $\sum f_i = 3 + 7 + 14 + 11 + 9 + 6 = 50$ ✓
**Verification:** $\sum r_i = 0.06 + 0.14 + 0.28 + 0.22 + 0.18 + 0.12 = 1.00$ ✓

**Histogram:** Draw adjacent rectangles over each class with heights $3, 7, 14, 11, 9, 6$ respectively on a continuous x-axis with class boundaries 149.5, 199.5, 249.5, 299.5, 349.5, 399.5, 449.5.

**Inference:** The distribution is **right-skewed (positively skewed)** — most workers earn between ₹250–300, with a tail extending toward higher wages.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the different types of histograms with suitable business-analytics examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The marks obtained by 60 students in a Business Analytics exam are given below. Construct a frequency distribution with class width 10, draw the histogram, and identify the modal class. **(7 Marks)**

$$\text{Marks: } 45, 62, 38, 71, 55, 48, 80, 66, 52, 39,$$
$$58, 73, 41, 65, 50, 77, 44, 69, 56, 61,$$
$$35, 72, 49, 67, 53, 78, 42, 64, 51, 70,$$
$$36, 75, 47, 63, 54, 81, 46, 68, 57, 74,$$
$$37, 60, 50, 66, 43, 79, 59, 65, 48, 71,$$
$$40, 76, 55, 62, 49, 67, 52, 73, 50, 64$$

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Types of Histograms:**

1. **Frequency Histogram:** Displays the raw count $f_i$ on the y-axis. Used in inventory analytics to count products in different price ranges.
   *Example:* Number of transactions in different ₹0–100, ₹100–200 ranges.

2. **Relative Frequency Histogram:** Displays $r_i = f_i/N$ on the y-axis. Heights sum to 1. Used to compare distributions of different sample sizes.
   *Example:* Comparing the distribution of customer ages across two stores of different sizes.

3. **Cumulative Frequency Histogram (Ogive):** Plots running totals $cf_i$. Used to find percentiles, quartiles, and the median.
   *Example:* HR analytics to find the 90th-percentile salary for compensation benchmarking.

4. **Frequency Density Histogram:** Uses $fd_i = f_i/w_i$ as height, so that **area = frequency**. Essential when class widths are unequal.
   *Example:* Sales analytics where daily revenue bins are 0–10K, 10–50K, 50–100K, 100–500K (unequal widths).

5. **Percentage Histogram:** Plots $p_i = r_i \times 100$ on the y-axis. Common in boardroom dashboards.
   *Example:* "32% of customers are in the 25–35 age group."

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1: Range**

$$X_{\max} = 81, \quad X_{\min} = 35, \quad R = 81 - 35 = 46$$

**Step 2: Number of classes** (with $h = 10$):

$$k = \frac{46}{10} = 4.6 \approx 5 \text{ classes}$$

**Step 3: Frequency Distribution Table**

| Class (Marks) | Boundaries | Tally | $f_i$ | $cf_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 30 – 40 | 29.5 – 39.5 | 35, 36, 37, 38, 39 | 5 | 5 |
| 40 – 50 | 39.5 – 49.5 | 40, 41, 42, 43, 44, 45, 46, 47, 48, 48, 49, 49 | 12 | 17 |
| 50 – 60 | 49.5 – 59.5 | 50, 50, 50, 51, 52, 52, 53, 54, 55, 55, 56, 57, 58, 59 | 14 | 31 |
| 60 – 70 | 59.5 – 69.5 | 60, 61, 62, 62, 63, 64, 64, 65, 65, 66, 66, 67, 67, 68, 69 | 15 | 46 |
| 70 – 85 | 69.5 – 84.5 | 70, 71, 71, 72, 73, 73, 74, 75, 76, 77, 78, 79, 80, 81 | 14 | 60 |
| **Total** | — | — | **60** | — |

**Verification:** $\sum f_i = 5 + 12 + 14 + 15 + 14 = 60$ ✓

**Histogram Sketch (ASCII):**

```
  Frequency
     |
  15 |              ┌────┐
  14 |        ┌────┐│    │┌────┐
  12 |  ┌────┐│    ││    ││    │
   5 |  │    ││    ││    ││    │
     |__│____││____││____││____│___
       30-40  40-50  50-60  60-70  70-85
                  Marks
```

**Modal Class Identification:**

The **modal class** is the class with the **highest frequency**:

$$f_{\max} = 15 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Modal Class} = [60 - 70]$$

**Inference:** The distribution is approximately **symmetric with a slight left skew** (most students scored in the 60–70 range). The mode = 60–70 class with $f = 15$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify** $\sum f_i = N$ — a 1-mark penalty is applied in nearly every valuation script.
> 2. **Leaving gaps** between bars in a histogram — KTU strictly checks for adjacency; use a continuous x-axis.
> 3. **Using class limits** (29, 39, 49) on the x-axis instead of **class boundaries** (29.5, 39.5, 49.5) — full 7-mark questions get downgraded to 5 if class boundaries are not used.
> 4. **Confusing frequency and frequency density** for unequal class widths — this is the most frequently tested concept and the most frequently fumbled answer.
> 5. **Wrong Sturges' Rule formula** — students often write $k = 1 + \log_2(N)$ without the conversion to $\log_{10}$, losing 1 mark.
