topics.title: Discrete Probability Distribution: Binomial, Poisson
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT632 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Discrete Probability Distribution: Binomial, Poisson

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Discrete Probability Distribution — Formal Definition

A **Discrete Probability Distribution** is a mathematical function that assigns a probability $P(X = x_i)$ to each possible value $x_i$ of a discrete random variable $X$. The probabilities must satisfy two fundamental axioms:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} P(X = x_i) = 1 \quad \text{and} \quad 0 \le P(X = x_i) \le 1 \; \forall \; i$$

A *discrete* random variable takes only a countable set of values (integers such as 0, 1, 2, 3, ...), as opposed to a continuous random variable which can take any value within an interval.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (PECAT632 — Module 2):** The two cornerstone discrete distributions for business analytics are the **Binomial Distribution** and the **Poisson Distribution**. Mastery of their Probability Mass Functions (PMF), means, variances, and real-world business applications is mandatory for ESE and internal assessments.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bakery Counter"

Imagine you are the owner of a small bakery. Every day, you observe a **countable** event:
- "How many customers walked in today?" → could be 50, 51, 52, ...
- "How many of those customers bought a cupcake?" → could be 0, 1, 2, ...

These counts are **discrete** — they jump from one whole number to the next; you cannot have 2.7 customers. A probability distribution tells you, *given a long-run pattern*, what is the likelihood of observing any specific count. If you know that 30% of customers buy a cupcake, and 100 customers come in, the **Binomial** distribution tells you the probability of getting *exactly* 30 buyers. If customers trickle in at an *average rate* of 4 per hour, the **Poisson** distribution tells you the probability of *exactly* 2 customers arriving in the next 10 minutes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Continuous vs. Discrete Analogy:**
> - **Discrete** = Counting whole things (customers, defects, goals).
> - **Continuous** = Measuring things (time, weight, height).
> 
> The Binomial and Poisson are *counting* tools — they belong to the discrete family.

### 1.3 Why These Two Distributions Matter in Business Analytics

In business analytics, decisions are driven by counts:
- **Binomial**: "Will the customer buy (Yes/No)?" "Is the transaction fraudulent (Fraud/Not Fraud)?"
- **Poisson**: "How many service calls arrive per hour?" "How many defects occur per batch?"

Both distributions transform raw data into **probabilistic forecasts** that feed directly into risk assessment, inventory planning, and quality control.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Binomial Distribution

#### 2.1.1 Conceptual Setup

A **Bernoulli trial** is a random experiment with exactly two outcomes: **Success** (with probability $p$) and **Failure** (with probability $q = 1 - p$). A Binomial distribution is formed by performing $n$ **independent** Bernoulli trials and counting the number of successes $X$.

#### 2.1.2 The Four Essential Conditions for a Binomial Experiment

1. **Fixed number of trials ($n$):** The experiment is repeated exactly $n$ times.
2. **Two outcomes per trial:** Each trial yields either Success or Failure.
3. **Constant probability ($p$):** The probability of success remains the same for every trial.
4. **Independence:** The outcome of one trial does not influence the outcome of another.

If all four conditions hold, $X \sim \text{Binomial}(n, p)$.

#### 2.1.3 Probability Mass Function (PMF) Derivation Logic

Suppose we want exactly $x$ successes in $n$ trials. Each specific arrangement of $x$ successes and $(n-x)$ failures has probability:

$$p^x \cdot (1-p)^{n-x}$$

The number of ways to *choose* which $x$ trials are successes is given by the binomial coefficient $\binom{n}{x}$.

#### 2.1.4 Mean, Variance, and Standard Deviation — The Why

- **Mean ($\mu$ or $E[X]$):** Average successes = trials $\times$ success rate per trial = $n \cdot p$.
- **Variance ($\sigma^2$):** Each Bernoulli trial has variance $p(1-p)$. For $n$ independent trials, variances add: $\sigma^2 = n \cdot p \cdot q$.
- **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \sqrt{n p q}$.

### 2.2 The Poisson Distribution

#### 2.2.1 Conceptual Setup

The Poisson distribution models the **number of events occurring in a fixed interval of time or space**, when these events happen with a known constant mean rate $\lambda$ and independently of the time since the last event. It is the *limit* of the Binomial distribution when $n \to \infty$ and $p \to 0$ such that $n \cdot p = \lambda$ remains fixed.

#### 2.2.2 The Three Essential Conditions for a Poisson Process

1. **Stationarity (Constant rate $\lambda$):** Events occur at a constant average rate per unit interval.
2. **Independence:** The occurrence of one event does not affect the probability of another event.
3. **Non-simultaneity (Rare events):** The probability of two events occurring at the exact same instant is negligible.

If these hold, $X \sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)$.

#### 2.2.3 Mean and Variance — The Why

- The Poisson distribution is uniquely characterized by the fact that **mean = variance = $\lambda$**.
- This is a powerful business diagnostic: if observed variance significantly exceeds the mean, the process is **not** Poisson (over-dispersion).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Binomial $X \sim B(n,p)$** | **Poisson $X \sim P(\lambda)$** |
|---|---|---|
| **PMF** $P(X = x)$ | $\binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$ | $\dfrac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$ |
| **Notation** | $n$ = trials, $p$ = success prob., $q = 1-p$ | $\lambda$ = mean rate per interval |
| **Support** | $x \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ | $x \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots\}$ |
| **Mean** $E[X]$ | $n \cdot p$ | $\lambda$ |
| **Variance** $\text{Var}(X)$ | $n \cdot p \cdot q$ | $\lambda$ |
| **Std. Deviation** $\sigma$ | $\sqrt{n p q}$ | $\sqrt{\lambda}$ |
| **MGF** $M_X(t)$ | $(q + p e^t)^n$ | $e^{\lambda(e^t - 1)}$ |
| **Skewness** | $\dfrac{q - p}{\sqrt{n p q}}$ | $\lambda^{-1/2}$ |
| **Parameters** | 2 ($n$ and $p$) | 1 ($\lambda$) |
| **Range of parameters** | $n \in \mathbb{Z}^+$, $0 < p < 1$ | $\lambda > 0$ |
| **Special identity** | $\sum_{x=0}^{n} P(X=x) = 1$ | $\sum_{x=0}^{\infty} P(X=x) = 1$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cumulative Probability Notation:**
> - $P(X \le a) = \sum_{x=0}^{a} P(X = x)$
> - $P(X \ge a) = \sum_{x=a}^{n} P(X = x)$ (Binomial) or $\sum_{x=a}^{\infty} P(X = x)$ (Poisson)
> - $P(a \le X \le b) = \sum_{x=a}^{b} P(X = x)$

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering and Business Fields

- **Quality Control (Binomial):** A semiconductor manufacturer tests 50 chips per batch. The probability a chip is defective is 0.02. The number of defective chips follows $B(50, 0.02)$. Engineers use this to set acceptance sampling thresholds.
- **Telecommunications (Poisson):** Call arrivals at a customer service center average 5 per minute. Network engineers use $\lambda = 5$ to size buffer capacities and staff helpdesks.
- **Insurance (Poisson):** Accident claims per month modeled as Poisson to compute premiums.
- **Marketing (Binomial):** A/B testing — determining if a higher click-through rate is statistically significant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Mean of the Binomial Distribution

We start with a single Bernoulli trial. Let $Y_i$ be the indicator of success on the $i$-th trial:

$$Y_i = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{with probability } p \\ 0 & \text{with probability } q = 1-p \end{cases}$$

The Binomial random variable $X$ is the sum of $n$ independent such indicators:

$$X = Y_1 + Y_2 + Y_3 + \cdots + Y_n$$

**Step 1:** Compute $E[Y_i]$ (the mean of a single Bernoulli trial).

$$E[Y_i] = (1)(p) + (0)(q) = p$$

**Step 2:** Apply the linearity of expectation. Even though the $Y_i$ are independent, linearity holds *without* requiring independence:

$$E[X] = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[Y_i]$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $E[Y_i] = p$ into the summation:

$$E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p = n \cdot p$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\mu_{Binomial} = n \cdot p}$

### 3.2 Derivation: Variance of the Binomial Distribution

**Step 1:** Compute $\text{Var}(Y_i)$ for a single Bernoulli trial.

$$\text{Var}(Y_i) = E[Y_i^2] - (E[Y_i])^2$$

Since $Y_i$ only takes values 0 and 1, $Y_i^2 = Y_i$, so:

$$E[Y_i^2] = (1^2)(p) + (0^2)(q) = p$$

$$\text{Var}(Y_i) = p - p^2 = p(1-p) = p \cdot q$$

**Step 2:** Apply the variance-addition rule for **independent** random variables:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \text{Var}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} Y_i\right) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(Y_i)$$

(Note: this requires independence, which is one of the Binomial conditions.)

**Step 3:** Substitute $\text{Var}(Y_i) = pq$:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} p \cdot q = n \cdot p \cdot q$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\sigma^2_{Binomial} = n \cdot p \cdot q}$

### 3.3 Derivation: Mean of the Poisson Distribution

**Step 1:** Write the PMF:

$$P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots$$

**Step 2:** Apply the definition of expectation for a discrete random variable:

$$E[X] = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot P(X = x) = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

**Step 3:** The $x = 0$ term is zero, so we can start from $x = 1$. Also, write $x = x \cdot (x-1)!$ to simplify:

$$E[X] = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \frac{x \cdot e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!} = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{(x-1)!}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $k = x - 1$ (so $k$ goes from 0 to $\infty$, and $x = k + 1$):

$$E[X] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^{k+1}}{k!} = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^k}{k!}$$

**Step 5:** Recognize the Maclaurin series for the exponential: $\sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^k}{k!} = e^{\lambda}$:

$$E[X] = \lambda \cdot e^{-\lambda} \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda \cdot e^0 = \lambda$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\mu_{Poisson} = \lambda}$

### 3.4 Derivation: Variance of the Poisson Distribution

**Step 1:** We need $E[X^2]$. Use the identity $E[X^2] = \text{Var}(X) + (E[X])^2$, or compute directly:

$$E[X^2] = \sum_{x=0}^{\infty} x^2 \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

**Step 2:** Separate the $x = 0$ term (which is zero) and use $x^2 = x(x-1) + x$:

$$E[X^2] = \sum_{x=1}^{\infty} \bigl[x(x-1) + x\bigr] \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

$$E[X^2] = \underbrace{\sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x(x-1) \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}}_{T_1} + \underbrace{\sum_{x=1}^{\infty} x \cdot \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}}_{T_2}$$

**Step 3:** For $T_1$, write $x(x-1) \cdot \frac{1}{x!} = \frac{1}{(x-2)!}$ (valid for $x \ge 2$):

$$T_1 = \sum_{x=2}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{(x-2)!}$$

Let $k = x - 2$:

$$T_1 = \lambda^2 \cdot e^{-\lambda} \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \frac{\lambda^k}{k!} = \lambda^2 \cdot e^{-\lambda} \cdot e^{\lambda} = \lambda^2$$

**Step 4:** For $T_2$, from our earlier derivation, $T_2 = E[X] = \lambda$:

$$E[X^2] = \lambda^2 + \lambda$$

**Step 5:** Compute variance:

$$\text{Var}(X) = E[X^2] - (E[X])^2 = (\lambda^2 + \lambda) - \lambda^2 = \lambda$$

**Result:** $\boxed{\sigma^2_{Poisson} = \lambda}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Beautiful Conclusion:** For a Poisson random variable, $\text{Mean} = \text{Variance} = \lambda$. This is the unique "equi-dispersion" property and is the most testable fact in KTU exams.

### 3.5 Derivation: Binomial-to-Poisson Limit

This is a frequently asked derivation in KTU exams — show that as $n \to \infty$ and $p \to 0$ with $n p = \lambda$ (fixed), the Binomial PMF converges to the Poisson PMF.

**Step 1:** Start with the Binomial PMF:

$$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $p = \lambda / n$:

$$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} \left(\frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^x \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}$$

**Step 3:** Expand the binomial coefficient:

$$\binom{n}{x} = \frac{n!}{x!\,(n-x)!} = \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-x+1)}{x!}$$

**Step 4:** Form the limit:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(X = x) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-x+1)}{x!} \cdot \frac{\lambda^x}{n^x} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}$$

**Step 5:** Split the limit into three parts:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-x+1)}{n^x} \cdot \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\lambda^x}{x!} \cdot \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}$$

**Step 6:** Evaluate each part:
- **Part A:** $\frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-x+1)}{n^x} = 1 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{n}\right) \cdots \left(1 - \frac{x-1}{n}\right) \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$.
- **Part B:** $\frac{\lambda^x}{x!}$ is a constant.
- **Part C:** $\left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x} = \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^n \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{-x} \to e^{-\lambda} \cdot 1 = e^{-\lambda}$.

**Step 7:** Multiply the three parts:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(X = x) = 1 \cdot \frac{\lambda^x}{x!} \cdot e^{-\lambda} = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

**Result:** The Binomial PMF converges to the Poisson PMF under the stated limiting conditions. This is called the **Poisson approximation to the Binomial** and is valid when $n \ge 20$ and $p \le 0.05$ (rule of thumb for KTU numerical problems).

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which?

| **Scenario** | **Use Binomial** | **Use Poisson** |
|---|---|---|
| Fixed number of trials $n$? | ✓ Yes | ✗ No |
| Counting occurrences over an interval? | ✗ No | ✓ Yes |
| Each trial has only 2 outcomes? | ✓ Yes | ✗ No |
| Mean number of events per interval $\lambda$? | ✗ No | ✓ Yes |
| Success probability $p$ is constant? | ✓ Yes | ✗ No |
| Events are rare (small $p$, large $n$)? | Approximate by Poisson | ✓ Yes |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Hierarchical Relationship of Discrete Distributions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["DISCRETE PROBABILITY DISTRIBUTIONS"] --> B["BINOMIAL DISTRIBUTION"]
    A --> C["POISSON DISTRIBUTION"]
    
    B --> B1["Parameters: n and p"]
    B --> B2["PMF: nCx px q nx"]
    B --> B3["Mean: np"]
    B --> B4["Variance: npq"]
    B --> B5["Examples: Defectives in batch, Yes No surveys"]
    
    C --> C1["Parameters: lambda"]
    C --> C2["PMF: e lambda x by x factorial"]
    C --> C3["Mean: lambda"]
    C --> C4["Variance: lambda"]
    C --> C5["Examples: Calls per hour, Arrivals per day"]
    
    B --> D["LIMITING CASE"]
    D --> C
    
    E["BERNOULLI TRIAL"] --> B
    E --> E1["Single trial with p success and q failure"]
    
    F["POISSON PROCESS ASSUMPTIONS"] --> C
    F --> F1["Stationarity of rate lambda"]
    F --> F2["Independent occurrences"]
    F --> F3["Rare simultaneous events"]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Analytical Workflow for Solving Business Problems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["STEP 1: Read the problem and identify the random variable X"] --> S2["STEP 2: Is X a count of successes in n fixed trials"]
    S2 -->|YES| S3["STEP 3A: Apply Binomial Distribution B of n p"]
    S2 -->|NO| S4["STEP 3B: Is X a count of events in a fixed time or space interval"]
    S4 -->|YES| S5["STEP 4: Apply Poisson Distribution with parameter lambda"]
    S4 -->|NO| S6["STEP 5: Consider alternative distributions"]
    
    S3 --> S7["STEP 6: Compute required probability using PMF and summation"]
    S5 --> S7
    S6 --> S7
    
    S7 --> S8["STEP 7: Compute mean and variance for verification"]
    S8 --> S9["STEP 8: Interpret result in business context"]
    
    P1["n large, p small approximately 20 and 0 point 0 5"] --> S5
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Parameter Space Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BINO["BINOMIAL PARAMETER SPACE"]
        BX["n: positive integer"]
        BY["p: between 0 and 1"]
    end
    
    subgraph POIS["POISSON PARAMETER SPACE"]
        PX["lambda: positive real number"]
    end
    
    BX --> L1["Limit as n approaches infinity"]
    BY --> L1
    L1 --> PX
    
    BINO --> APP["Poisson Approximation"]
    APP --> POIS
    
    POIS --> PROP["Equidispersion: Mean equals Variance"]
    BINO --> NORM["When np and nq are both greater than 5, approximates Normal"]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: MGF and Distribution Properties Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    MG["MOMENT GENERATING FUNCTION M sub X of t equals E of e to the power tX"] --> BINF["Binomial: q plus p e to the t, raised to n"]
    MG --> POISF["Poisson: e to the power lambda e to the t minus 1"]
    
    BINF --> M1["M prime at 0 equals Mean np"]
    POISF --> M2["M prime at 0 equals Mean lambda"]
    
    M1 --> M2B["M double prime at 0 minus square of Mean equals Variance npq"]
    M2 --> M2P["M double prime at 0 minus square of Mean equals Variance lambda"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the Binomial distribution. State the conditions under which a random variable follows a Binomial distribution.**

**Model Answer:**

A random variable $X$ is said to follow a Binomial distribution with parameters $n$ and $p$ if its probability mass function is given by:

$$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

where $q = 1 - p$, $0 < p < 1$, and $n$ is a positive integer.

**The four essential conditions are:**
1. The experiment consists of a **fixed number of trials** $n$.
2. Each trial has only **two possible outcomes**: success or failure.
3. The **probability of success $p$ is constant** for every trial.
4. The trials are **independent** of one another.

**Notation:** $X \sim B(n, p)$. The mean is $np$ and the variance is $npq$.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Poisson distribution with its probability mass function. Mention any two business applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A random variable $X$ follows a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda$ if its PMF is:

$$P(X = x) = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}, \quad x = 0, 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

where $\lambda > 0$ is the average number of occurrences per unit interval. The mean and variance are both equal to $\lambda$.

**Two business applications:**
1. **Call Center Management:** Number of incoming calls per hour at a customer helpline follows a Poisson distribution with parameter $\lambda$ equal to the historical average call rate. This is used for **staffing decisions**.
2. **Quality Control:** Number of defects per unit length of fabric, or per square meter of sheet metal, is modeled as Poisson. Used to set **control limits** in Six Sigma manufacturing.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Format:** Part B questions carry 14 marks each. They typically have sub-parts (a) for 7 marks and (b) for 7 marks. Students must answer *either* the full Question A *or* the full Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A manufacturing company finds that 10% of its products are defective. If a sample of 15 products is inspected, find the probability that:
**(i)** Exactly 3 products are defective.
**(ii)** At most 2 products are defective.
**(iii)** At least 1 product is defective.

**(b)** Derive the mean and variance of the Binomial distribution from first principles.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

**Solution to (a):** Given $n = 15$, $p = 0.10$, $q = 0.90$, and $X \sim B(15, 0.10)$.

**(i) Exactly 3 defective products:**

$$P(X = 3) = \binom{15}{3} (0.10)^3 (0.90)^{12}$$

Computing each factor:

$$\binom{15}{3} = \frac{15!}{3! \cdot 12!} = \frac{15 \times 14 \times 13}{3 \times 2 \times 1} = 455$$

$$(0.10)^3 = 0.001$$

$$(0.90)^{12} = 0.282429536 \text{ (from calculator)}$$

Therefore:

$$P(X = 3) = 455 \times 0.001 \times 0.282429536 = 0.1285$$

**Answer:** $P(X = 3) \approx 0.1285$

**(ii) At most 2 defective products** = $P(X = 0) + P(X = 1) + P(X = 2)$:

$$P(X = 0) = (0.90)^{15} = 0.205891$$

$$P(X = 1) = \binom{15}{1}(0.10)^1 (0.90)^{14} = 15 \times 0.10 \times 0.228768 = 0.343152$$

$$P(X = 2) = \binom{15}{2}(0.10)^2 (0.90)^{13} = 105 \times 0.01 \times 0.254187 = 0.266896$$

Adding:

$$P(X \le 2) = 0.205891 + 0.343152 + 0.266896 = 0.815939$$

**Answer:** $P(X \le 2) \approx 0.8159$

**(iii) At least 1 defective product** = $1 - P(X = 0)$:

$$P(X \ge 1) = 1 - 0.205891 = 0.794109$$

**Answer:** $P(X \ge 1) \approx 0.7941$

---

**Solution to (b): Derive mean and variance of Binomial distribution.**

Let $Y_i$ be the indicator of success in the $i$-th trial. Then $Y_i = 1$ with probability $p$ and $Y_i = 0$ with probability $q$.

The Binomial random variable is $X = Y_1 + Y_2 + \cdots + Y_n$.

**Mean:**

$$E[Y_i] = 1 \cdot p + 0 \cdot q = p$$

By linearity of expectation (which does not require independence):

$$E[X] = \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[Y_i] = n p$$

**Variance:**

$$E[Y_i^2] = 1^2 \cdot p + 0^2 \cdot q = p$$

$$\text{Var}(Y_i) = E[Y_i^2] - (E[Y_i])^2 = p - p^2 = p(1-p) = pq$$

Using independence of trials:

$$\text{Var}(X) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \text{Var}(Y_i) = n \cdot p \cdot q$$

**Final Answer:** Mean $= np$, Variance $= npq$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** On average, a customer service center receives 4 calls per hour. Assuming the calls follow a Poisson distribution, find the probability that:
**(i)** Exactly 6 calls are received in a given hour.
**(ii)** No calls are received in a given 15-minute interval.
**(iii)** More than 4 calls are received in 90 minutes.

**(b)** Show that the Binomial distribution converges to the Poisson distribution as $n \to \infty$ and $p \to 0$ with $np = \lambda$ held constant.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

**Solution to (a):** Given $\lambda = 4$ calls per hour.

**(i) Exactly 6 calls in 1 hour** ($\lambda = 4$):

$$P(X = 6) = \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4^6}{6!} = \frac{e^{-4} \cdot 4096}{720}$$

$$e^{-4} \approx 0.018316$$

$$P(X = 6) = \frac{0.018316 \times 4096}{720} = \frac{75.022}{720} = 0.1042$$

**Answer:** $P(X = 6) \approx 0.1042$

**(ii) No calls in 15 minutes:** The rate must be rescaled to 15 minutes:

$$\lambda_{15} = 4 \times \frac{15}{60} = 1 \text{ call per 15 minutes}$$

$$P(X = 0) = \frac{e^{-1} \cdot 1^0}{0!} = e^{-1} \approx 0.3679$$

**Answer:** $P(X = 0) \approx 0.3679$

**(iii) More than 4 calls in 90 minutes:** Rescale the rate:

$$\lambda_{90} = 4 \times \frac{90}{60} = 6 \text{ calls per 90 minutes}$$

$P(X > 4) = 1 - P(X \le 4) = 1 - \sum_{x=0}^{4} \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 6^x}{x!}$

Compute each term:

$$P(X = 0) = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 1}{1} = 0.002479$$

$$P(X = 1) = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 6}{1} = 0.014873$$

$$P(X = 2) = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 36}{2} = 0.044618$$

$$P(X = 3) = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 216}{6} = 0.089235$$

$$P(X = 4) = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot 1296}{24} = 0.133853$$

Sum:

$$P(X \le 4) = 0.002479 + 0.014873 + 0.044618 + 0.089235 + 0.133853 = 0.285058$$

Therefore:

$$P(X > 4) = 1 - 0.285058 = 0.714942$$

**Answer:** $P(X > 4) \approx 0.7149$

---

**Solution to (b): Binomial converges to Poisson.**

Start with the Binomial PMF:

$$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} p^x (1-p)^{n-x}$$

Substitute $p = \lambda/n$ (so $q = 1 - \lambda/n$):

$$P(X = x) = \binom{n}{x} \left(\frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^x \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}$$

Expand the binomial coefficient:

$$P(X = x) = \frac{n!}{x! (n-x)!} \cdot \frac{\lambda^x}{n^x} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}$$

Rewrite the factorial term:

$$P(X = x) = \frac{n(n-1)(n-2)\cdots(n-x+1)}{x!} \cdot \frac{\lambda^x}{n^x} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x}$$

Now take $\lim_{n \to \infty}$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{n(n-1)\cdots(n-x+1)}{n^x} = 1 \quad \text{(since each factor } \to 1\text{)}$$

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{n-x} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^n \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\lambda}{n}\right)^{-x} = e^{-\lambda} \cdot 1 = e^{-\lambda}$$

Combining:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} P(X = x) = \frac{\lambda^x}{x!} \cdot e^{-\lambda} = \frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$$

**Final Answer:** This is exactly the Poisson PMF. Hence the Binomial distribution converges to the Poisson distribution under the stated limiting conditions.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to rescale $\lambda$ for Poisson:** In Question B(a)(ii) and (iii), students often use $\lambda = 4$ for a 15-minute or 90-minute interval. The rate MUST be rescaled: $\lambda_{\text{new}} = \lambda_{\text{old}} \times \frac{\text{new interval}}{\text{old interval}}$. Failure to do this is a **direct 2-3 mark deduction**.
> 
> 2. **Confusing $P(X \le a)$ with $P(X < a)$:** $P(X \le a)$ includes $a$, $P(X < a)$ excludes it. Off-by-one errors are heavily penalized.
> 
> 3. **Using Binomial formula for Poisson or vice versa:** Check whether the problem gives $n$ and $p$ (Binomial) or just a rate (Poisson). A 14-mark question with wrong distribution = 0 marks for the calculation part.
> 
> 4. **Computational errors in $\binom{n}{x}$:** Always show the expansion $\binom{n}{x} = \frac{n!}{x!(n-x)!}$ and the intermediate arithmetic. Examiners give **partial credit** for correct method even if the final answer is wrong.
> 
> 5. **Independence assumption:** When applying $\text{Var}(X) = npq$, you MUST mention that the trials are independent. This is a frequently tested conceptual point.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting $e^{-\lambda}$:** Many students drop the exponential constant in the Poisson PMF. It is mandatory.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Binomial Distribution $B(n, p)$:** Counts successes in $n$ fixed independent trials with constant success probability $p$. PMF: $\binom{n}{x} p^x q^{n-x}$. Mean $= np$, Variance $= npq$.
- **Poisson Distribution $P(\lambda)$:** Counts events in a fixed interval with constant average rate $\lambda$. PMF: $\frac{e^{-\lambda} \lambda^x}{x!}$. Mean $= \lambda$, Variance $= \lambda$.
- **Key Identity for Poisson:** Mean = Variance (equidispersion). Use this as a quick sanity check.
- **Rescaling Rule for Poisson:** $\lambda_{\text{new}} = \lambda_{\text{old}} \times \frac{\text{new interval length}}{\text{old interval length}}$.
- **Complement Rule:** $P(X \ge 1) = 1 - P(X = 0)$ — fastest way to compute "at least one" probabilities.
- **Poisson Approximation to Binomial:** Valid when $n \ge 20$ and $p \le 0.05$. Use $\lambda = np$.
- **Binomial Approximation to Poisson:** Valid when $\lambda$ is large (typically $\lambda \ge 10$) and we want to use normal approximation. Not directly in Module 2 scope but useful conceptually.
- **Bernoulli Trial:** The atom of the Binomial. $X \sim B(1, p)$ is Bernoulli.
- **MGF of Binomial:** $(q + pe^t)^n$. MGF of Poisson: $e^{\lambda(e^t - 1)}$.
- **MGF Property:** $M'(0) = E[X]$ and $M''(0) = E[X^2]$. $\text{Var}(X) = M''(0) - [M'(0)]^2$.
- **Cumulative Sum Rule:** $P(a \le X \le b) = \sum_{x=a}^{b} P(X = x)$ for both distributions.
- **KTU Frequently Tested:** Mean and variance derivation, Poisson approximation, parameter rescaling, and applications to call centers / quality control.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuous Probability Distribution: Uniform, Normal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT632 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Continuous Probability Distribution: Uniform, Normal # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Continuous Probability Distribution? A **Continuous Probability Distribution** is a probability distribution that describes the probabilities of a continuous random variable — a variable that can take any real value within a given interval (often an infinite set of uncountable values). Unlike discrete distributions, the probability of any single exact value is mathematically **zero**; instead, we calculate probabilities over **intervals** of values. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A continuous random variable $X$ is a function $X: \Omega \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$ such that the cumulative distribution function (CDF) $F_X(x) = P(X \leq x)$ is continuous. Its behavior is described by a **Probability Density Function (PDF)** $f(x)$ where $f(x) \geq 0$ and $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$. ### Two Fundamental Properties (Must Remember) 1. **Non-negativity:** $f(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$ 2. **Total Area = 1:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$ The probability that $X$ lies in the interval $[a, b]$ is given by: $$P(a \leq X \leq b) = \int_{a}^{b} f(x) \, dx$$ > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Box — "The Water Under the Curve"** > Imagine a glass tank filled with water. The total water is **1 litre** (representing 100% probability). The PDF curve $f(x)$ is the *shape of the water surface*. Probability is the *amount of water* between two vertical lines drawn at $x = a$ and $x = b$. You cannot ask "how much water sits exactly at $x = 5$?" — that is an infinitesimally thin slice, equal to **zero**. You can only ask "how much water lies between $x = 5$ and $x = 10$?" --- ## 1.2 The Uniform Distribution (Continuous) ### Formal Definition A continuous random variable $X$ follows a **Uniform Distribution** on the interval $[a, b]$ (where $a < b$) if its probability density function is **constant** over the entire interval and zero elsewhere. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Uniform Distribution (Rectangular Distribution):** $X \sim U(a, b)$ > $$f(x) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{b - a}, & a \leq x \leq b \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$ The CDF is given by: $$F(x) = \begin{cases} 0, & x < a \\ \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}, & a \leq x \leq b \\ 1, & x > b \end{cases}$$ ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinner Game" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Intuition:** Picture a perfectly fair circular spinner divided into 100 equal slices numbered 0 to 100. Every angle is *equally likely* to be the stopping point. There is no "favorite" angle. This is the **uniform distribution in action** — *no value is more probable than any other value in the allowed range*. In business analytics, the uniform distribution models scenarios like: > - The waiting time for a randomly arriving bus (within a known window) > - The rounded-off decimal part of a random transaction amount > - Random number generation in simulations (Monte Carlo) ### Expected Value and Variance (Key Metrics) - **Mean (Expected Value):** $E(X) = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$ — the *midpoint* of the interval - **Variance:** $\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{(b - a)^2}{12}$ - **Standard Deviation:** $\sigma = \dfrac{b - a}{\sqrt{12}} = \dfrac{b - a}{2\sqrt{3}}$ --- ## 1.3 The Normal Distribution (Gaussian Distribution) ### Formal Definition A continuous random variable $X$ follows a **Normal Distribution** with parameters $\mu$ (mean) and $\sigma^2$ (variance), denoted $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, if its PDF is: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Normal Distribution PDF:** $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ > $$f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}\right)^2}, \quad -\infty < x < \infty$$ Where: - $\mu$ = **mean** of the distribution (location parameter) - $\sigma^2$ = **variance** of the distribution (spread parameter) - $\sigma$ = **standard deviation** - $\pi \approx 3.14159$ and $e \approx 2.71828$ are universal mathematical constants - The constant $1 / (\sigma\sqrt{2\pi})$ is the **normalization constant** that ensures total area equals 1 ### Conceptual Analogy — "The Bell Curve of Life" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Intuition:** The normal distribution is the most important distribution in all of statistics — sometimes called the **"Bell Curve"** because of its symmetric, bell-like shape. Think of measuring the **heights of 10,000 adult males in Kerala**. Most men cluster around the average height (~170 cm). Very short men (140 cm) and very tall men (200 cm) are rare, but not impossible. This natural clustering around the average — driven by the **Central Limit Theorem** — is what produces the bell shape. In business analytics: > - Stock market daily returns > - Manufacturing tolerances (weight of a 1 kg rice packet) > - IQ scores, exam scores > - Measurement errors in scientific experiments > - Customer purchase amounts (often log-normal, but normal approximations work) ### The Standard Normal Distribution A special case where $\mu = 0$ and $\sigma = 1$, denoted $Z \sim N(0, 1)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Normal PDF:** $f(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-z^2 / 2}, \quad -\infty < z < \infty$ Any normal variable can be **standardized** using the Z-transformation: $$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$ This allows us to use pre-computed **Z-tables** for probability calculations — a vital skill for KTU exams. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why These Two Distributions Matter (KTU 2024 Perspective) Business Analytics relies heavily on probability theory because every dataset has *uncertainty*. The **Uniform Distribution** serves as the foundation of **random sampling and Monte Carlo simulations**, while the **Normal Distribution** is the cornerstone of **inferential statistics, hypothesis testing, regression, and Six Sigma quality control**. --- ## 2.2 Properties of the Uniform Distribution — Detailed Logical Steps ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. **PDF is constant:** Since every value in $[a, b]$ is equally likely, the height of the curve must be the same. Call this constant $c$. 2. **Apply the area rule:** The total area under the curve must equal 1. $$\int_{a}^{b} c \, dx = c(b - a) = 1 \implies c = \frac{1}{b - a}$$ 3. **CDF construction:** The CDF $F(x) = P(X \leq x)$ gives the area from $a$ to $x$: $$F(x) = \int_{a}^{x} \frac{1}{b - a} \, dt = \frac{x - a}{b - a}$$ 4. **Mean intuition:** Since the distribution is symmetric, the average must be the **midpoint** $\frac{a + b}{2}$. 5. **Variance intuition:** The spread depends on the *width* of the interval. A wider interval $(b - a)$ produces greater variance, proportional to the *square* of the width (divided by 12, a constant that arises from the integration). ### Real-World Utility in Engineering & Business - **Operations Research:** Modeling uniformly distributed demand lead times - **Quality Control:** Random sampling of items from a production line where every item is equally likely to be picked - **Cryptography & Simulations:** Pseudo-random number generation (e.g., `rand()` in Excel follows uniform logic) - **Network Engineering:** Modeling random packet arrival times within a polling window --- ## 2.3 Properties of the Normal Distribution — Detailed Logical Steps ### Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Symmetry around mean:** $f(\mu + k) = f(\mu - k)$ for all $k$. Hence, the mean = median = mode = $\mu$. 2. **Inflection points:** The curve changes concavity at $x = \mu - \sigma$ and $x = \mu + \sigma$. 3. **Asymptotic tails:** The PDF approaches zero but never reaches it as $x \to \pm\infty$. 4. **Area below mean:** Exactly **50%** of the area lies below $\mu$ (due to symmetry). 5. **The "68-95-99.7" Empirical Rule:** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Empirical Rule (Three Sigma Rule):** > - **68.27%** of data lies within $\mu \pm 1\sigma$ > - **95.45%** of data lies within $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ > - **99.73%** of data lies within $\mu \pm 3\sigma$ 6. **The Central Limit Theorem (CLT) Connection:** The sum (or average) of $n$ independent, identically distributed random variables approaches a normal distribution as $n \to \infty$, *regardless* of the original distribution. ### Real-World Utility in Business Analytics - **Stock Returns:** Daily log-returns of stocks approximately follow $N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ — used in Value-at-Risk (VaR) calculations - **Process Control (Six Sigma):** Manufacturing defects per million opportunities are calculated assuming normality - **Customer Behavior:** Average purchase amounts in large samples tend to be normal (CLT) - **Risk Management:** Insurance claim sizes (after log-transformation) - **Machine Learning:** Gaussian Naive Bayes classifier, Gaussian kernels in SVMs --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Notes / Units | |---|---------|---------|----------------| | 1 | Uniform PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{b - a}$ for $a \leq x \leq b$ | Height of rectangle; must equal 1 only when $a \leq x \leq b$ | | 2 | Uniform CDF | $F(x) = \dfrac{x - a}{b - a}$ | Linear ramp from 0 to 1 on $[a, b]$ | | 3 | Uniform Mean | $E(X) = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$ | Midpoint of interval | | 4 | Uniform Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{(b - a)^2}{12}$ | Squared units of $X$ | | 5 | Uniform Std. Dev. | $\sigma = \dfrac{b - a}{2\sqrt{3}}$ | Square root of variance | | 6 | Normal PDF | $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}}$ | Bell-shaped curve | | 7 | Normal Mean | $E(X) = \mu$ | Also median and mode | | 8 | Normal Variance | $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$ | Squared units of $X$ | | 9 | Standard Normal PDF | $f(z) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-z^2/2}$ | Special case: $\mu = 0, \sigma = 1$ | | 10 | Z-Transformation | $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Converts $X$ to $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ | | 11 | 68% Interval | $[\mu - \sigma, \; \mu + \sigma]$ | Empirical rule | | 12 | 95% Interval | $[\mu - 2\sigma, \; \mu + 2\sigma]$ | Empirical rule | | 13 | 99.7% Interval | $[\mu - 3\sigma, \; \mu + 3\sigma]$ | Empirical rule | | 14 | Linear Transformation | If $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then $aX + b \sim N(a\mu + b, \; a^2\sigma^2)$ | Variance scales by $a^2$ | | 15 | Sum of Normals | If $X_1 \sim N(\mu_1, \sigma_1^2)$ and $X_2 \sim N(\mu_2, \sigma_2^2)$ are independent, then $X_1 + X_2 \sim N(\mu_1 + \mu_2, \; \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2)$ | Variances ADD, means ADD | > [!NOTE] > **Critical LaTeX Reminder for Students:** The vertical pipe symbol $\vert$ is used for absolute value, e.g., $\vert x - \mu \vert$. Do **NOT** confuse it with conditional probability $P(A \mid B)$. --- ## 2.5 How to Solve KTU-Style Problems — A Framework ### For Uniform Distribution Problems: 1. Identify the interval $[a, b]$ from the problem statement. 2. Compute $P(a \leq X \leq b) = (b - a) \times \dfrac{1}{b - a} = 1$ (sanity check). 3. For a sub-interval $[c, d] \subset [a, b]$: $P(c \leq X \leq d) = \dfrac{d - c}{b - a}$. 4. Compute $E(X)$ and $\text{Var}(X)$ using the formulas. ### For Normal Distribution Problems: 1. Identify $\mu$ and $\sigma$ (or $\sigma^2$). 2. Standardize: $Z = \dfrac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$. 3. Look up $P(Z \leq z)$ from the Z-table. 4. Apply the empirical rule or use Z-tables for probabilities. 5. **Always draw the bell curve** in your answer script and *shade* the required area. Examiners reward this. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Mean of the Uniform Distribution We compute $E(X) = \int_{a}^{b} x \cdot f(x) \, dx = \int_{a}^{b} x \cdot \dfrac{1}{b - a} \, dx$. $$ \begin{aligned} E(X) &= \frac{1}{b - a} \int_{a}^{b} x \, dx \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{b - a} \left[ \frac{x^2}{2} \right]_{a}^{b} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{b - a} \cdot \left( \frac{b^2}{2} - \frac{a^2}{2} \right) \\[6pt] &= \frac{b^2 - a^2}{2(b - a)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(b - a)(b + a)}{2(b - a)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{a + b}{2} \end{aligned} $$ **Conversion logic:** The integral of $x$ is $x^2 / 2$. We evaluate at the limits $b$ and $a$, take the difference, factor the numerator as a difference of squares, and cancel the common factor $(b - a)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result:** $E(X) = \dfrac{a + b}{2}$ — the midpoint, exactly as intuition suggests. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Variance of the Uniform Distribution We use the formula $\text{Var}(X) = E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2$. ### Step A — Compute $E(X^2)$: $$ \begin{aligned} E(X^2) &= \int_{a}^{b} x^2 \cdot \frac{1}{b - a} \, dx \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{b - a} \left[ \frac{x^3}{3} \right]_{a}^{b} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{b - a} \cdot \left( \frac{b^3}{3} - \frac{a^3}{3} \right) \\[6pt] &= \frac{b^3 - a^3}{3(b - a)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step B — Factor the difference of cubes: Using $b^3 - a^3 = (b - a)(b^2 + ab + a^2)$: $$ \begin{aligned} E(X^2) &= \frac{(b - a)(b^2 + ab + a^2)}{3(b - a)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{b^2 + ab + a^2}{3} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step C — Compute the variance: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Var}(X) &= E(X^2) - [E(X)]^2 \\[6pt] &= \frac{b^2 + ab + a^2}{3} - \left( \frac{a + b}{2} \right)^2 \\[6pt] &= \frac{b^2 + ab + a^2}{3} - \frac{a^2 + 2ab + b^2}{4} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step D — Common denominator (LCM of 3 and 4 is 12): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Var}(X) &= \frac{4(b^2 + ab + a^2) - 3(a^2 + 2ab + b^2)}{12} \\[6pt] &= \frac{4b^2 + 4ab + 4a^2 - 3a^2 - 6ab - 3b^2}{12} \\[6pt] &= \frac{b^2 - 2ab + a^2}{12} \\[6pt] &= \frac{(b - a)^2}{12} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result:** $\text{Var}(X) = \dfrac{(b - a)^2}{12}$ — derived rigorously. --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Mean and Variance of the Normal Distribution The full analytical proof that the normal PDF integrates to 1 and yields $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ involves advanced calculus (Gaussian integrals, completing the square). For KTU board exam purposes, students should know the *result* and the *standardization* proof. The Gaussian integral is: $$ \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-x^2} \, dx = \sqrt{\pi} $$ Applying the substitution $u = (x - \mu) / \sigma$ in the normalization integral: $$ \begin{aligned} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}} \, dx &= \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2 / 2} \cdot \sigma \, du \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-u^2 / 2} \, du \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \cdot \sqrt{2\pi} = 1 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Board Exam Note:** The full derivation of $E(X) = \mu$ and $\text{Var}(X) = \sigma^2$ is a 3-mark concept, but examiners typically accept stating the result with the symmetry argument. Focus on **applying** the formulas to numerical problems. --- ## 3.4 Worked Example 1: Uniform Distribution **Problem:** The arrival time of a metro train at a station is uniformly distributed between 8:00 AM and 8:20 AM. Find: 1. The probability density function $f(x)$. 2. The probability that the train arrives between 8:05 and 8:10 AM. 3. The mean and standard deviation of the arrival time. ### Solution: **Part 1 — PDF:** Here $a = 0$ minutes and $b = 20$ minutes (measuring from 8:00 AM). $$ f(x) = \frac{1}{20 - 0} = \frac{1}{20} = 0.05 \quad \text{for } 0 \leq x \leq 20 $$ **Part 2 — Probability:** We need $P(5 \leq X \leq 10)$. $$ P(5 \leq X \leq 10) = \int_{5}^{10} \frac{1}{20} \, dx = \frac{1}{20} \cdot (10 - 5) = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 = 25\% $$ **Part 3 — Mean and Standard Deviation:** $$ E(X) = \frac{a + b}{2} = \frac{0 + 20}{2} = 10 \text{ minutes (i.e., 8:10 AM)} $$ $$ \text{Var}(X) = \frac{(b - a)^2}{12} = \frac{(20 - 0)^2}{12} = \frac{400}{12} \approx 33.33 \text{ min}^2 $$ $$ \sigma = \sqrt{33.33} \approx 5.77 \text{ minutes} $$ --- ## 3.5 Worked Example 2: Normal Distribution **Problem:** The marks scored by B.Tech students in a Business Analytics exam are normally distributed with mean $\mu = 65$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 10$. Find: 1. The probability that a randomly selected student scored between 55 and 75. 2. The probability that a student scored more than 85. 3. The cut-off mark for the top 10% of students. ### Solution: **Part 1 — $P(55 \leq X \leq 75)$:** Standardize the limits. $$ z_1 = \frac{55 - 65}{10} = -1.00, \quad z_2 = \frac{75 - 65}{10} = 1.00 $$ From the Z-table (or empirical rule): $P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = 0.6827$ (the 68% rule). $$ P(55 \leq X \leq 75) = 0.6827 \approx 68.27\% $$ **Part 2 — $P(X > 85)$:** $$ z = \frac{85 - 65}{10} = 2.00 $$ By symmetry, $P(Z > 2) = P(Z < -2)$. From Z-table: $P(Z < 2) = 0.9772$, so $P(Z > 2) = 1 - 0.9772 = 0.0228 \approx 2.28\%$. **Part 3 — Top 10% cut-off:** We need the value $x$ such that $P(X > x) = 0.10$, i.e., $P(X \leq x) = 0.90$. From the Z-table, the z-value corresponding to a cumulative probability of 0.90 is $z = 1.28$. $$ x = \mu + z \cdot \sigma = 65 + 1.28 \times 10 = 65 + 12.8 = 77.8 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Answer:** Students scoring above **77.8 marks** fall in the top 10% of the class. --- ## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix | Problem Type | Standardization Required? | Tool Used | KTU Mark Weightage | |---|---|---|---| | Find $P(a \leq X \leq b)$ for $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ | Yes — convert to Z | Z-table or empirical rule | 3–5 marks | | Find $X$ given a probability | Yes — reverse lookup | Z-table inverse | 3–5 marks | | Uniform $P(c \leq X \leq d)$ | Not needed | $(d - c)/(b - a)$ | 2–3 marks | | Compute $E(X)$, $\text{Var}(X)$ | Not needed | Direct formulas | 2–3 marks | | Linear combinations of normals | Not needed | Means add, variances add | 5–7 marks | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Continuous Probability Distributions The following Mermaid block-diagram maps how the **Uniform** and **Normal** distributions fit into the broader landscape of continuous probability theory, following the KTU 2024 syllabus structure. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Continuous Probability Distributions] --> B[Uniform Distribution] A --> C[Normal Distribution] A --> D[Exponential Distribution] A --> E[Other Continuous Dists] B --> B1["Parameters: a and b
Interval endpoints"] B --> B2["PDF: 1 over b minus a
for a to x to b"] B --> B3["Mean: midpoint of a and b
Variance: squared width over 12"] B --> B4["Application: Monte Carlo
Random Sampling"] C --> C1["Parameters: mu and sigma squared
Mean and Variance"] C --> C2["Bell Shaped Symmetric PDF
Gaussian Curve"] C --> C3["Special Case: Standard Normal
Mean zero, Std Dev one"] C --> C4["68 95 99.7 Empirical Rule
Three Sigma Rule"] C --> C5["Central Limit Theorem
Foundation"] C3 --> C3a["Z Transformation
X minus mu over sigma"] C3 --> C3b["Z Table Lookup
Inverse Probability"] D --> D1["Memoryless Property
Waiting Times"] C5 --> C5a["Sum of n iid random variables
approaches Normal"] C5 --> C5b["Used in Hypothesis Testing
Confidence Intervals"] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff style B fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C3 fill:#e06666,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style C5 fill:#7f6000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Reading Guide:** The diagram flows from the broad concept (top) to specific applications (bottom). The Normal Distribution branch (in red) is the most heavily weighted topic in KTU 2024 examinations. --- ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Solving a Normal Distribution Problem The following flow chart depicts the **decision procedure** a student must follow when solving a typical KTU 2024 Board Exam problem on Normal Distribution. ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Read the Problem Statement] --> P1[Identify the Type of Question] P1 --> Q1[Find Probability for Given Value of X] P1 --> Q2[Find X for Given Probability] P1 --> Q3[Compute Mean or Variance] Q1 --> S1[Step 1: Identify mu and sigma] S1 --> S2[Step 2: Compute Z equals X minus mu over sigma] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Look up Z in Standard Normal Table] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply area rules for complement or symmetry] S4 --> End1[Final Probability Answer] Q2 --> T1[Step 1: Identify the required cumulative probability p] T1 --> T2[Step 2: Find the z value such that Phi of z equals p] T2 --> T3[Step 3: Solve x equals mu plus z times sigma] T3 --> End2[Final Cut off Value] Q3 --> U1[Step 1: Read mu and sigma from problem] U1 --> U2[Step 2: If sigma given, square it for variance] U2 --> U3[Step 3: If only mu given, sigma equals 1] U3 --> End3[Final Mean and Variance] style Start fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style P1 fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style End1 fill:#548235,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style End2 fill:#548235,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style End3 fill:#548235,stroke:#fff,color:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students who **draw such decision flowcharts in rough work** before attempting the main answer are statistically more likely to score full marks. It prevents confusion between "find $X$" vs "find probability" questions. --- ## 4.3 Visual Representation: The Normal Curve with Key Areas The normal distribution is symmetric and bell-shaped. The diagram below shows the three "sigma bands" that the empirical rule refers to. ```mermaid flowchart LR L[Tail minus infinity] --> M1[mu minus 3 sigma] M1 --> M2[mu minus 2 sigma] M2 --> M3[mu minus 1 sigma] M3 --> C[mu: Peak of the Bell] C --> P1[mu plus 1 sigma] P1 --> P2[mu plus 2 sigma] P2 --> P3[mu plus 3 sigma] P3 --> R[Tail plus infinity] C --> Note1["50 percent of area
lies below the mean"] M3 --> Note2["68.27 percent within
plus or minus 1 sigma"] M2 --> Note3["95.45 percent within
plus or minus 2 sigma"] M1 --> Note4["99.73 percent within
plus or minus 3 sigma"] style C fill:#c00000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff style M3 fill:#e8a4a4,stroke:#fff style P1 fill:#e8a4a4,stroke:#fff style M2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#fff style P2 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#fff style M1 fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#fff style P3 fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#fff ``` > [!NOTE] > **Pedagogical Insight:** The red center represents the mean (highest point). Each successive band outward captures progressively more of the total probability, ultimately covering **99.73%** within $\pm 3\sigma$. This is why manufacturing quality control uses the "**three-sigma limit**" — anything outside is treated as a defect. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions test *definition recall* and *conceptual understanding*. Answers should be 3–5 sentences, supported by the relevant formula. ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define a continuous probability distribution. State any two properties of the probability density function of a continuous random variable.** **Model Answer:** A continuous probability distribution describes the probabilities of a continuous random variable — a variable that can take any real value within a given range. It is characterized by a **Probability Density Function (PDF)** $f(x)$, where probability is computed as the area under the curve over an interval. **Two properties of the PDF:** 1. **Non-negativity:** $f(x) \geq 0$ for all $x \in \mathbb{R}$. 2. **Total area equals unity:** $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(x) \, dx = 1$. Additionally, the probability of any single point is zero: $P(X = c) = 0$ for any constant $c$. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the significance of the standard normal distribution in statistical analysis. How is a normal random variable converted to a standard normal variate?** **Model Answer:** The **Standard Normal Distribution** $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ is the special case of the normal distribution with mean $\mu = 0$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 1$. Its significance lies in the fact that: 1. Probabilities for *any* normal distribution can be computed using a **single universal Z-table**, eliminating the need for infinite tables. 2. It forms the foundation of **hypothesis testing** and **confidence interval estimation**. **Conversion formula (Z-transformation):** $$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$ This transformation is linear and preserves the area under the curve. For example, if $X \sim N(100, 16)$ (mean 100, variance 16), then $X = 80$ corresponds to $Z = (80 - 100)/4 = -5$. --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pattern Note:** Part B Module-2 questions carry **14 marks** and provide internal choice. Each sub-part (a) and (b) carries 7 marks. Solutions must be step-by-step with clearly labeled diagrams where applicable. --- ### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **(a)** The time (in minutes) taken for a customer service representative to resolve a complaint is uniformly distributed between 5 and 15 minutes. Find: 1. The probability density function. 2. The mean and variance of the resolution time. 3. The probability that a randomly selected call takes more than 12 minutes. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** The daily sales (in ₹ thousands) of a retail store follow a normal distribution with mean $\mu = 50$ and standard deviation $\sigma = 8$. Find: 1. The probability that sales on a given day lie between ₹42,000 and ₹58,000. 2. The probability that sales exceed ₹66,000. 3. The sales level such that only 5% of days have sales below it. **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) Solution — Uniform Distribution **Given:** $a = 5$, $b = 15$. **1. PDF:** $$ f(x) = \frac{1}{b - a} = \frac{1}{15 - 5} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 \quad \text{for } 5 \leq x \leq 15 $$ **2. Mean and Variance:** $$ E(X) = \frac{a + b}{2} = \frac{5 + 15}{2} = 10 \text{ minutes} $$ $$ \text{Var}(X) = \frac{(b - a)^2}{12} = \frac{(15 - 5)^2}{12} = \frac{100}{12} \approx 8.33 \text{ min}^2 $$ **3. Probability $P(X > 12)$:** $$ P(X > 12) = \int_{12}^{15} \frac{1}{10} \, dx = \frac{1}{10} \cdot (15 - 12) = \frac{3}{10} = 0.30 = 30\% $$ #### Part (b) Solution — Normal Distribution **Given:** $\mu = 50$, $\sigma = 8$ (sales in ₹ thousands). **1. Probability $P(42 \leq X \leq 58)$:** Standardize the limits: $$ z_1 = \frac{42 - 50}{8} = -1.00, \quad z_2 = \frac{58 - 50}{8} = 1.00 $$ By the empirical rule (68% rule), $P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = 0.6827$. $$ \boxed{P(42 \leq X \leq 58) = 0.6827 \approx 68.27\%} $$ **2. Probability $P(X > 66)$:** $$ z = \frac{66 - 50}{8} = 2.00 $$ By symmetry, $P(Z > 2) = 1 - P(Z \leq 2) = 1 - 0.9772 = 0.0228$. $$ \boxed{P(X > 66) = 0.0228 \approx 2.28\%} $$ **3. Sales level for the bottom 5%:** We need $x$ such that $P(X \leq x) = 0.05$. From the Z-table, the z-value for cumulative probability 0.05 is $z = -1.645$. $$ x = \mu + z \cdot \sigma = 50 + (-1.645)(8) = 50 - 13.16 = 36.84 $$ $$ \boxed{\text{Sales level} = ₹36{,}840} $$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **(a)** Define the normal distribution. State and explain the **empirical (68-95-99.7) rule** for a normal distribution with the help of a suitable diagram. Mention any **four real-world applications** of the normal distribution in business analytics. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** The weight of a packaged product (in grams) is normally distributed with mean $\mu = 500$ g and variance $\sigma^2 = 36$ g². Find: 1. The probability that a randomly selected packet weighs between 494 g and 512 g. 2. The probability that a packet weighs less than 488 g. 3. The maximum weight such that only 2.5% of packets exceed it. **[7 Marks]** --- ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) Solution — Definition + Empirical Rule A random variable $X$ follows a **Normal Distribution** with parameters $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ (denoted $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$) if its PDF is: $$ f(x) = \frac{1}{\sigma \sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-\frac{(x - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2}} $$ **Empirical Rule (Three-Sigma Rule):** The empirical rule states that for any normal distribution: - Approximately **68.27%** of observations lie within **one** standard deviation of the mean: $P(\mu - \sigma \leq X \leq \mu + \sigma) \approx 0.6827$. - Approximately **95.45%** of observations lie within **two** standard deviations: $P(\mu - 2\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 2\sigma) \approx 0.9545$. - Approximately **99.73%** of observations lie within **three** standard deviations: $P(\mu - 3\sigma \leq X \leq \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 0.9973$. **Diagram (Bell Curve with bands):** A bell-shaped curve centered at $\mu$ with the three bands shaded from inside out — innermost band covering 68.27%, next 95.45% (cumulative), and outermost 99.73%. **Four Real-World Applications in Business Analytics:** 1. **Stock Market Returns:** Daily log-returns of equity indices are approximately normal — used in Value-at-Risk (VaR) calculations. 2. **Quality Control (Six Sigma):** Manufacturing tolerances are set at $\pm 3\sigma$ limits, ensuring defect rates below 0.27%. 3. **Customer Spending Behavior:** Average transaction values in large retail samples approximate a normal distribution (via the Central Limit Theorem). 4. **Risk Management & Insurance:** Claim sizes (after log-transformation) and credit scores follow normal-like distributions, enabling premium calculations. #### Part (b) Solution — Normal Distribution Computation **Given:** $\mu = 500$ g, $\sigma^2 = 36 \implies \sigma = 6$ g. **1. Probability $P(494 \leq X \leq 512)$:** Standardize: $$ z_1 = \frac{494 - 500}{6} = -1.00, \quad z_2 = \frac{512 - 500}{6} = 2.00 $$ $$ P(-1 \leq Z \leq 2) = P(Z \leq 2) - P(Z \leq -1) = 0.9772 - 0.1587 = 0.8185 $$ $$ \boxed{P(494 \leq X \leq 512) = 0.8185 \approx 81.85\%} $$ **2. Probability $P(X < 488)$:** $$ z = \frac{488 - 500}{6} = -2.00 $$ By symmetry, $P(Z < -2) = P(Z > 2) = 1 - 0.9772 = 0.0228$. $$ \boxed{P(X < 488) = 0.0228 \approx 2.28\%} $$ **3. Maximum weight $x$ such that $P(X > x) = 0.025$:** We need $P(X \leq x) = 1 - 0.025 = 0.975$. From the Z-table, $z = 1.96$. $$ x = \mu + z \cdot \sigma = 500 + 1.96 \times 6 = 500 + 11.76 = 511.76 $$ $$ \boxed{x \approx 511.76 \text{ g}} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to standardize** — Many students directly plug $X$ values into the Z-table instead of computing $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$. This is an **instant 0** for that sub-part. > > 2. **Confusing variance with standard deviation** — When a problem says "standard deviation is 8", the variance is $8^2 = 64$, **not** 8. Always check whether the given value is $\sigma$ or $\sigma^2$. > > 3. **Symmetry misuse** — $P(Z < -1.5) = P(Z > 1.5)$ is correct. But $P(-1 \leq Z \leq 1) = 2 \times P(Z \leq 1) - 1$ is *also* correct — choose whichever is faster. Examiners want the *computation*, not a specific path. > > 4. **Uniform distribution: writing $f(x) = 1/(b-a)$ outside $[a,b]$** — The PDF is zero outside the interval. Students who write $f(x) = 0.1$ for all $x$ lose 1–2 marks. > > 5. **Not sketching the bell curve** — In 7-mark problems, always draw the curve, label $\mu$, $\sigma$, and shade the area. Examiners explicitly reward this. > > 6. **Unit mismatches** — If the mean is in ₹ thousands, keep it consistent. Converting back to ₹ at the end is **mandatory** for full marks. > > 7. **Rounding Z-values** — Do not round $z = 1.28$ to $z = 1.3$ prematurely. Keep precision until the final step. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Before the Exam** ### Uniform Distribution - ✅ Defined on an interval $[a, b]$ with constant PDF $f(x) = 1/(b-a)$. - ✅ CDF is a linear ramp: $F(x) = (x - a)/(b - a)$ on $[a, b]$. - ✅ Mean: $E(X) = (a + b)/2$ — the **midpoint**. - ✅ Variance: $\text{Var}(X) = (b - a)^2 / 12$. - ✅ Application: Random sampling, Monte Carlo, equally likely scenarios. ### Normal Distribution - ✅ Bell-shaped, symmetric about $\mu$, characterized by $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$. - ✅ PDF: $f(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} e^{-(x-\mu)^2 / (2\sigma^2)}$. - ✅ Standard normal: $Z \sim N(0, 1)$ with $\mu = 0$, $\sigma = 1$. - ✅ Z-transformation: $Z = (X - \mu)/\sigma$. - ✅ Empirical Rule: 68% within $1\sigma$, 95% within $2\sigma$, 99.7% within $3\sigma$. - ✅ Linear transformation: $aX + b \sim N(a\mu + b, \, a^2\sigma^2)$. - ✅ Sum of independent normals: variances **add**, means **add**. ### Problem-Solving Strategy - ✅ Identify the distribution type from the problem statement. - ✅ For Normal: **always standardize** before using the Z-table. - ✅ For Uniform: use the simple ratio $(d - c)/(b - a)$. - ✅ **Draw the bell curve** and shade the required area. - ✅ State assumptions clearly (e.g., "Given $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$..."). - ✅ Show every integration step for full marks on derivation questions. ### High-Yield Keywords for KTU Answers - "Continuous random variable" · "Probability density function" · "Cumulative distribution function" - "Standardization" · "Z-score" · "Empirical rule" · "Three-sigma limit" - "Central Limit Theorem" · "Bell curve" · "Symmetric distribution" - "Equally likely outcomes" · "Rectangular distribution" (synonym for Uniform) ### Numerical Constants to Memorize - $1/\sqrt{2\pi} \approx 0.3989$ — peak height of standard normal PDF - Z-table reference values: $P(Z \leq 1.96) = 0.975$, $P(Z \leq 2.58) = 0.995$ - $12$ in the uniform variance formula — **never** change this constant ### Common Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Treating continuous probability as $P(X = x)$ — it is always **zero** for continuous variables. - ❌ Confusing $\sigma$ and $\sigma^2$ in Z-transformation. - ❌ Forgetting to convert ₹ thousands back to ₹ in the final answer. - ❌ Using $\sigma$ as the variance — it is the **standard deviation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip:** The normal distribution is the **most frequently tested** topic in KTU Business Analytics Module 2. Master the Z-transformation, memorize the empirical rule, and practice at least 10 numerical problems before the exam. The uniform distribution typically appears in 2–3 mark sub-parts as a "warm-up" question.

topics.title: Statistical Inference: Hypothesis Testing Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT632 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Statistical Inference: Hypothesis Testing Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Statistical Hypothesis Testing** is a systematic, formal procedure used in statistical inference to make decisions about population parameters based on sample data. Under the KTU 2024 scheme, it is defined as a method of reasoning under uncertainty where we assume a default position (the null hypothesis) and use sample evidence to either reject or fail to reject that assumption, quantified by a probability value (the **p-value**) and compared against a pre-fixed threshold called the **Level of Significance (α)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):**
> *"Hypothesis testing is a two-step decision-making process that evaluates a claim about a population parameter by formulating two competing hypotheses and using sample data to determine which hypothesis is more strongly supported by the evidence, while controlling the probability of making an incorrect decision."*

---

### 1.2 Key Terminology (Syllabus-Weighted Vocabulary)

| Term | Formal Meaning | KTU Board Tip |
|------|----------------|---------------|
| **Hypothesis (H)** | A statement/assumption about a population parameter | Always a statement, never a question |
| **Null Hypothesis (H₀)** | The default/assumption of "no effect", "no change", or "no difference" | Always contains equality: `=`, `≤`, `≥` |
| **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁ or Hₐ)** | The research claim — the statement we want to prove | Contains inequality: `≠`, `<`, `>` |
| **Test Statistic** | A standardized value calculated from sample data | Z, t, χ², F are the four pillars |
| **Critical Value** | The threshold value from the sampling distribution | Depends on α and test type |
| **p-value** | Probability of obtaining the observed result, assuming H₀ is true | Smaller p → stronger evidence against H₀ |
| **Level of Significance (α)** | Pre-fixed maximum allowable probability of Type I error | Standard values: **0.05** (5%) or **0.01** (1%) |
| **Type I Error (α)** | Rejecting a TRUE null hypothesis (False Positive) | "False alarm" |
| **Type II Error (β)** | Failing to reject a FALSE null hypothesis (False Negative) | "Missed detection" |
| **Power of Test (1−β)** | Probability of correctly rejecting a false H₀ | Good tests have power **≥ 0.80** |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Courtroom Trial"

Imagine you are a **judge in a courtroom**. The fundamental philosophy of hypothesis testing mirrors the legal principle of *"innocent until proven guilty"*:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Courtroom Analogy**
> - **The Accused (Defendant)** = The **Null Hypothesis (H₀)**. We always *assume H₀ is true* (innocent) at the start.
> - **The Prosecutor** = The **Alternative Hypothesis (H₁)**. They must present *evidence* (sample data) to challenge H₀.
> - **The Jury** = The **Test Statistic**. They evaluate the strength of evidence.
> - **"Beyond reasonable doubt"** = The **Level of Significance (α)**. The threshold the evidence must cross.
> - **Convicting an innocent person** = **Type I Error (α)**.
> - **Letting a guilty person go free** = **Type II Error (β)**.

**Key Insight:** Just as a court never declares someone "proven innocent" (only "not guilty"), we never *prove* H₀ — we only *fail to reject* it.

---

### 1.4 Visual Summary of the Decision Matrix

| | **H₀ is TRUE (Reality)** | **H₀ is FALSE (Reality)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Fail to Reject H₀** | ✅ Correct Decision (Probability = 1 − α) | ❌ **Type II Error (β)** |
| **Reject H₀** | ❌ **Type I Error (α)** | ✅ Correct Decision (Probability = **Power** = 1 − β) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "failing to reject H₀" with "accepting H₀". These are **NOT** the same. Failing to reject simply means the evidence was *insufficient*, not that H₀ is proven true.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six Logical Steps of Hypothesis Testing (The KTU "Golden Steps")

The KTU board almost always awards **2–3 marks** for stating the steps correctly. Memorize the exact sequence:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Six Steps of Hypothesis Testing (Board-Favorite Question)**
>
> 1. **State the Hypotheses** — Formulate H₀ and H₁ in terms of the population parameter (μ, p, σ²).
> 2. **Select the Test Statistic** — Choose Z, t, χ², or F based on data type and sample size.
> 3. **Specify the Level of Significance (α)** — Usually **0.05**.
> 4. **Determine the Critical Value(s) and Decision Rule** — From the standard distribution table.
> 5. **Compute the Test Statistic from Sample Data** — Plug numbers into the formula.
> 6. **Make the Decision** — Compare the calculated value with the critical value (or compare p-value with α):
>    - If `|Calculated| > |Critical|` → **Reject H₀**
>    - If `|Calculated| ≤ |Critical|` → **Fail to Reject H₀**

---

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

**Step 1 — Why formulate H₀ and H₁?**
Because we need a *mathematical starting point*. H₀ must contain equality because it represents a precise, testable value (e.g., μ = 50). H₁ is its logical negation.

**Step 2 — Why choose a test statistic?**
Because raw sample means are not comparable across studies. The test statistic *standardizes* the difference between sample and hypothesized values, allowing us to use universal probability tables.

**Step 3 — Why pre-fix α?**
To prevent *data dredging* — the unethical practice of running tests repeatedly until you get a significant result. Fixing α *before* seeing data ensures objectivity.

**Step 4 — Why critical values?**
Critical values define the *rejection region* — values so extreme that they would be very unlikely if H₀ were true. They create a mathematical fence.

**Step 5 — Why compute?**
The actual numerical evidence must be quantified for comparison.

**Step 6 — Why compare?**
A decision rule must be objective and reproducible. Comparison with critical value (or p-value) provides this.

---

### 2.3 One-Tailed vs. Two-Tailed Tests

This is a **favourite 3-mark KTU question**.

| Feature | **Two-Tailed Test** | **One-Tailed Test (Right)** | **One-Tailed Test (Left)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **H₁ form** | H₁: μ ≠ μ₀ | H₁: μ > μ₀ | H₁: μ < μ₀ |
| **Rejection Region** | Both extremes | Upper tail only | Lower tail only |
| **α split** | α/2 on each side | α in upper tail | α in lower tail |
| **Use when** | Direction of effect is unknown | Expecting an *increase* | Expecting a *decrease* |
| **Critical Z (α=0.05)** | ±1.96 | +1.645 | −1.645 |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **high-frequency KTU board formulas**. Master the structure, not just the symbols.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Z-Test Statistic (Mean, σ known)** | $Z = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$ | $\bar{x}$ = sample mean, $\mu_0$ = hypothesized mean, $\sigma$ = population SD, $n$ = sample size | Large samples (n ≥ 30) with known σ |
| 2 | **t-Test Statistic (Mean, σ unknown)** | $t = \dfrac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{s / \sqrt{n}}$ | $s$ = sample SD, df = n − 1 | Small samples (n < 30) with unknown σ |
| 3 | **Z-Test for Proportion** | $Z = \dfrac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}}$ | $\hat{p}$ = sample proportion, $p_0$ = hypothesized proportion | Testing population proportion |
| 4 | **Chi-Square Test (Variance)** | $\chi^2 = \dfrac{(n-1)s^2}{\sigma_0^2}$ | $\sigma_0^2$ = hypothesized variance, df = n − 1 | Testing variance/homogeneity |
| 5 | **Standard Error (Mean)** | $SE = \dfrac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | — | Always in Z/t test |
| 6 | **Standard Error (Proportion)** | $SE_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{p_0(1-p_0)}{n}}$ | — | Proportion tests |
| 7 | **Type I Error (α)** | Pre-fixed by researcher | — | Rejecting true H₀ |
| 8 | **Type II Error (β)** | Depends on true parameter, n, α | — | Failing to reject false H₀ |
| 9 | **Power of Test** | $1 - \beta$ | — | Probability of correct rejection |
| 10 | **p-value Decision Rule** | If p ≤ α → Reject H₀; If p > α → Fail to Reject H₀ | — | Modern reporting standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** In exam answer sheets, always write the formula *first*, then substitute values, *then* compute. This guarantees **full marks for procedure** even if the final arithmetic has a small slip.

---

| Field | Application of Hypothesis Testing |

| **Manufacturing Quality Control** | Testing if the mean weight of a product matches the label (e.g., 500g packs) |
| **Software Engineering** | A/B testing of two website designs to check conversion rate differences |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Clinical trials to test if a new drug is more effective than placebo |
| **Telecommunications** | Testing if average call drop rate is below industry SLA threshold |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Z-Test Statistic Has the Form $Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}$

This derivation is often asked as a **5-mark derivation question**. We start from first principles using the **Central Limit Theorem (CLT)**.

**Starting Assumption:** The sample comes from a population with mean $\mu_0$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. We draw a random sample of size $n$.

**Step 1 — The Sampling Distribution of the Mean**

By the Central Limit Theorem, for large $n$, the sample mean $\bar{x}$ is approximately normally distributed:

$$\bar{x} \sim N\left(\mu_0, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right)$$

This means:
- The mean of $\bar{x}$ equals the population mean $\mu_0$.
- The standard deviation of $\bar{x}$ (called the **standard error**) is $\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$.

**Step 2 — Standardize Using the Z-Transformation**

To convert any normal variable $X \sim N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ to the standard normal $Z \sim N(0, 1)$, we use:

$$Z = \frac{X - \mu}{\sigma}$$

Applying this to $\bar{x}$:

$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation**

The numerator $\bar{x} - \mu_0$ is the *deviation of the sample mean from the hypothesized mean*. The denominator $\sigma/\sqrt{n}$ is the *natural unit of variability* for sample means. The ratio tells us **how many standard errors** our sample mean is away from $\mu_0$.

> If $|Z| < 1.96$ (for α = 0.05) → the sample is *consistent* with H₀.
> If $|Z| > 1.96$ → the sample is *too extreme* to be a chance fluctuation under H₀.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: One-Sample Z-Test (Full 14-Mark Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A biscuit manufacturing company claims that the average weight of its 500g packet is exactly 500g. A quality inspector randomly samples **64 packets** and finds the mean weight to be **495g** with a population standard deviation of **20g**. At a **5% level of significance**, test whether the company’s claim is valid.

---

**Step 1 — State the Hypotheses**

The claim is "mean = 500g" → this becomes H₀. We test against "mean ≠ 500g" → H₁.

$$H_0: \mu = 500 \text{ grams}$$
$$H_1: \mu \neq 500 \text{ grams}$$

This is a **two-tailed test** because the inspector has no prior direction (could be over- or under-filling).

**Step 2 — Select the Test Statistic**

Given: $n = 64 \geq 30$ (large sample), $\sigma = 20$ is known. Therefore, use the **Z-test**.

$$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}}$$

**Step 3 — Specify the Level of Significance**

$$\alpha = 0.05$$

**Step 4 — Determine Critical Value and Decision Rule**

For a two-tailed test at $\alpha = 0.05$, the critical Z-values are:

$$Z_{critical} = \pm 1.96$$

**Decision Rule:** Reject H₀ if $Z_{calculated} < -1.96$ OR $Z_{calculated} > +1.96$.

**Step 5 — Compute the Test Statistic**

Given values:
- $\bar{x} = 495$
- $\mu_0 = 500$
- $\sigma = 20$
- $n = 64$, so $\sqrt{n} = 8$

Standard Error:

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}} = \frac{20}{8} = 2.5$$

Test Statistic:

$$\begin{aligned}
Z_{calculated} &= \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma / \sqrt{n}} \\
&= \frac{495 - 500}{2.5} \\
&= \frac{-5}{2.5} \\
&= -2.00
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Decision**

Compare:
$$Z_{calculated} = -2.00 \quad \text{vs} \quad Z_{critical} = -1.96$$

Since $|-2.00| > |-1.96|$, the calculated value lies in the rejection region.

**Decision: Reject H₀** at the 5% level of significance.

**Step 7 — Conclusion (Business Interpretation)**

There is sufficient statistical evidence at the 5% significance level to conclude that the **average weight of the biscuit packet is NOT equal to 500g**. Specifically, the company appears to be **under-filling** the packets. The quality claim is not supported by the sample data.

---

### 3.3 p-value Approach (Modern Method)

For the same problem, the p-value for $Z = -2.00$ in a two-tailed test:

$$p\text{-value} = 2 \times P(Z < -2.00) = 2 \times 0.0228 = 0.0456$$

**Comparison:** $p = 0.0456 < \alpha = 0.05$ → **Reject H₀** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> Both methods give the **same conclusion** — they are mathematically equivalent. KTU accepts either. The p-value method is increasingly preferred in modern reporting because it provides the *exact* significance level.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Correct Test

This is a **3-mark decision question** that appears frequently:

| Condition | Test to Use | Formula Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Mean, σ known, n ≥ 30 | **Z-test** | Formula #1 |
| Mean, σ known, n < 30 | **Z-test** (CLT assumed) | Formula #1 |
| Mean, σ unknown, n ≥ 30 | **Z-test** (use s ≈ σ) | Formula #1 |
| Mean, σ unknown, n < 30 | **t-test** | Formula #2 |
| Proportion, np ≥ 5 and n(1−p) ≥ 5 | **Z-test for proportion** | Formula #3 |
| Variance test | **Chi-Square test** | Formula #4 |
| Comparing two means, independent samples | **Two-sample t-test** | $t = \frac{\bar{x}_1 - \bar{x}_2}{s_p\sqrt{\frac{1}{n_1}+\frac{1}{n_2}}}$ |
| Comparing two means, paired | **Paired t-test** | $t = \frac{\bar{d}}{s_d/\sqrt{n}}$ on differences |
| Comparing >2 means | **ANOVA (F-test)** | Not in Module 2 scope |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Hypothesis Testing Decision Flow (Mermaid Flowchart)

This is the **single most important diagram** for Module 2. Memorize this flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Research Question or Claim"] --> B["Step 1: Formulate H0 and H1"]
    B --> C["Step 2: Choose Test Statistic Z, t, chi-square, F"]
    C --> D["Step 3: Fix Level of Significance alpha = 0.05 or 0.01"]
    D --> E["Step 4: Determine Critical Value from Standard Table"]
    E --> F["Step 5: Compute Test Statistic from Sample Data"]
    F --> G{"Step 6a: Compare Calculated vs Critical Value"}
    G -->|"Calculated falls in Rejection Region"| H["REJECT H0"]
    G -->|"Calculated falls in Acceptance Region"| I["FAIL TO REJECT H0"]
    F --> J["Step 6b: Alternative Compute p-value"]
    J --> K{"p-value compared to alpha"}
    K -->|"p less than or equal to alpha"| H
    K -->|"p greater than alpha"| I
    H --> L["Conclusion: Strong evidence supports H1"]
    I --> M["Conclusion: Insufficient evidence against H0"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows from `A` (your research question) down to the final `L` or `M` (your conclusion). The *diamond shapes* (G, K) are **decision points** where the comparison happens.

---

### 4.2 The Hypothesis Testing Logic Map (Mermaid Block Diagram)

This diagram shows the *logical architecture* of how H₀ and H₁ relate to the two possible errors.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph REALITY["REALITY Population Truth"]
        R1["H0 is TRUE"]
        R2["H0 is FALSE"]
    end

    subgraph DECISION["OUR DECISION Based on Sample"]
        D1["Fail to Reject H0"]
        D2["Reject H0"]
    end

    R1 -->|"Correct Acceptance Probability 1 minus alpha"| D1
    R1 -->|"TYPE I ERROR alpha False Positive"| D2
    R2 -->|"TYPE II ERROR beta False Negative"| D1
    R2 -->|"CORRECT REJECTION POWER 1 minus beta"| D2
```

---

### 4.3 The Power-Error Trade-off Visualization (Conceptual Schematic)

This block diagram explains the **inverse relationship** between Type I and Type II errors.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Increase Sample Size n"] --> B["Smaller Standard Error SE"]
    B --> C["Higher Test Power 1 minus beta"]
    C --> D["Lower Type II Error beta"]

    E["Decrease Alpha Threshold"] --> F["Stricter Rejection Criterion"]
    F --> G["Lower Type I Error alpha"]
    G --> H["Higher Type II Error beta"]

    I["Increase Alpha Threshold"] --> J["Looser Rejection Criterion"]
    J --> K["Higher Type I Error alpha"]
    K --> L["Lower Type II Error beta"]

    style C fill:#d4f4d4
    style G fill:#f4d4d4
    style K fill:#f4d4d4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Takeaway from the Diagram:**
> 1. **Increasing n** ↑ Power without changing α — *the only free lunch*.
> 2. **Changing α** always creates a trade-off: lowering α ↓ Type I error but ↑ Type II error.

---

### 4.4 Critical Region Diagram (Two-Tailed Test)

This is the **visual signature** of a two-tailed test. KTU frequently asks students to *draw and label* this.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph BELLCURVE["Standard Normal Distribution Curve Z"]
        A1["Left Rejection Region area = alpha/2 = 0.025"] --> A2["Acceptance Region area = 0.95"]
        A2 --> A3["Right Rejection Region area = alpha/2 = 0.025"]
    end

    A1 -.->|"Z less than -1.96"| B1["REJECT H0"]
    A2 -.->|"Z between -1.96 and +1.96"| B2["FAIL TO REJECT H0"]
    A3 -.->|"Z greater than +1.96"| B3["REJECT H0"]

    style B1 fill:#ffcccc
    style B2 fill:#ccffcc
    style B3 fill:#ffcccc
```

> [!TIP]
> **Drawing Tip for Answer Sheets:** Sketch a bell curve, mark the mean at 0, and shade the two tails (left of −1.96 and right of +1.96) as the rejection region. Label the center shaded area as the acceptance region. **This single diagram is worth 2–3 marks** when properly drawn.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
