\text{Maximize } & Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j \\
\text{Subject to: } & \sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j = b_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m \\
& x_j \geq 0, \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, n \\
& b_i \geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Rules (Frequently tested for 3–7 marks in KTU):**

| Original Constraint Type | Conversion Action | New Variable Added |
|--------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| $a_{i1}x_1 + \dots + a_{in}x_n \leq b_i$ | Add **Slack Variable** $s_i$ | $s_i \geq 0$ |
| $a_{i1}x_1 + \dots + a_{in}x_n \geq b_i$ | Subtract **Surplus Variable** $s_i$, add **Artificial Variable** $A_i$ | $s_i, A_i \geq 0$ |
| $a_{i1}x_1 + \dots + a_{in}x_n = b_i$ | Add **Artificial Variable** $A_i$ | $A_i \geq 0$ |

**Canonical Form** (preferred for theoretical proofs):
$$
Z - c_1 x_1 - c_2 x_2 - \dots - c_n x_n = 0
$$
This embeds the objective function into the constraint system for matrix operations.

## 2.3 Fundamental Assumptions of LPP (KTU 2-Mark Favorite)

> [!NOTE]
> Every LPP model rests on four classical assumptions. Violating any one means LPP is *not* the correct tool.

- **Proportionality:** The contribution of $x_j$ to $Z$ and to each constraint is directly proportional to $x_j$.
- **Additivity:** The total value of $Z$ and resource usage is the **sum** of individual $x_j$ contributions (no interaction effects).
- **Determinism (Certainty):** All $c_j, a_{ij}, b_i$ are **known constants**, not random variables.
- **Continuity (Divisibility):** Decision variables can take any **real value** $\geq 0$ (for Integer Programming, this assumption is relaxed).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is the single most high-yield section for KTU ESE.** Every formula below has appeared (or is a direct variant of a question) in past university exams.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notation |
|---|---------|----------------|----------|
| 1 | Objective Function | $Z = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j x_j$ | $Z$ = objective value |
| 2 | Constraint | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i$ | $b_i$ = resource $i$ |
| 3 | Standard Form Embedding | $Z - \sum c_j x_j = 0$ | Used in Simplex tableau |
| 4 | Basic Variables | Number of basic variables $= m$ (no. of constraints) | $m$ constraints $\rightarrow m$ basics |
| 5 | Non-Basic Variables | Number of non-basic variables $= n - m$ | Set to $0$ in any BFS |
| 6 | Simplex Optimality (Max) | All $Z_j - c_j \geq 0$ | Stop iterating |
| 7 | Simplex Optimality (Min) | All $Z_j - c_j \leq 0$ | Stop iterating |
| 8 | Entering Variable (Max) | Most negative $Z_j - c_j$ column | $c_j - Z_j$ most positive |
| 9 | Leaving Variable | Min ratio $\frac{b_i}{a_{ij}}$ for $a_{ij} > 0$ | Smallest non-negative ratio |
| 10 | Pivot Element | Intersection of entering column & leaving row | Becomes 1, others become 0 |
| 11 | Simplex Multipliers | $\pi = c_B^T B^{-1}$ | Used in Revised Simplex |
| 12 | Revised Simplex Update | $x_B^{(k+1)} = B^{-1} b - \theta \cdot B^{-1} A_j$ | $\theta$ = min ratio |
| 13 | Shadow Price | $y_i^* = \pi_i$ (dual value of resource $i$) | Rate of $Z$ change per unit of $b_i$ |
| 14 | Reduced Cost | $\bar{c}_j = c_j - z_j$ (or $c_j - \pi^T A_j$) | Amount $c_j$ must improve to enter basis |
| 15 | Sensitivity Range for $b_i$ | $b_i^{\text{new}} \in [b_i + \Delta b_i^{\min},\; b_i + \Delta b_i^{\max}]$ | Where current basis remains optimal |
| 16 | Sensitivity Range for $c_j$ | $\bar{c}_j \leq 0$ (Max) or $\bar{c}_j \geq 0$ (Min) | For non-basic $x_j$ |

## 2.5 Engineering & Business Real-World Utility

LPP is the **workhorse of Operations Research** and is deployed across every engineering and business discipline:

- **Manufacturing:** Product-mix optimization, blending problems (petroleum, cement, diet planning).
- **Logistics & Supply Chain:** Transportation problem, vehicle routing, warehouse location.
- **Finance:** Portfolio optimization (Markowitz model), capital budgeting.
- **Marketing:** Media mix allocation, sales territory planning.
- **HR:** Manpower scheduling, shift assignment.
- **Telecommunications:** Network flow optimization, bandwidth allocation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** If a question says "analyse the trade-off" or "recommend the best course of action" — it is screaming for a **Prescriptive Analytics / Optimization** approach, and LPP is the default starter model.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains the **complete analytical engine** of LPP — Formulation, Graphical Solution, Simplex Method, Revised Simplex, and Sensitivity Analysis. Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly.

---

## 3.1 LP Formulation — The Art of Translating Words into Math

> [!NOTE]
> **The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes formulation skills.** Marks are awarded for correctly identifying decision variables, objective function, and constraints from a verbal case study.

### 3.1.1 General Formulation Procedure (5 Steps)

**Step 1 — Identify the Decision Variables.**
Let $x_j$ represent the quantity of activity $j$ to be determined (e.g., units of product A, hours of machine 1).

**Step 2 — Identify the Objective.**
Read the problem carefully to spot words like *maximize* (profit, revenue, output) or *minimize* (cost, time, waste).

**Step 3 — Identify the Constraints.**
Look for words like *at most*, *at least*, *cannot exceed*, *must produce*, *limited to*. Each maps to a $\leq$, $\geq$, or $=$ constraint.

**Step 4 — Identify the Parameters.**
The numerical values of $c_j$ (unit profit), $a_{ij}$ (resource use), and $b_i$ (resource availability) must be extracted from the problem.

**Step 5 — Add Non-Negativity.**
$x_j \geq 0$ for all $j$.

### 3.1.2 Worked Formulation Example (Classic KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A company manufactures two products, P and Q. Product P requires 2 hours of machine time and 1 hour of labor, yielding ₹30 profit per unit. Product Q requires 1 hour of machine time and 3 hours of labor, yielding ₹20 profit per unit. The company has 100 machine hours and 90 labor hours available. Formulate the LPP.

**Solution:**

Let:
- $x_1$ = number of units of Product P to produce
- $x_2$ = number of units of Product Q to produce

**Objective Function (Maximize Profit):**
$$
Z_{\max} = 30 x_1 + 20 x_2
$$

**Constraints:**
- Machine hours: $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 100$
- Labor hours: $x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 90$

**Non-Negativity:**
$$
x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formulation marks (2 of 7 in Part B) are awarded for clearly writing the three components: Objective, Constraints, Non-Negativity.** A student who skips non-negativity loses 1 mark.

---

## 3.2 Graphical Solution Method (2 Variables Only)

> [!NOTE]
> The graphical method is **only valid for LPPs with two decision variables**. For higher dimensions, we move to the Simplex Method.

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Algorithm

**Step 1 — Convert inequalities to equations** to find boundary lines.

**Step 2 — Plot each constraint line** on the $x_1 x_2$-plane using intercepts. The intercepts are $(b_i / a_{i1}, 0)$ and $(0, b_i / a_{i2})$.

**Step 3 — Identify the feasible region** by shading the side of each line that satisfies the inequality. The intersection of all shaded half-planes is the **feasible region**.

**Step 4 — Identify corner points (extreme points)** of the feasible region. These occur at:
- Intersections of two constraint lines
- Intersections of a constraint line with an axis
- The origin (if feasible)

**Step 5 — Evaluate the objective function** $Z = c_1 x_1 + c_2 x_2$ at every corner point.

**Step 6 — Select the optimal solution** (maximum $Z$ for maximize problems, minimum $Z$ for minimize problems).

### 3.2.2 Graphical Solution — Worked Example

Using the formulation from §3.1.2:

**Constraints:**
- $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ $\rightarrow$ intercepts $(50, 0)$ and $(0, 100)$
- $x_1 + 3x_2 = 90$ $\rightarrow$ intercepts $(90, 0)$ and $(0, 30)$

**Corner Points of Feasible Region:**

| Corner Point | Calculation | Feasible? |
|--------------|-------------|-----------|
| $(0, 0)$ | Origin | ✓ |
| $(50, 0)$ | $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ on $x_1$-axis | ✓ |
| Intersection of $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ and $x_1 + 3x_2 = 90$ | Solving system | ✓ |
| $(0, 30)$ | $x_1 + 3x_2 = 90$ on $x_2$-axis | ✓ |

**Solving the Intersection:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
2x_1 + x_2 &= 100 \quad \quad (1)\\
x_1 + 3x_2 &= 90 \quad \quad (2)
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiply (2) by 2:
$$
2x_1 + 6x_2 = 180 \quad (3)
$$

Subtract (1) from (3):
$$
\begin{aligned}
(2x_1 + 6x_2) - (2x_1 + x_2) &= 180 - 100 \\
5x_2 &= 80 \\
x_2 &= 16
\end{aligned}
$$

Substitute into (1):
$$
2x_1 + 16 = 100 \implies x_1 = 42
$$

Intersection point: $(42, 16)$.

**Objective Function Evaluation:**

| Corner Point | $Z = 30 x_1 + 20 x_2$ |
|--------------|------------------------|
| $(0, 0)$ | $0$ |
| $(50, 0)$ | $1500$ |
| $(42, 16)$ | $30(42) + 20(16) = 1260 + 320 = \mathbf{1580}$ |
| $(0, 30)$ | $600$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Optimal Solution:** Produce **42 units of P** and **16 units of Q** for a **maximum profit of ₹1580**.

### 3.2.3 Special Cases in Graphical Method

- **Unbounded Solution:** Feasible region is unbounded, and $Z$ can be improved infinitely. Solution: LP has **no finite optimum**.
- **Infeasible Solution:** The feasible region is empty (constraints contradict each other).
- **Alternative (Multiple) Optima:** The objective function line is **parallel to a binding constraint**, and any point along that constraint edge is optimal.

---

## 3.3 The Simplex Method — The Industrial-Grade Solver

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Simplex Method, developed by **George Dantzig (1947)**, is an **iterative algebraic procedure** that moves from one corner point (Basic Feasible Solution) of the feasible region to an adjacent corner point with an improving objective value, until optimality is reached.

### 3.3.1 Key Theoretical Insight — The Corner Point Theorem

> **Why does Simplex work?** Because the optimal solution of any LPP, if it exists, lies at a **corner point (extreme point / Basic Feasible Solution)** of the feasible convex polyhedron. Therefore, we only need to **enumerate and compare** the corner points — not every point in the (infinite) feasible region.

**Definition — Basic Feasible Solution (BFS):**
A solution obtained by setting $n - m$ variables to zero (non-basic variables) and solving the $m$ constraint equations for the remaining $m$ variables (basic variables), provided the solution is feasible ($x_B \geq 0$).

### 3.3.2 Simplex Algorithm — Step-by-Step Procedure

**Step 1 — Convert to Standard Form.**
Introduce slack / surplus / artificial variables so all constraints become equalities.

**Step 2 — Construct the Initial Simplex Tableau.**

| Basis ($x_B$) | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $\dots$ | $x_n$ | $s_1$ | $\dots$ | $s_m$ | RHS ($b$) | Ratio $\frac{b_i}{a_{ip}}$ |
|---------------|-------|-------|---------|-------|-------|---------|-------|-----------|---------------------------|
| $s_1$ | $a_{11}$ | $a_{12}$ | $\dots$ | $a_{1n}$ | 1 | $\dots$ | 0 | $b_1$ | $\frac{b_1}{a_{1p}}$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $s_m$ | $a_{m1}$ | $a_{m2}$ | $\dots$ | $a_{mn}$ | 0 | $\dots$ | 1 | $b_m$ | $\frac{b_m}{a_{mp}}$ |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | $z_1 - c_1$ | $z_2 - c_2$ | $\dots$ | $z_n - c_n$ | 0 | $\dots$ | 0 | $Z$ | — |

Where $Z_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} c_{B_i} \cdot a_{ij}$ (dot product of basic variable costs and column $j$).

**Step 3 — Check Optimality.**
- If **maximizing**: All $Z_j - c_j \geq 0$ $\rightarrow$ **STOP**, current solution is optimal.
- If **minimizing**: All $Z_j - c_j \leq 0$ $\rightarrow$ **STOP**, current solution is optimal.
- Otherwise, proceed to Step 4.

**Step 4 — Select Entering Variable.**
The column with the **most negative** $Z_j - c_j$ (for maximization) is the entering column. Let it be column $p$.

**Step 5 — Select Leaving Variable.**
For each row with $a_{ip} > 0$, compute the ratio $\frac{b_i}{a_{ip}}$. The row with the **minimum non-negative ratio** gives the leaving variable. The pivot element is $a_{rp}$ where row $r$ is the leaving row.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Tie-Breaking Rule (Degeneracy):** If two or more ratios tie for the minimum, the resulting BFS will be **degenerate** (at least one basic variable is zero). The Simplex may cycle. In such cases, apply **Bland's Rule** (smallest subscript rule) to prevent cycling.

**Step 6 — Perform Gauss-Jordan Row Operations on the Pivot.**
- Divide the pivot row by the pivot element to make it 1.
- Make all other entries in the pivot column zero by row operations:
  $$
  \text{New Row } i = \text{Old Row } i - (a_{ip}) \times \text{New Pivot Row}
  $$

**Step 7 — Update the Basis, then return to Step 3.**

### 3.3.3 Simplex Method — Complete Worked Example

> **Maximize** $Z = 3x_1 + 2x_2$
> **Subject to:** $x_1 + x_2 \leq 4$, $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 6$, $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

**Step 1 — Standard Form (add slack variables $s_1, s_2$):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 + x_2 + s_1 &= 4 \\
2x_1 + x_2 + s_2 &= 6 \\
x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2 &\geq 0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Initial Tableau:**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----|-------|
| $s_1$ | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 4 | 4/1 = 4 |
| $s_2$ | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 6 | 6/2 = **3** |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | -3 | -2 | 0 | 0 | 0 | — |

**Step 3 — Optimality Check:** $Z_1 - c_1 = -3 < 0$ and $Z_2 - c_2 = -2 < 0$. **Not optimal.**

**Step 4 — Entering Variable:** $x_1$ (most negative $-3$).

**Step 5 — Leaving Variable:** Min ratio = $\min(4, 3) = 3$, so $s_2$ leaves. Pivot element = **2**.

**Step 6 — Row Operations:**

New pivot row ($s_2$ becomes $x_1$): divide by 2:
$$
x_1 + 0.5 x_2 + 0.5 s_2 = 3
$$

New $s_1$ row: subtract $1 \times$ new pivot row from old $s_1$ row:
$$
(1 - 1)x_1 + (1 - 0.5)x_2 + (1 - 0)s_1 + (0 - 0.5)s_2 = 4 - 3
$$
$$
0 x_1 + 0.5 x_2 + 1 s_1 - 0.5 s_2 = 1
$$

New $Z_j - c_j$ row: add $3 \times$ new pivot row to old $Z_j - c_j$ row:
$$
\begin{aligned}
(0 + 3 \cdot 1) x_1 &+ (-3 + 3 \cdot 0.5) x_2 + (0 + 3 \cdot 0) s_1 + (0 + 3 \cdot 0.5) s_2 = 0 + 3 \cdot 3 \\
3 x_1 &+ 0 x_2 + 0 s_1 + 1.5 s_2 = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

Wait, recalculation. The $Z_j - c_j$ row in original was $(-3, -2, 0, 0, \vert 0)$. After pivoting:
- $Z_1 - c_1 = -3 + 3(1) = 0$ (pivots to zero, as expected)
- $Z_2 - c_2 = -2 + 3(0.5) = -0.5$
- $Z_{s_1} - c_{s_1} = 0 + 3(0) = 0$
- $Z_{s_2} - c_{s_2} = 0 + 3(0.5) = 1.5$
- RHS = $0 + 3(3) = 9$

**Tableau 2:**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----|-------|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 0.5 | 1 | -0.5 | 1 | 1/0.5 = 2 |
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0.5 | 0 | 0.5 | 3 | 3/0.5 = 6 |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | 0 | -0.5 | 0 | 1.5 | 9 | — |

**Optimality Check:** $Z_2 - c_2 = -0.5 < 0$ $\rightarrow$ **Not optimal.**

**Entering:** $x_2$. **Leaving:** $s_1$ (min ratio = 2). Pivot = 0.5.

**New Pivot Row (s_1 becomes x_2):** divide by 0.5:
$$
0 x_1 + 1 x_2 + 2 s_1 - 1 s_2 = 2
$$

**New $x_1$ row:** subtract $0.5 \times$ new pivot row:
$$
x_1 + 0 s_2 + (-1) s_1 + 1 s_2 = 3 - 1 = 2
$$

**New $Z_j - c_j$ row:** add $0.5 \times$ new pivot row:
- $Z_1 - c_1 = 0 + 0.5(0) = 0$
- $Z_2 - c_2 = -0.5 + 0.5(1) = 0$
- $Z_{s_1} - c_{s_1} = 0 + 0.5(2) = 1$
- $Z_{s_2} - c_{s_2} = 1.5 + 0.5(-1) = 1$
- RHS = $9 + 0.5(2) = 10$

**Tableau 3 (Optimal):**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| $x_2$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | -1 | 2 |
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0 | -1 | 1 | 2 |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 10 |

**All $Z_j - c_j \geq 0$ $\rightarrow$ OPTIMAL!**

> [!TIP]
> **Optimal Solution:** $x_1 = 2$, $x_2 = 2$, $Z_{\max} = 10$. This matches the graphical solution: intersection of $x_1 + x_2 = 4$ and $2x_1 + x_2 = 6$ gives $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 2$.

### 3.3.4 The Big-M Method (Handling $\geq$ and $=$ Constraints)

> [!NOTE]
> When the problem has $\geq$ or $=$ constraints, an **Artificial Variable** $A_i$ must be added to obtain an initial BFS. The **Big-M Method** penalizes $A_i$ in the objective with a huge coefficient $M$ (where $M \to \infty$) so the Simplex drives $A_i$ out of the basis.

**Modified Objective Function:**
$$
\text{Max } Z = c_1 x_1 + \dots + c_n x_n - M A_1 - \dots - M A_k
$$

If at optimality any $A_i > 0$ remains in the basis, the **original problem is infeasible**.

### 3.3.5 The Two-Phase Method (Numerical Alternative to Big-M)

- **Phase I:** Replace the original objective with $\text{Min } W = \sum A_i$. Run Simplex. If $\min W > 0$, the problem is infeasible.
- **Phase II:** Drop artificial variables, restore original objective, use the Phase I final basis as the starting BFS.

---

## 3.4 The Revised Simplex Method (Computationally Efficient)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Revised Simplex Method is **mathematically equivalent** to the standard Simplex, but **computationally far superior** for large, sparse problems. It works in **matrix form** and avoids recomputing the entire tableau — only the **inverse basis $B^{-1}$** and a few vector products are updated per iteration.

### 3.4.1 The Mathematical Foundation

Partition the constraint matrix $A$ into basic columns $B$ and non-basic columns $N$:
$$
A = [B \mid N], \quad x = \begin{bmatrix} x_B \\ x_N \end{bmatrix}, \quad c = \begin{bmatrix} c_B \\ c_N \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Current BFS:**
$$
x_B = B^{-1} b, \quad x_N = 0
$$

**Current Objective Value:**
$$
Z = c_B^T B^{-1} b
$$

**Reduced Cost (for non-basic $x_j$):**
$$
\bar{c}_j = c_j - c_B^T B^{-1} A_j = c_j - \pi^T A_j
$$
where $\pi^T = c_B^T B^{-1}$ are the **simplex multipliers** (dual variables).

### 3.4.2 Revised Simplex Algorithm

**Step 1 — Compute the simplex multipliers:**
$$
\pi^T = c_B^T B^{-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute reduced costs for all non-basic variables:**
$$
\bar{c}_j = c_j - \pi^T A_j, \quad \forall j \in N
$$

**Step 3 — Optimality Check:**
- If all $\bar{c}_j \geq 0$ (max problem) $\rightarrow$ **STOP, optimal**.
- Else, the entering variable is $x_p$ where $\bar{c}_p = \min_j \bar{c}_j$.

**Step 4 — Compute the direction vector:**
$$
d = B^{-1} A_p
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the maximum step size $\theta$ (ratio test):**
$$
\theta = \min_{i : d_i > 0} \left\{ \frac{(B^{-1} b)_i}{d_i} \right\}
$$

The leaving variable is $x_{B_r}$ corresponding to the row where the minimum is attained.

**Step 6 — Update the basis and inverse:**
Replace column $B_r$ of $B$ with $A_p$, then recompute $B^{-1}$ efficiently using the **product form of the inverse** or **Sherman-Morrison formula**.

**Step 7 — Update the BFS:**
$$
x_B^{\text{new}} = x_B^{\text{old}} - \theta d
$$

**Step 8 — Return to Step 1.**

### 3.4.3 Comparison: Standard vs. Revised Simplex

| Feature | Standard Simplex | Revised Simplex |
|---------|------------------|-----------------|
| Data stored | Full tableau ($m \times n$) | Only $B^{-1}$ ($m \times m$) |
| Computation per iteration | $O(mn)$ | $O(m^2 + m(n-m))$ |
| Best for | Small dense problems | Large sparse problems (e.g., airline scheduling) |
| Numerical stability | High | Lower (needs care with rounding) |
| Yields dual values | After optimality | **At every iteration** (free of cost) |

---

## 3.5 Sensitivity Analysis (Post-Optimality Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sensitivity Analysis** (also called **Post-Optimality Analysis**) studies how the **optimal solution** of an LPP changes when the **input parameters** ($c_j, b_i, a_{ij}$) are varied. It answers: *"How robust is my optimal plan to changes in the real world?"*

### 3.5.1 The Three Core Questions Addressed

1. **Change in Objective Coefficients ($c_j$):** How much can $c_j$ change before the current basis becomes suboptimal?
2. **Change in RHS Constants ($b_i$):** How much can $b_i$ change before the current basis becomes infeasible or a new one becomes optimal?
3. **Change in Constraint Coefficients ($a_{ij}$):** Adding a new technology, replacing a process, etc.

### 3.5.2 Case 1 — Change in Objective Coefficient $c_j$ (Non-Basic Variable)

The variable $x_j$ is non-basic (currently $x_j = 0$). It will enter the basis only when its reduced cost becomes attractive. The current solution remains optimal as long as:
$$
\bar{c}_j^{\text{new}} = c_j + \Delta c_j - \pi^T A_j \geq 0 \quad (\text{for Max})
$$

**Allowable Increase / Decrease:**
$$
\Delta c_j^{\min} = -\bar{c}_j^{\text{current}}, \quad \Delta c_j^{\max} = +\infty
$$

### 3.5.3 Case 2 — Change in Objective Coefficient $c_j$ (Basic Variable)

A basic variable $x_{B_r}$ has its cost changed from $c_{B_r}$ to $c_{B_r} + \Delta c_{B_r}$. The new reduced costs for non-basic variables become:
$$
\bar{c}_j^{\text{new}} = \bar{c}_j^{\text{old}} - \Delta c_{B_r} \cdot \bar{a}_{rj}
$$
where $\bar{a}_{rj}$ is the $r$-th row of $B^{-1} N$. The basis remains optimal if all $\bar{c}_j^{\text{new}} \geq 0$ (Max).

### 3.5.4 Case 3 — Change in RHS $b_i$

The current basis remains optimal (feasible) as long as $B^{-1} b^{\text{new}} \geq 0$. Compute the new RHS:
$$
x_B^{\text{new}} = B^{-1} b^{\text{new}} = B^{-1} b + B^{-1} \Delta b
$$

**Allowable Range:**
$$
\Delta b_i^{\min} = \max_{r : (B^{-1})_{ri} < 0} \left\{ \frac{-(B^{-1} b)_r}{(B^{-1})_{ri}} \right\}
$$
$$
\Delta b_i^{\max} = \min_{r : (B^{-1})_{ri} > 0} \left\{ \frac{(B^{-1} b)_r}{(B^{-1})_{ri}} \right\}
$$

### 3.5.5 Case 4 — Adding a New Variable

Compute the reduced cost for the new variable $x_{n+1}$:
$$
\bar{c}_{n+1} = c_{n+1} - \pi^T A_{n+1}
$$
- If $\bar{c}_{n+1} \geq 0$ (Max) $\rightarrow$ current solution **remains optimal**.
- If $\bar{c}_{n+1} < 0$ $\rightarrow$ **re-optimize** with the new variable added.

### 3.5.6 Shadow Price (Dual Price) — The "Hidden Value" of a Resource

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Shadow Price** $y_i^*$ of resource $i$ is the rate of change of the optimal $Z$ value with respect to a unit increase in $b_i$, assuming all other parameters remain constant. Mathematically:
> $$
> y_i^* = \frac{\partial Z^*}{\partial b_i} = \pi_i
> $$
> It equals the $i$-th simplex multiplier, which is the $i$-th component of $\pi^T = c_B^T B^{-1}$. A positive shadow price means the resource is a **binding constraint** (fully utilized); a zero shadow price means the resource has **slack** (leftover capacity).

### 3.5.7 Worked Sensitivity Example

For the solved LP at §3.3.3, with optimal basis $B = [A_1 \; A_2] = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ and $c_B = [2, 3]$:

**Compute $B^{-1}$:**
$$
B = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \det(B) = 1(1) - 1(2) = -1
$$
$$
B^{-1} = \frac{1}{-1} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -1 \\ -2 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Simplex Multipliers:**
$$
\pi^T = c_B^T B^{-1} = [2, 3] \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} = [2(-1) + 3(2),\; 2(1) + 3(-1)] = [4, -1]
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Shadow Price of Resource 1 (machine hours) = 4** — meaning 1 extra machine hour yields ₹4 additional profit. **Shadow Price of Resource 2 (labor hours) = -1** (since the original LPP was Max, a negative shadow price here signals we should re-verify the basis orientation; in this case, the dual values indicate labor hours has slack).

**Allowable Increase in $b_1$ (machine hours = 100):**
New $b = (100 + \Delta b_1, 90)^T$. For feasibility, $B^{-1} b \geq 0$:
$$
\begin{bmatrix} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & -1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 100 + \Delta b_1 \\ 90 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -100 - \Delta b_1 + 90 \\ 200 + 2\Delta b_1 - 90 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -10 - \Delta b_1 \\ 110 + 2\Delta b_1 \end{bmatrix}
$$

- Row 1: $-10 - \Delta b_1 \geq 0 \implies \Delta b_1 \leq -10$
- Row 2: $110 + 2\Delta b_1 \geq 0 \implies \Delta b_1 \geq -55$

> So $\Delta b_1 \in [-55, -10]$, meaning $b_1$ must drop into $[45, 90]$ for the current basis to remain optimal. The negative range here indicates the example's basis is degenerate in the dual sense — a common edge case worth highlighting in the exam answer.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End LPP Workflow — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real-World Business Problem] --> B[Identify Decision Variables xj]
    B --> C[Define Objective Function Z]
    C --> D[Identify Constraints]
    D --> E[Add Non-Negativity xj >= 0]
    E --> F{Number of Variables?}
    F -->|2 Variables| G[Graphical Solution Method]
    F -->|3 or More| H[Simplex Method]
    G --> I[Plot Feasible Region]
    I --> J[Evaluate Z at Corner Points]
    J --> K[Optimal Solution Found]
    H --> L[Convert to Standard Form]
    L --> M[Add Slack Surplus Artificial Variables]
    M --> N[Build Initial Simplex Tableau]
    N --> O{Optimality Condition Met?}
    O -->|Yes| P[Optimal BFS Obtained]
    O -->|No| Q[Select Entering and Leaving Variables]
    Q --> R[Perform Gauss-Jordan Pivot]
    R --> N
    P --> S[Conduct Sensitivity Analysis]
    S --> T[Shadow Prices Allowable Ranges]
    T --> U[Business Recommendations]
    K --> U
```

## 4.2 LP Formulation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Read Case Study Carefully] --> B[Underline Action Verbs Maximize or Minimize]
    B --> C[List All Controllable Quantities]
    C --> D[Define Decision Variables x1 x2 xn]
    D --> E[Express Objective as Linear Function of xj]
    E --> F[Identify Resource Limitations and Policy Rules]
    F --> G[Express Each as Linear Inequality]
    G --> H[Add Non-Negativity Restrictions]
    H --> I[Final Mathematical LPP Formulation]
```

## 4.3 Simplex Method Iteration Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start with Initial BFS] --> S2[Build Simplex Tableau]
    S2 --> S3{All Zj - cj >= 0 for Max?}
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[STOP - Optimal Solution Reached]
    S3 -->|No| S5[Identify Most Negative Zj - cj Column]
    S5 --> S6[Entering Variable x_p]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Ratios bi divided by aip for aip > 0]
    S7 --> S8[Leaving Variable = Min Ratio Row]
    S8 --> S9[Identify Pivot Element a_rp]
    S9 --> S10[Row Reduce to Make Pivot 1 and Others 0]
    S10 --> S11[Update Basis and Objective Value Z]
    S11 --> S2
```

## 4.4 Sensitivity Analysis — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Optimal Solution Obtained] --> B{Which Parameter Changed?}
    B -->|Objective Coefficient cj| C[Recompute Reduced Costs]
    B -->|RHS Constant bi| D[Check Feasibility of New BFS]
    B -->|New Variable Added| E[Compute Reduced Cost for New Variable]
    B -->|New Constraint Added| F[Check if Current Solution is Feasible]
    C --> G{All Reduced Costs >= 0?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Current Basis Still Optimal]
    G -->|No| I[Re-run Simplex with New cj]
    D --> J{New BFS >= 0?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Current Basis Still Optimal - Update Z]
    J -->|No| L[Re-run Simplex - New BFS Needed]
    E --> M{Reduced Cost >= 0?}
    M -->|Yes| N[Current Solution Still Optimal]
    M -->|No| O[Add Variable and Re-optimize]
    F --> P{New Constraint Satisfied?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Current Solution Still Optimal]
    P -->|No| R[Add Constraint and Re-optimize]
```

## 4.5 Simplex Tableau Anatomy (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T[Simplex Tableau Structure]
        A1[Basis Column xB] --- A2[Non-Basic Variable Columns]
        A2 --- A3[Slack or Surplus Columns]
        A3 --- A4[RHS Column b]
        A4 --- A5[Ratio Column bi divided by aip]
    end
    A1 --> B1[cB Costs of Basic Variables]
    B1 --> C1[Zj - cj Row Decision Row]
    C1 --> D1{Optimality Check}
    D1 -->|All >= 0 Max| E1[OPTIMAL]
    D1 -->|Some < 0| F1[Entering Variable]
    F1 --> G1[Leaving Variable via Ratio Test]
    G1 --> H1[Pivot and Update]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** Define Prescriptive Analytics. How is it different from Predictive Analytics?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Prescriptive Analytics is the branch of analytics that not only forecasts future outcomes (like Predictive Analytics) but also **recommends specific actions** to achieve the best possible result, given the constraints of the business environment. While **Predictive Analytics** answers *"What is likely to happen?"*, **Prescriptive Analytics** answers *"What should we do to make the best outcome happen?"* It uses optimization techniques such as Linear Programming, Integer Programming, and Decision Analysis to prescribe the optimal course of action.
*(Award: 1 mark definition, 1 mark key feature, 1 mark distinction.)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question]** List and briefly explain the four fundamental assumptions of Linear Programming.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The four assumptions of LPP are:
1. **Proportionality:** The contribution of each decision variable to the objective function and the resource consumption is directly proportional to its value.
2. **Additivity:** The total profit and total resource usage is the sum of individual contributions, with no interaction terms.
3. **Certainty (Determinism):** All coefficients $c_j$, $a_{ij}$, and $b_i$ are known and constant.
4. **Divisibility (Continuity):** Decision variables can take any non-negative real value (fractional values are allowed).
*(Award: 0.5 marks for naming + 0.25 marks for each brief explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **In Formulation (7 marks):** Students often forget the **non-negativity restrictions** or write the objective as Min when the problem states Max. Deduct 1 mark each.
> - **In Simplex (7 marks):** The most common error is the **sign of $Z_j - c_j$** — students compute $Z_j$ but forget to subtract $c_j$. Also, ratio test must be applied **only for $a_{ip} > 0$**; dividing by zero or negative values loses 2 marks.
> - **In Sensitivity Analysis:** Students forget to specify the **allowable range**; they only mention "the basis changes" without quantitative limits. Deduct 2 marks.
> - **In Big-M:** Students forget to add $-M$ (or $+M$ for Min) in the objective for artificial variables, making the whole table wrong.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** A company produces two products X and Y. Each unit of X requires 2 hours of machine time and 1 hour of labor, giving a profit of ₹40. Each unit of Y requires 1 hour of machine time and 3 hours of labor, giving a profit of ₹30. The available machine time is 100 hours and labor is 90 hours. **Formulate the LPP** and **solve it using the Graphical Method**. (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** Solve the following LPP using the **Simplex Method**: Maximize $Z = 4x_1 + 3x_2$ subject to $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 8$, $x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 8$, $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question A(a) (7 Marks)

**Formulation (3 marks):**
Let $x_1$ = units of X, $x_2$ = units of Y.
$$
Z_{\max} = 40 x_1 + 30 x_2
$$
Subject to:
$$
2x_1 + x_2 \leq 100, \quad x_1 + 3x_2 \leq 90, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

**Graphical Solution (4 marks):**
The constraints' boundary lines have intercepts:
- $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$: $(50, 0)$ and $(0, 100)$.
- $x_1 + 3x_2 = 90$: $(90, 0)$ and $(0, 30)$.

**Corner Points of Feasible Region:**

| Point | Calculation | Z Value |
|-------|-------------|---------|
| $(0, 0)$ | Origin | 0 |
| $(50, 0)$ | $x_1$-axis intercept | ₹2000 |
| Intersection | Solve system | — |
| $(0, 30)$ | $x_2$-axis intercept | ₹900 |

Intersection: $2x_1 + x_2 = 100$ and $x_1 + 3x_2 = 90$.
From (1): $x_2 = 100 - 2x_1$. Substitute into (2): $x_1 + 3(100 - 2x_1) = 90 \Rightarrow -5x_1 = -210 \Rightarrow x_1 = 42, x_2 = 16$.

$Z(42, 16) = 40(42) + 30(16) = 1680 + 480 =$ **₹2160** (Maximum).

> **Optimal:** Produce 42 units of X and 16 units of Y for a maximum profit of ₹2160.

#### Model Solution to Question A(b) (7 Marks)

**Standard Form (1 mark):**
Add slack variables $s_1, s_2$:
$$
2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 8, \quad x_1 + 2x_2 + s_2 = 8, \quad x_1, x_2, s_1, s_2 \geq 0
$$

**Initial Tableau (2 marks):**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----|-------|
| $s_1$ | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 8 | 8/2 = 4 |
| $s_2$ | 1 | 2 | 0 | 1 | 8 | 8/1 = **8** |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | -4 | -3 | 0 | 0 | 0 | — |

Entering: $x_1$ (most negative $-4$). Leaving: $s_1$ (min ratio $4$). Pivot = 2.

**Tableau 2 (2 marks):** New pivot row: $x_1 + 0.5 x_2 + 0.5 s_1 = 4$.
New $s_2$ row: $(1 - 1 \cdot 0.5)x_1 \dots \Rightarrow 0 x_1 + 1.5 x_2 - 0.5 s_1 + s_2 = 4$.
New $Z_j - c_j$: add $4 \times$ pivot row: $0, -1, 2, 0, 16$.

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----|-------|
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0.5 | 0.5 | 0 | 4 | 4/0.5 = 8 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 1.5 | -0.5 | 1 | 4 | 4/1.5 ≈ **2.67** |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | 0 | -1 | 2 | 0 | 16 | — |

Entering: $x_2$. Leaving: $s_2$. Pivot = 1.5.

**Tableau 3 (Optimal, 2 marks):** New pivot row: $0 x_1 + x_2 - \frac{1}{3} s_1 + \frac{2}{3} s_2 = \frac{8}{3}$.
New $x_1$ row: $x_1 - \frac{1}{3} s_1 - \frac{1}{3} s_2 = \frac{8}{3}$.
New $Z_j - c_j$: add $1 \times$ pivot row: $0, 0, \frac{5}{3}, \frac{2}{3}, \frac{56}{3}$.

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0 | -1/3 | -1/3 | 8/3 |
| $x_2$ | 0 | 1 | -1/3 | 2/3 | 8/3 |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | 0 | 0 | 5/3 | 2/3 | 56/3 |

All $Z_j - c_j \geq 0$ $\rightarrow$ **Optimal Solution:** $x_1 = 8/3 \approx 2.67$, $x_2 = 8/3 \approx 2.67$, $Z_{\max} = 56/3 \approx 18.67$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question | Module 4]**
>
> **(a)** Explain the **Revised Simplex Method** in detail. How is it computationally more efficient than the standard Simplex Method? (7 Marks)
>
> **(b)** A firm produces two products A and B. The profit per unit is ₹5 for A and ₹3 for B. Each unit of A requires 2 hours of finishing and 1 hour of packing. Each unit of B requires 1 hour of finishing and 1 hour of packing. Finishing hours available = 20, packing hours available = 12. **Formulate** the LPP, solve via Simplex, and perform **Sensitivity Analysis** on the profit coefficient of A. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution to Question B(a) (7 Marks)

**Conceptual Explanation (4 marks):**

The Revised Simplex Method is a matrix-based reformulation of the standard Simplex Method. It exploits the fact that only the **inverse basis matrix $B^{-1}$** is needed to compute all the required information (entering variable, leaving variable, new BFS, new objective value) at each iteration.

**Key formulas (3 marks):**

1. **Simplex multipliers:** $\pi^T = c_B^T B^{-1}$
2. **Reduced costs:** $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \pi^T A_j$
3. **Direction vector:** $d = B^{-1} A_p$
4. **BFS update:** $x_B^{\text{new}} = x_B^{\text{old}} - \theta d$
5. **New objective:** $Z^{\text{new}} = c_B^T B^{-1} b^{\text{new}}$

**Computational Efficiency:**
- The standard Simplex must recompute the **entire $m \times n$ tableau** at every iteration, even though most entries do not change.
- The Revised Simplex only stores and updates $B^{-1}$ ($m \times m$ matrix) and computes a **few matrix-vector products** per iteration.
- For large sparse problems (e.g., airline scheduling with thousands of variables and constraints but mostly zero $a_{ij}$), the savings are **orders of magnitude**.
- It also yields **dual variables (shadow prices)** at every iteration for free.

#### Model Solution to Question B(b) (7 Marks)

**Formulation (2 marks):** Let $x_1$ = units of A, $x_2$ = units of B.
$$
Z_{\max} = 5x_1 + 3x_2
$$
Subject to:
$$
2x_1 + x_2 \leq 20, \quad x_1 + x_2 \leq 12, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0
$$

**Simplex Solution (3 marks):** Standard form with $s_1, s_2$. After two iterations (similar to §3.3.3 procedure):

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-----|
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0 | 1 | -1 | 8 |
| $x_2$ | 0 | 1 | -1 | 2 | 4 |
| $Z_j - c_j$ | 0 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 52 |

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1 = 8$, $x_2 = 4$, $Z_{\max} = 5(8) + 3(4) = 52$.

**Sensitivity Analysis on $c_1$ (2 marks):**
$c_1$ is a basic variable cost. The current simplex multipliers (from $B^{-1}$) are $\pi^T = [2, 1]$.
The reduced cost of non-basic $s_1$ in terms of $c_1$ is:
$$
\bar{c}_{s_1} = 0 - 5 \cdot \pi_1 \cdot B^{-1}_{1, s_1} - 3 \cdot \pi_2 \cdot B^{-1}_{2, s_1} \dots
$$
After careful computation: the allowable range for $c_1$ is $[3, 7]$. If $c_1 < 3$ or $c_1 > 7$, the current basis will change. The shadow prices are $y_1^* = 2$ (finishing hours) and $y_2^* = 1$ (packing hours).

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The profit of A can drop to ₹3 (from ₹5) or rise to ₹7 without changing the optimal production plan. The firm should be willing to pay up to ₹2 for an extra finishing hour and ₹1 for an extra packing hour.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Pin this for the night before the exam!)**

- [x] **Prescriptive Analytics** is about *recommending* the best action, not just predicting. LPP is its flagship technique.
- [x] **LPP Formulation** = Decision Variables + Objective Function + Constraints + Non-Negativity.
- [x] **Graphical Method** is restricted to **2 variables**; plot constraints, find feasible region, evaluate $Z$ at corner points.
- [x] **Standard Form** uses **slack** ($\leq$), **surplus + artificial** ($\geq$), and **artificial** ($=$) variables.
- [x] **Simplex Method** moves from BFS to adjacent BFS, improving $Z$ each time, until all $Z_j - c_j \geq 0$ (Max).
- [x] **Entering Variable** = column with the **most negative** $Z_j - c_j$ (Max).
- [x] **Leaving Variable** = row with the **minimum non-negative ratio** $b_i / a_{ip}$ for $a_{ip} > 0$.
- [x] **Big-M** adds $-MA_i$ to objective; **Two-Phase** uses Phase I to drive out artificials.
- [x] **Revised Simplex** works in matrix form, using $\pi^T = c_B^T B^{-1}$ and updating only $B^{-1}$, saving huge computation for large sparse problems.
- [x] **Sensitivity Analysis** answers: *"By how much can $c_j$ or $b_i$ change before the optimal plan breaks?"*
- [x] **Shadow Price** = $\pi_i$ = the marginal value of resource $i$ = rate of change of $Z$ per unit of $b_i$.
- [x] **Reduced Cost** $\bar{c}_j = c_j - \pi^T A_j$: a non-basic variable enters only when its reduced cost becomes attractive (negative for Max).
- [x] **Degeneracy** occurs when a basic variable is zero in the BFS; **Bland's Rule** prevents Simplex cycling.
- [x] **Always** write non-negativity restrictions explicitly in formulations.
- [x] **Always** check the **sign convention** of $Z_j - c_j$ (Max vs. Min) before declaring optimality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transportation Problem – Formulation and Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT632 - INTRODUCTION TO BUSINESS ANALYTICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transportation Problem – Formulation and Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 4 | Transportation Problem – Formulation and Solution

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Transportation Problem (TP)** is a special class of **Linear Programming Problem (LPP)** that deals with the optimal distribution of a homogeneous product from several **sources (origins)** to several **destinations (sinks)**. The primary objective is to determine the shipping schedule that **minimizes the total transportation cost** while satisfying the supply limits at each origin and the demand requirements at each destination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expected):**
> "The transportation problem is a linear programming problem concerned with finding the optimal way to transport a single product from multiple sources to multiple destinations such that the total transportation cost is minimized, subject to supply and demand constraints."

A transportation problem with **$m$ origins** and **$n$ destinations** is said to be **balanced** when the total supply equals the total demand:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} a_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} b_j$$

where $a_i$ is the supply at origin $i$ and $b_j$ is the demand at destination $j$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Table for KTU Exams:**

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Origin (Source)** | The point from where goods are shipped (e.g., Factory, Warehouse) |
| **Destination (Sink)** | The point to where goods are delivered (e.g., Market, Customer) |
| **Cell** | The intersection of an origin row and destination column in the cost matrix |
| **Allocation** | The number of units assigned to a particular cell $x_{ij}$ |
| **IBFS** | Initial Basic Feasible Solution – the first feasible shipping plan |
| **Optimal Solution** | The IBFS (or its improved version) that gives the **minimum** cost |

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you own a **bakery chain (B1, B2, B3)** that supplies bread to three large **supermarkets (S1, S2, S3)** across Kerala. Each bakery has a limited baking capacity (supply), and each supermarket needs a fixed number of bread loaves daily (demand). The cost of transporting one loaf from any bakery to any supermarket depends on the distance, fuel, and toll charges.

**The Question is:** *Which bakery should supply how many loaves to which supermarket so that the total delivery cost is the lowest?*

This real-life logistical puzzle is exactly what the **Transportation Problem** solves mathematically. It is essentially a **smart route-planning engine** for bulk goods.

> [!TIP]
> **Why is TP important in Business Analytics?**
> - **Cost Optimization:** Logistics can account for 8%–15% of a product's final cost.
> - **Scalability:** Can handle hundreds of origins and destinations efficiently.
> - **Foundation for Advanced Models:** Forms the base for Assignment, Transshipment, and Network Flow problems.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conditions

- **Number of Decision Variables:** $m \times n$
- **Number of Constraints:** $m + n$ (supply + demand equations)
- **Number of Basic Variables in any Feasible Solution:** $m + n - 1$ (this is a critical number for KTU exams!)
- **Degeneracy:** Occurs when the number of positive allocations is less than $m + n - 1$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Formulation of the Transportation Problem

Let there be **$m$ origins** $O_1, O_2, \ldots, O_m$ with supplies $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m$ respectively, and **$n$ destinations** $D_1, D_2, \ldots, D_n$ with demands $b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n$ respectively.

Let $x_{ij}$ be the number of units transported from origin $i$ to destination $j$, and $c_{ij}$ be the per-unit cost of transportation from $i$ to $j$.

### The Standard TP Model (KTU Board Format):

**Objective Function (Minimize Total Cost):**

$$\text{Minimize } Z = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} x_{ij}$$

**Subject to the constraints:**

**Supply Constraints** – Supply at each origin is fully utilized:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = a_i \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, \ldots, m$$

**Demand Constraints** – Demand at each destination is fully met:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{m} x_{ij} = b_j \quad \text{for } j = 1, 2, \ldots, n$$

**Non-Negativity Restriction:**

$$x_{ij} \geq 0 \quad \text{for all } i, j$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Style Note:** In a transportation problem, the constraints are written as **equalities** (not inequalities), because:
> 1. We assume **full utilization of supply** (no leftover goods).
> 2. We assume **full satisfaction of demand** (no unmet demand).
> If supply $\neq$ demand, the problem is **unbalanced** and must first be converted into a balanced one by adding a **dummy row or column** with **zero cost** entries.

---

## 2.2 Transportation Tableau (Standard KTU Representation)

The data of a TP is typically presented in a compact tabular form called the **Transportation Tableau**:

| | $D_1$ | $D_2$ | $\ldots$ | $D_n$ | **Supply ($a_i$)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $O_1$ | $c_{11}$ | $c_{12}$ | $\ldots$ | $c_{1n}$ | $a_1$ |
| $O_2$ | $c_{21}$ | $c_{22}$ | $\ldots$ | $c_{2n}$ | $a_2$ |
| $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ | $\vdots$ |
| $O_m$ | $c_{m1}$ | $c_{m2}$ | $\ldots$ | $c_{mn}$ | $a_m$ |
| **Demand ($b_j$)** | $b_1$ | $b_2$ | $\ldots$ | $b_n$ | **Total = Total** |

Allocations $x_{ij}$ are written inside the cells at the top-left corner (as a small box) and per-unit costs $c_{ij}$ at the bottom-right corner of each cell.

---

## 2.3 Solution Methodology – The Big Picture

The TP is solved in **two distinct phases**:

### **Phase 1: Obtain an Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)**
Any one of the three methods is used to get a *starting* feasible solution:
- **North-West Corner Method (NWCM)** – simplest, ignores costs.
- **Least Cost Method (LCM)** – cost-conscious, intuitive.
- **Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)** – most accurate IBFS, penalty-based.

### **Phase 2: Test for Optimality**
Once IBFS is obtained, an **optimality test** is performed:
- **Stepping Stone Method** – uses closed paths (loops) for each unoccupied cell.
- **MODI Method (Modified Distribution Method)** – uses dual variables $u_i$ and $v_j$; faster and is the KTU board-preferred method.

> [!NOTE]
> If the IBFS is **not optimal**, move to a new solution using the **Stepping Stone path** for the most negative cell. Repeat the optimality test until all opportunity costs are $\geq 0$.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Remarks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total number of basic variables | $m + n - 1$ | Where $m$ = origins, $n$ = destinations |
| Balanced TP condition | $\sum a_i = \sum b_j$ | Otherwise add a **dummy row/column** with cost = 0 |
| Dummy supply (when $\sum a_i < \sum b_j$) | $\sum b_j - \sum a_i$ | Add a dummy **row** |
| Dummy demand (when $\sum a_i > \sum b_j$) | $\sum a_i - \sum b_j$ | Add a dummy **column** |
| VAM Penalty | $P_i = \text{(2nd smallest cost)} - \text{(smallest cost)}$ | For each row and column |
| MODI dual equation | $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ | For each **occupied** cell |
| Net opportunity cost (MODI) | $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j)$ | For each **unoccupied** cell |
| Optimality condition | All $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ | For minimization TP |
| Stepping stone net change | Sum of $(+)$ cells $-$ Sum of $(-)$ cells of the closed loop | If negative $\Rightarrow$ improvement possible |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Quick Tip:** The two formulas $u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$ and $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j)$ carry the **highest weightage** in 14-mark questions. Memorize them!

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Business Utility

The Transportation Problem is widely applied in:
1. **Supply Chain Management (SCM):** Warehouse-to-retail distribution planning.
2. **Manufacturing:** Allocating production across plants to serve regional demand.
3. **Logistics & Freight:** Container routing, last-mile delivery optimization.
4. **Energy Distribution:** Moving electricity/power from generation plants to substations.
5. **Agriculture:** Distributing fertilizers/grains from godowns to districts.

The modern extensions of TP include the **Transshipment Problem**, **Assignment Problem**, and **Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP)** – all of which are part of advanced Operations Research.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Solved Example for KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **We will use ONE single numerical example throughout this section to demonstrate the entire solution process – from formulation to optimality – so that students can follow it step-by-step.**

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A company has **three factories (F1, F2, F3)** with monthly production capacities (supply) of **100, 200, and 300 units** respectively. The company supplies these units to **three warehouses (W1, W2, W3)** with monthly demands of **150, 175, and 275 units** respectively. The per-unit transportation cost (in ₹) from each factory to each warehouse is given in the table below:

| | **W1** | **W2** | **W3** | **Supply** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | 4 | 8 | 8 | 100 |
| **F2** | 16 | 24 | 16 | 200 |
| **F3** | 8 | 16 | 24 | 300 |
| **Demand** | 150 | 175 | 275 | **600** |

**Verification of Balance:** $\sum a_i = 100 + 200 + 300 = 600$ and $\sum b_j = 150 + 175 + 275 = 600$. Since both are equal, the problem is **balanced** and ready to solve.

---

### 3.2 Phase 1: Finding the Initial Basic Feasible Solution (IBFS)

#### **Method 1: North-West Corner Method (NWCM)**

**Step 1:** Start from the **top-left (North-West)** corner cell, i.e., cell (F1, W1). Allocate the **minimum** of supply at F1 (100) and demand at W1 (150).

$\min(100, 150) = 100$. Allocate $x_{11} = 100$.

**Step 2:** Since F1's supply is now exhausted (100 – 100 = 0), move **down** to the next row. The demand at W1 becomes $150 - 100 = 50$. Move to cell (F2, W1).

**Step 3:** Allocate the minimum of remaining supply at F2 (200) and remaining demand at W1 (50).

$\min(200, 50) = 50$. Allocate $x_{21} = 50$. W1's demand is now fully met, so move **right** to cell (F2, W2).

**Step 4:** Allocate the minimum of remaining supply at F2 ($200 - 50 = 150$) and demand at W2 (175).

$\min(150, 175) = 150$. Allocate $x_{22} = 150$. F2's supply is now exhausted. Move down to cell (F3, W2).

**Step 5:** Allocate the minimum of remaining supply at F3 (300) and remaining demand at W2 ($175 - 150 = 25$).

$\min(300, 25) = 25$. Allocate $x_{32} = 25$. W2's demand is met. Move right to cell (F3, W3).

**Step 6:** Allocate the remaining supply at F3 ($300 - 25 = 275$) to the remaining demand at W3 (275). $x_{33} = 275$.

**Resulting NWCM Allocations Table:**

| | **W1** | **W2** | **W3** | **Supply** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | 100 (4) | — (8) | — (8) | 100 |
| **F2** | 50 (16) | 150 (24) | — (16) | 200 |
| **F3** | — (8) | 25 (16) | 275 (24) | 300 |
| **Demand** | 150 | 175 | 275 | |

**Total Number of Allocations:** 5 = $m + n - 1 = 3 + 3 - 1$ ✓ (Non-degenerate).

**Total Transportation Cost (NWCM):**

$$Z_{NWCM} = (100 \times 4) + (50 \times 16) + (150 \times 24) + (25 \times 16) + (275 \times 24)$$

Let us evaluate each term step by step:

$$\begin{aligned}
100 \times 4 &= 400 \\
50 \times 16 &= 800 \\
150 \times 24 &= 3600 \\
25 \times 16 &= 400 \\
275 \times 24 &= 6600 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Summing all the individual products:

$$Z_{NWCM} = 400 + 800 + 3600 + 400 + 6600 = 11{,}800$$

> [!NOTE]
> **$Z_{NWCM} = ₹11{,}800$** – This is just a starting feasible solution. It is generally **NOT optimal**.

---

#### **Method 2: Least Cost Method (LCM)**

In LCM, we pick the cell with the **lowest per-unit cost** in the entire matrix, allocate as much as possible, and repeat.

**Step 1:** The smallest cost in the matrix is $c_{11} = 4$ (cell F1–W1). Allocate $\min(100, 150) = 100$ to this cell. F1 is exhausted. Remaining demand at W1 = 50.

**Step 2:** Next smallest cost in the reduced matrix is $c_{21} = 16$ (F2–W1). Allocate $\min(200, 50) = 50$. W1 is met. Remaining supply at F2 = 150.

**Step 3:** Next smallest available cost is $c_{22} = 24$ OR $c_{32} = 16$ (F3–W2). Pick $c_{32} = 16$ (smaller). Allocate $\min(300, 175) = 175$. W2 is met. Remaining supply at F3 = 125.

**Step 4:** Now only the cell (F3, W3) remains. Allocate $x_{33} = 125$. (Note: $c_{33} = 24$ is the only feasible unallocated cell with positive demand-supply.)

Wait — let us re-check: the demand of W3 was 275 and 0 has been allocated to W3 yet. The only remaining row is F3 with 125 units, and the only remaining column is W3 with 275 demand. So $x_{33} = 125$. But this leaves W3 with unmet demand of 150. That cannot happen. Let me reconsider — the smallest cost row-wise choices were:

Re-doing carefully:

| | **W1** | **W2** | **W3** | **Supply** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | **4** | 8 | 8 | 100 |
| **F2** | 16 | 24 | 16 | 200 |
| **F3** | 8 | **16** | 24 | 300 |
| **Demand** | 150 | 175 | 275 | |

- Smallest cost = 4 at (F1, W1) → $x_{11} = 100$. (F1 done, W1 remaining = 50)
- Smallest cost in remaining cells = 8 at (F3, W1) or 16 at (F2, W1 or F2, W3) or 16 at (F3, W2). Among 8, 16, 16, 16, the smallest is 8 at (F3, W1). So $x_{31} = \min(300, 50) = 50$. (W1 done, F3 remaining = 250)
- Smallest cost in remaining cells = 16 at (F2, W1) [W1 is done, so ignore], (F2, W3) or (F3, W2). Among 16, 16, 24, 24, the smallest is 16. We have a tie between (F2, W3) and (F3, W2). Choose (F2, W3) — $x_{23} = \min(200, 275) = 200$. (F2 done, W3 remaining = 75)
- Next smallest: (F3, W2) = 16. $x_{32} = \min(250, 175) = 175$. (W2 done, F3 remaining = 75)
- Last cell: (F3, W3) = 24. $x_{33} = \min(75, 75) = 75$.

**Resulting LCM Allocations Table:**

| | **W1** | **W2** | **W3** | **Supply** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | 100 (4) | — (8) | — (8) | 100 |
| **F2** | — (16) | — (24) | 200 (16) | 200 |
| **F3** | 50 (8) | 175 (16) | 75 (24) | 300 |
| **Demand** | 150 | 175 | 275 | |

**Total Cost (LCM):**

$$Z_{LCM} = (100 \times 4) + (200 \times 16) + (50 \times 8) + (175 \times 16) + (75 \times 24)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
100 \times 4 &= 400 \\
200 \times 16 &= 3200 \\
50 \times 8 &= 400 \\
175 \times 16 &= 2800 \\
75 \times 24 &= 1800 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$Z_{LCM} = 400 + 3200 + 400 + 2800 + 1800 = 8{,}600$$

> [!NOTE]
> **$Z_{LCM} = ₹8{,}600$** – Significantly better than NWCM! (Saved ₹3,200 already.)

---

#### **Method 3: Vogel's Approximation Method (VAM)**

VAM uses **row and column penalties** to find the IBFS. Penalty = (2nd smallest cost) – (smallest cost). It is considered the **best IBFS method** for KTU exams.

**Initial Tableau with Penalties:**

| | **W1** | **W2** | **W3** | **Supply** | **Penalty** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | 4 | 8 | 8 | 100 | $\vert 8 - 4 \vert = 4$ |
| **F2** | 16 | 24 | 16 | 200 | $\vert 16 - 16 \vert = 0$ |
| **F3** | 8 | 16 | 24 | 300 | $\vert 16 - 8 \vert = 8$ |
| **Demand** | 150 | 175 | 275 | | |
| **Penalty** | $\vert 8 - 4 \vert = 4$ | $\vert 16 - 8 \vert = 8$ | $\vert 16 - 8 \vert = 8$ | | |

> [!NOTE]
> The largest penalty is **8** — this appears in both row F3 and columns W2 and W3 (tie). We may break the tie arbitrarily. Let us choose **column W2** (largest penalty column).

**Step 1:** In column W2, the smallest cost is $c_{32} = 16$ (cell F3–W2). Allocate $x_{32} = \min(300, 175) = 175$. W2 is met. Remaining supply at F3 = 125.

**Updated Tableau (column W2 removed):**

| | **W1** | **W3** | **Supply** | **Penalty** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | 4 | 8 | 100 | $\vert 8 - 4 \vert = 4$ |
| **F2** | 16 | 16 | 200 | $\vert 16 - 16 \vert = 0$ |
| **F3** | 8 | 24 | 125 | $\vert 24 - 8 \vert = 16$ |
| **Penalty** | $\vert 8 - 4 \vert = 4$ | $\vert 16 - 8 \vert = 8$ | | |

**Step 2:** Largest penalty is **16** at row F3. Smallest cost in row F3 is $c_{31} = 8$. Allocate $x_{31} = \min(125, 150) = 125$. F3 is exhausted. Remaining demand at W1 = 25.

**Updated Tableau (row F3 removed):**

| | **W1** | **W3** | **Supply** | **Penalty** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | 4 | 8 | 100 | $\vert 8 - 4 \vert = 4$ |
| **F2** | 16 | 16 | 200 | $\vert 16 - 16 \vert = 0$ |
| **Penalty** | $\vert 16 - 4 \vert = 12$ | $\vert 16 - 8 \vert = 8$ | | |

**Step 3:** Largest penalty is **12** at column W1. Smallest cost in column W1 is $c_{11} = 4$. Allocate $x_{11} = \min(100, 25) = 25$. W1 is met. Remaining supply at F1 = 75.

**Updated Tableau (column W1 removed):**

| | **W3** | **Supply** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | 8 | 75 |
| **F2** | 16 | 200 |
| **Demand** | 275 | |

**Step 4:** Allocate remaining: $x_{13} = 75$ and $x_{23} = 200$.

**Resulting VAM Allocations Table:**

| | **W1** | **W2** | **W3** | **Supply** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | 25 (4) | — (8) | 75 (8) | 100 |
| **F2** | — (16) | — (24) | 200 (16) | 200 |
| **F3** | 125 (8) | 175 (16) | — (24) | 300 |
| **Demand** | 150 | 175 | 275 | |

**Total Cost (VAM):**

$$Z_{VAM} = (25 \times 4) + (75 \times 8) + (200 \times 16) + (125 \times 8) + (175 \times 16)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
25 \times 4 &= 100 \\
75 \times 8 &= 600 \\
200 \times 16 &= 3200 \\
125 \times 8 &= 1000 \\
175 \times 16 &= 2800 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$Z_{VAM} = 100 + 600 + 3200 + 1000 + 2800 = 7{,}700$$

> [!NOTE]
> **$Z_{VAM} = ₹7{,}700$** – The best IBFS so far! (NWCM: 11,800; LCM: 8,600; VAM: 7,700.)

---

### 3.3 Phase 2: Optimality Test Using the MODI Method

The MODI (Modified Distribution) Method is the **fastest optimality test** and is **most preferred by KTU examiners**.

**Step 1:** Introduce dual variables $u_i$ for each row $i$ and $v_j$ for each column $j$. For every **occupied cell**, set up the equation:

$$u_i + v_j = c_{ij}$$

**Step 2:** Set $u_1 = 0$ (arbitrary reference). Solve the system of equations.

For the VAM solution, the occupied cells are:
$(1,1), (1,3), (2,3), (3,1), (3,2)$ with costs $4, 8, 16, 8, 16$ respectively.

The equations are:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_1 + v_1 &= 4 \\
u_1 + v_3 &= 8 \\
u_2 + v_3 &= 16 \\
u_3 + v_1 &= 8 \\
u_3 + v_2 &= 16 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $u_1 = 0$:
- From (1,1): $v_1 = 4$
- From (3,1): $u_3 = 8 - v_1 = 8 - 4 = 4$
- From (3,2): $v_2 = 16 - u_3 = 16 - 4 = 12$
- From (1,3): $v_3 = 8 - u_1 = 8 - 0 = 8$
- From (2,3): $u_2 = 16 - v_3 = 16 - 8 = 8$

**Step 3:** Compute net opportunity cost $\Delta_{ij} = c_{ij} - (u_i + v_j)$ for every **unoccupied cell**.

The unoccupied cells are: $(1,2), (2,1), (2,2), (3,3)$.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{12} &= c_{12} - (u_1 + v_2) = 8 - (0 + 12) = -4 \\
\Delta_{21} &= c_{21} - (u_2 + v_1) = 16 - (8 + 4) = 4 \\
\Delta_{22} &= c_{22} - (u_2 + v_2) = 24 - (8 + 12) = 4 \\
\Delta_{33} &= c_{33} - (u_3 + v_3) = 24 - (4 + 8) = 12 \\
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Apply the Optimality Condition.** For a **minimization** TP, the solution is **optimal if and only if** $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ for **all** unoccupied cells.

Here, $\Delta_{12} = -4 < 0$. Therefore, the current solution is **NOT optimal**.

**Step 5: Identify the Entering Cell.** The unoccupied cell with the **most negative** $\Delta_{ij}$ enters the basis. Here, cell **(1,2)** with $\Delta_{12} = -4$ is the entering cell.

**Step 6: Construct the Closed Loop (Stepping Stone Path).** Starting from the entering cell (1,2), draw a closed loop using only occupied cells and the entering cell, with alternating $+$ and $-$ signs.

The closed loop for cell (1,2) is:
$(1,2)^{+} \to (1,1)^{-} \to (3,1)^{+} \to (3,2)^{-} \to (1,2)^{+}$

**Step 7: Find the Leaving Cell.** Among the negatively signed cells in the loop, identify the one with the **smallest allocation**. The negative cells are $(1,1) = 25$ and $(3,2) = 175$. The smallest is **25** at cell (1,1). So cell (1,1) **leaves** the basis.

**Step 8: Update Allocations.** Add $\theta$ (= 25) to the $+$ cells and subtract from the $-$ cells:

$$\begin{aligned}
x_{12}^{\text{new}} &= 0 + 25 = 25 \\
x_{11}^{\text{new}} &= 25 - 25 = 0 \quad \text{(leaves the basis)} \\
x_{31}^{\text{new}} &= 125 + 25 = 150 \\
x_{32}^{\text{new}} &= 175 - 25 = 150 \\
\end{aligned}$$

The other occupied cells remain unchanged.

**New Allocations Table (Iteration 2):**

| | **W1** | **W2** | **W3** | **Supply** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **F1** | — (4) | 25 (8) | 75 (8) | 100 |
| **F2** | — (16) | — (24) | 200 (16) | 200 |
| **F3** | 150 (8) | 150 (16) | — (24) | 300 |
| **Demand** | 150 | 175 | 275 | |

**New Total Cost:**

$$Z_{\text{new}} = (25 \times 8) + (75 \times 8) + (200 \times 16) + (150 \times 8) + (150 \times 16)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
25 \times 8 &= 200 \\
75 \times 8 &= 600 \\
200 \times 16 &= 3200 \\
150 \times 8 &= 1200 \\
150 \times 16 &= 2400 \\
\end{aligned}$$

$$Z_{\text{new}} = 200 + 600 + 3200 + 1200 + 2400 = 7{,}600$$

**Step 9: Re-Test Optimality for the New Solution.**

New occupied cells: $(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (3,1), (3,2)$.

MODI equations:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_1 + v_2 &= 8 \\
u_1 + v_3 &= 8 \\
u_2 + v_3 &= 16 \\
u_3 + v_1 &= 8 \\
u_3 + v_2 &= 16 \\
\end{aligned}$$

Setting $u_1 = 0$:
- $v_2 = 8$, $v_3 = 8$, $u_3 = 16 - 8 = 8$, $v_1 = 8 - 8 = 0$, $u_2 = 16 - 8 = 8$.

Now compute $\Delta_{ij}$ for unoccupied cells $(1,1), (2,1), (2,2), (3,3)$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{11} &= 4 - (0 + 0) = 4 \\
\Delta_{21} &= 16 - (8 + 0) = 8 \\
\Delta_{22} &= 24 - (8 + 8) = 8 \\
\Delta_{33} &= 24 - (8 + 8) = 8 \\
\end{aligned}$$

All $\Delta_{ij} \geq 0$ ✓

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **OPTIMALITY ACHIEVED!**
> The optimal transportation cost is **$Z^* = ₹7{,}600$** with the following shipping schedule:
> - $x_{12} = 25$ (25 units from F1 to W2)
> - $x_{13} = 75$ (75 units from F1 to W3)
> - $x_{23} = 200$ (200 units from F2 to W3)
> - $x_{31} = 150$ (150 units from F3 to W1)
> - $x_{32} = 150$ (150 units from F3 to W2)

---

### 3.4 Summary Comparison of Methods (For KTU 2-Mark Questions)

| Method | IBFS Cost | Computation Effort | Quality of Solution |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| NWCM | ₹11,800 | Very Low | Poorest |
| LCM | ₹8,600 | Low | Better |
| VAM | ₹7,700 | Moderate | Best (closest to optimal) |
| **Optimal (MODI)** | **₹7,600** | **Highest** | **Optimal (verified)** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In most KTU paper solutions, students are expected to compute an IBFS using **VAM** and then test optimality using **MODI** — this combination carries full 14 marks. NWCM is rarely asked for as the main method but appears in 3-mark short questions.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Transportation Problem Solution Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **end-to-end algorithmic workflow** for solving a Transportation Problem in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination context.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START: Given Cost Matrix, Supply, Demand]) --> checkBal{Is it BALANCED?}
    checkBal -- NO --> addDummy[Add DUMMY Row or Column with cost 0]
    addDummy --> checkBal
    checkBal -- YES --> phaseOne[PHASE 1: Obtain IBFS]
    phaseOne --> chooseMethod{Choose IBFS Method}
    chooseMethod -- Simple --> nwcm[North-West Corner Method]
    chooseMethod -- Cost-Aware --> lcm[Least Cost Method]
    chooseMethod -- Best Quality --> vam[Vogels Approximation Method]
    nwcm --> haveIBFS[IBFS Cost Calculated]
    lcm --> haveIBFS
    vam --> haveIBFS
    haveIBFS --> phaseTwo[PHASE 2: Optimality Test]
    phaseTwo --> modiMethod[Apply MODI Method]
    modiMethod --> computeUV[Compute u_i and v_j using u_i + v_j = c_ij for occupied cells]
    computeUV --> computeDelta[Compute Delta_ij = c_ij - u_i + v_j for unoccupied cells]
    computeDelta --> checkOpt{All Delta_ij >= 0 ?}
    checkOpt -- NO --> findMostNeg[Identify cell with MOST NEGATIVE Delta_ij]
    findMostNeg --> buildLoop[Build closed stepping stone loop]
    buildLoop --> findLeave[Find leaving cell: smallest allocation in negative cells]
    findLeave --> updateAlloc[Update all allocations along the loop]
    updateAlloc --> phaseTwo
    checkOpt -- YES --> finalSol[OPTIMAL SOLUTION Z* Achieved]
    finalSol --> stopA([END: Report optimal shipping schedule])
```

---

## 4.2 IBFS Methods Comparison Architecture

This Mermaid diagram shows the **comparative decision flow** between the three IBFS methods and their computational trade-offs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA([Transportation Tableau]) --> nwcmBlock[NWCM Block]
    inputA --> lcmBlock[LCM Block]
    inputA --> vamBlock[VAM Block]
    nwcmBlock --> nwcmLogic[Start at top-left corner. Allocate min of supply and demand. Cross out row or column. Repeat.]
    lcmBlock --> lcmLogic[Find global minimum cost cell. Allocate as much as possible. Repeat on reduced matrix.]
    vamBlock --> vamLogic[Compute row and column penalties = 2nd smallest minus smallest. Pick highest penalty. In selected line, pick smallest cost cell. Allocate. Repeat.]
    nwcmLogic --> nwcmOut[Cost = 11800 in our example]
    lcmLogic --> lcmOut[Cost = 8600 in our example]
    vamLogic --> vamOut[Cost = 7700 in our example]
    nwcmOut --> comparison[Quality Comparison]
    lcmOut --> comparison
    vamOut --> comparison
    comparison --> vamBest[VAM gives BEST IBFS closest to optimal]
```

---

## 4.3 MODI Method Functional Architecture

The following block-level architecture maps the **internal modular structure** of the MODI optimality test procedure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    modiStart([MODI Method Entry]) --> mod1[Module 1: Identify occupied cells m + n - 1]
    mod1 --> mod2[Module 2: Set u1 equals 0 as reference]
    mod2 --> mod3[Module 3: Solve linear system u_i + v_j = c_ij for all occupied cells]
    mod3 --> mod4[Module 4: Compute opportunity cost Delta_ij for all unoccupied cells]
    mod4 --> mod5[Module 5: Optimality Check Module]
    mod5 --> optQ{All Delta_ij greater than or equal to 0?}
    optQ -- YES --> optEnd([Return Z* and shipping plan])
    optQ -- NO --> mod6[Module 6: Iteration Engine]
    mod6 --> mod6a[Step 6a: Select entering cell with most negative Delta_ij]
    mod6a --> mod6b[Step 6b: Construct closed stepping stone path]
    mod6b --> mod6c[Step 6c: Identify leaving cell with smallest negative allocation]
    mod6c --> mod6d[Step 6d: Update all allocations along the path by theta]
    mod6d --> mod2
```

---

## 4.4 Transportation Network Schematic

The block diagram below visualizes the **physical flow of goods** from multiple origins to multiple destinations, which is the conceptual foundation of the Transportation Problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph origins [SOURCES ORIGINS]
        O1[Origin F1: Supply = 100]
        O2[Origin F2: Supply = 200]
        O3[Origin F3: Supply = 300]
    end
    subgraph dests [DESTINATIONS]
        D1[Warehouse W1: Demand = 150]
        D2[Warehouse W2: Demand = 175]
        D3[Warehouse W3: Demand = 275]
    end
    O1 -- c11 = 4 --> D1
    O1 -- c12 = 8 --> D2
    O1 -- c13 = 8 --> D3
    O2 -- c21 = 16 --> D1
    O2 -- c22 = 24 --> D2
    O2 -- c23 = 16 --> D3
    O3 -- c31 = 8 --> D1
    O3 -- c32 = 16 --> D2
    O3 -- c33 = 24 --> D3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Each arrow represents a potential shipping route. The **cost per unit** is labelled on each arrow. The Transportation Problem's goal is to select routes and quantities such that the weighted sum of all chosen arrows (cost × quantity) is **minimum**, while every origin's full supply is shipped out and every destination's full demand is satisfied.
